reviews.rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.confidence: 3: The reviewer is fairly confident that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
The paper describes an interesting approach to self-supervised option learning for hierarchical reinforcement learning. In particular, the authors propose to learn options through an intrinsic entropy minimization objective conditioned on option trajectories. Empirical studies are performed to demonstrate the success rate and sample efficiency of this method over two state-of-the-art methods.

The key concern that I have with this paper is its organization and presentation. I am concerned that the paper takes too much space describing what other methods do not do (throughout the contributions sections), rather than concretely describing the approach. Furthermore, many sections seem to be an aggregation of contributions, related work, and results, which further makes the paper hard to follow. I believe that this does the paper a disservice by decreasing readability. Therefore, I am currently voting for rejecting the paper. 

Questions and Comments:
1) In the Introduction, the authors claim that their approach requires "little" manual design? Can this claim be better grounded? What design is required?
2) There is extensive work on option discovery [1,2]. as well as learning decompositions for reinforcement learning [3-6]. I would have liked to see a more extensive literature review comparing and contrasting with these works.
3) On page 2, the authors say, "for the purpose of this paper, we consider a hierarchy of two levels". However, it is not clear to me how this approach would extend to a hierarchy with more than two levels. Can the authors please clarify this?
4) What is k? h? (page 2)
5) On page 3, the authors claim that, unlike prior work, their formulation has no constraints on what the options can be. Can the authors comment on this claim in more detail?
6) In section 3.2, the authors mention the use of goals. Are the authors considering goal-conditioned MDPs [7]? If so, this should be made more explicit.
8) The MDP definition in section 3.2 is unclear. Could the authors please clarify this definition? 
9) What is meant by "resolution" (section 3.2)? 
10) Why does the objective defined by the authors enable skills that have more diverse semantics than previous approaches (claimed on page 4, section 3.2)?
11) Page 5, section 3.4: is the importance correction not used at all (for either the scheduler or discriminator)? 
12) What assumptions are needed for this method to work well?
13) In the experiment section, it says that in the pusher environment, success means that the goal is achieved in the final step of the episode. Does this mean that pushing the object to the position does not terminate the episode and, instead, the object must remain there until the episode terminates in a predetermined number of steps?
14) Can the authors please clarify the purpose of the additional suite of environments? What do these environments enable that existing ones do not?

[1] Brunskill, et al., PAC-inspired option discovery in lifelong reinforcement learning. ICML, 2014.
[2] Topin, et al., Portable option discovery for automated learning transfer in object-oriented Markov decision processes. IJCAI, 2015.
[3] Mehta et al., Automatic discovery and transfer of MAXQ hierarchies. ICML, 2008.
[4] Winder et al., Planning with abstract learned models while learning transferable subtasks. AAAI, 2020.
[5] Li, et al., An efficient approach to model-based hierarchical reinforcement learning. AAAI, 2017.
[6] Rafati, et al., Learning representations in model-free hierarchical reinforcement learning. AAAI, 2019.
[7] Nasiriany, et al., Planning with goal-conditioned policies. NeurIPS, 2019.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents HIDIO, a hierarchical reinforcement‐learning framework that discovers task‐agnostic latent options via a self‐supervised intrinsic‐entropy minimization objective and jointly learns a high‐level scheduler to solve sparse‐reward tasks. By training a low‐level “worker” policy to minimize the conditional entropy of a latent option given its K‐step sub‐trajectory—while maintaining high policy entropy—and by leveraging off‐policy SAC for both levels, the authors report improved success rates and sample efficiency over two leading baselines (SAC and HIRO) across several continuous‐control environments.

Overall, I find the core idea of unsupervised option discovery via a conditional‐entropy objective elegant and the empirical gains promising; however, the paper’s presentation, scope of evaluation, and theoretical grounding are insufficiently developed for publication in its current form. I therefore recommend rejection, with the hope that a thorough revision will address the concerns below.

Strengths  
• Novel Worker Objective: The conditional‐entropy minimization on option‐conditioned sub‐trajectories—in contrast to prior methods that ground skills directly in state or goal spaces—offers a clean, goal‐agnostic mechanism for encouraging diverse, temporally extended behaviors.  
• Joint Off‐Policy Training: Simultaneous SAC‐based learning of both scheduler and worker policies is well motivated and appears to improve sample efficiency relative to approaches that pretrain skills separately.  
• Empirical Performance: On four sparse‐reward robotic and navigation tasks (e.g., PUSHER, REACHER, GOALTASK, KICKBALL), HIDIO consistently outperforms SAC, SAC+action‐repeat, HIRO, and HiPPO in success rate and learning speed.  
• Ablations: The paper compares multiple sub‐trajectory feature extractors, evaluates “hard” vs. “soft” short‐sighted discounting, and sweeps over option lengths, providing useful insight into design choices.

Major Weaknesses and Required Revisions

1. Organization and Readability  
— The manuscript frequently interleaves contributions, related work, and experimental results in single sections, making it hard to follow the core algorithmic flow. A clearer separation of background, method, and experiments is essential.  
— Section 3 (method) is densely written: key derivations (e.g., the lower bound in Eq. 5) are terse, indices and notation are inconsistent, and several equations lack numbering. Including a step‐by‐step derivation or moving detailed proofs to an appendix—and providing a concise pseudo‐code listing in the main text—would greatly improve clarity.  
— The meta‐MDP formulation is only verbally sketched; readers would benefit from a precise definition with all terms (k, h, “resolution,” importance corrections) explicitly introduced.

2. Literature Context and Baselines  
— The related work discussion omits extensive prior research on option discovery and skill learning (e.g., Brunskill et al., Topin et al., Mehta et al., Winder et al., Li et al., Rafati et al.) as well as mutual‐information–based methods such as DADS and VALOR. A more thorough comparison—both in text and via empirical baselines—would clarify HIDIO’s novelty and relative merits.  
— Standard hierarchical RL baselines like option‐critic, DIAYN in a hierarchical setting, and recent model‐based approaches (e.g., DREAMER‐PEARL) are not evaluated. Without these, it is difficult to contextualize the reported gains.

3. Scope of Evaluation and Reproducibility  
— All experiments use low‐dimensional, fully observed state inputs. The claim of task‐agnostic, scalable option discovery would be strengthened by at least one vision‐based or higher‐dimensional benchmark using convolutional feature extractors.  
— Hyperparameter sensitivity (e.g., entropy coefficient β, discriminator architecture fψ, option dimension D) is not fully characterized. Reporting guidelines for tuning these parameters, along with systematic ablations, are needed for reproducibility.  
— Results are averaged over only three random seeds without statistical significance testing or confidence intervals. More seeds (e.g., 5–10) and standard error bars (95% CI) would bolster confidence in the reported improvements.

4. Theoretical and Practical Assumptions  
— The paper lacks formal analysis of convergence, option collapse modes, or conditions under which the conditional‐entropy objective reliably yields useful abstractions. Even informal discussion or a small toy MDP illustrating failure cases would be valuable.  
— It is unclear what manual design remains: the authors claim “little” manual design is required, but factors such as option length, feature extractor choice, and discriminator capacity appear hand‐tuned. Clarifying these requirements and any implicit assumptions (e.g., stationarity, granularity of sub‐trajectories) is essential.

5. Clarifications on Experimental Protocol  
— In the pusher environment, does success require the object to remain at goal until episode end, or is termination triggered immediately? How are additional custom environments chosen, and what unique challenges do they introduce?  
— How would the method extend to hierarchies deeper than two levels? The paper currently focuses on a two‐level hierarchy but makes broad claims about hierarchical flexibility.

Minor Comments  
• Please define all symbols (k, h, resolution) on first use.  
• Clarify whether goal‐conditioned MDPs are assumed (section 3.2) and if so, cite relevant works (e.g., Nasiriany et al.).  
• Discuss whether any importance‐sampling corrections are used for the scheduler or discriminator (section 3.4).  
• Improve references to figures and equations—they are sometimes mislabeled or referenced out of order.

In summary, while HIDIO’s self‐supervised option discovery and joint off‐policy training present a promising direction for hierarchical RL, the current manuscript requires substantial improvements in organization, literature context, theoretical grounding, and breadth of evaluation. I therefore recommend rejection at this stage, with encouragement to address the issues above for a future revision.

title: SkipW: Resource Adaptable RNN with Strict Upper Computational Limit

reviews.confidence: 3: The reviewer is fairly confident that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
The authors proposes a new framework skipW to strictly limit the computation in RNN. 

Pros: 
1. The idea of strictly limiting the computation in RNN is new.
2. The summary of related works is clear.

Cons:
1. The motivation why the authors wants to enforces the strict constraint on the number of updates is unclear:
a. What if a consequent subsequence in a sequence is important? Then limiting processing only the K of L elements will omit this subsequence.
b. Playing with lambda in equation (11) may also give you a tradeoff in computation and model performance and it will not omit the consequent subsequence example I give in (a).
2. Is the training harder if we use less inputs and use error gradients than the training with the whole sequence? Although the computation time is less for a forward process, the training may be harder and take more time. This detail needs to be included.
3. ThrRNN did one experiment in MNIST. I would like to see the comparison on the MNIST dataset.

Clarity:
1. The paper is clearly written in general.

Originality:
1. As far as I know, the paper is novel.

Minor:
1. It somehow sounds mysterious to me that the SKipW model can learn the adding task. The $\tilde{u}_{W,t+1}$ only takes inputs at the beginning of the length L subsequence. It may not see the inputs where the marker is. Then how can the model learn to not to skip the inputs where the marker is?

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper introduces Skip-Window (SkipW), a flexible wrapper for recurrent cells that enforces a strict upper bound on the number of time‐step updates in each fixed‐size window of length L. By computing a per‐window importance vector, selecting the top K entries via straight‐through binarization, and passing only those inputs to the underlying RNN cell, SkipW guarantees that at most K of every L inputs are processed. The authors evaluate SkipW on four benchmarks (HAR, sequential MNIST, IMDB sentiment, Adding Task), compare against SkipRNN and ThrRNN, and report both accuracy-computation trade‐offs and real-device latency and energy measurements on edge hardware (Jetson, Raspberry Pi).

Strengths:
1. Novel enforcement of a hard per-window compute budget addresses practical needs for predictable runtimes in resource‐constrained settings, eliminating intra- and inter-sequence variability present in prior skip‐update RNNs.
2. The related work summary and model description are clear, and SkipW integrates seamlessly with standard RNN/LSTM/GRU cells.
3. Empirical validation is extensive: results on four datasets show that SkipW matches or outperforms SkipRNN and ThrRNN at comparable or lower computational cost; ablations on window size, thresholding, and dynamic budget changes reinforce the design choices.
4. Real‐device measurements highlight meaningful latency and energy savings, demonstrating the wrapper’s practical value on edge platforms.

Weaknesses:
1. Motivation for enforcing a strict K-per-window cap could be made clearer. In particular, if an important consecutive subsequence spans beyond K selected inputs, a penalty‐based approach (e.g. tuning λ in Eq. 11) might better trade off computation and performance without risking omission of critical tokens.
2. While forward computation is reduced, training with sparse, straight‐through gradient estimators may be more challenging or slower to converge than full‐sequence training. No discussion or empirical analysis of training stability, convergence speed, or gradient variance is provided.
3. The evaluation is confined to relatively small‐scale or synthetic tasks; applicability to large‐vocabulary sequence tasks (e.g. speech recognition or machine translation) remains untested, limiting confidence in generality.
4. The overhead—both time and memory—of computing the importance vector and performing top‐K selection is not profiled, which is crucial for a method aimed at resource‐constrained deployment.
5. Hyperparameter tuning for window size L, budget K, and threshold thr can be nontrivial; the paper lacks heuristics or adaptive schemes to guide practitioners across different datasets.

Minor Concern:
It is somewhat mysterious that SkipW successfully learns the Adding Task when the gating vector only sees inputs at the beginning of each window—further ablation or explanation of how the model captures the marker information would be helpful.

Actionable Suggestions:
• Provide a detailed complexity analysis and profiling of the gating and top‐K operations (time, memory, and latency overhead).
• Extend experiments to at least one larger, real‐world sequence modeling task (e.g., speech or language modeling) to demonstrate scalability.
• Include an ablation or study of training convergence and stability under the straight‐through top‐K estimator, reporting variance and sensitivity to initialization.
• Offer guidance or simple heuristics for selecting window size L and budget K, or investigate an adaptive mechanism to reduce manual tuning.
• Release code, pretrained models, and exact hyperparameter settings in an accessible repository to facilitate reproducibility.

Decision: 6 (Marginally above acceptance threshold)
Despite some open questions around motivation, training dynamics, hyperparameter sensitivity, and overhead profiling, SkipW makes a meaningful and novel contribution to resource‐aware RNN design by providing strict per-window compute guarantees. With the suggested clarifications and additional analyses, it will be a valuable addition to the literature.

reviews.confidence: 5: The reviewer is absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct and very familiar with the relevant literature
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
Summary:
The paper proposes Skip-Window or SkipW an abstraction encapsulating RNN cells to actively skip updates similar to some earlier works like Skip-RNN, Skim-RNN, and ThrRNN. The novelty of the method comes is in having control over the total updates to control the overall computational budget compared to previous methods which didn't provide deterministic upper bounds and varied depending on the inputs. The idea is very simple and straightforward and can be looked at as a logical extension to the Skip-RNN line of work combined with a windowed approach on time series as used in ShaRNN (Dennis et al., NeurIPS 2019). The entire time series is divided into Windows of length L (which is a tunable parameter) and each window has a precomputed (from the final hidden state of the previous window) per-time step (update inside the window) importance vector which can be used as an indicator to update or not to update following the binarization as done in previous methods. The strict sparsification of this per-window importance vector to have only K non-zeros per window helps reduce compute to an upper bound ratio of K/L. The method further uses another threshold term over the sparsified importance vector to control finer budget requirements if needed. The experimentation is done on 2 tasks HAR-2D-Pose (with 32 time steps) and Adding task with 50 timesteps. The evaluation shows that Skip-Window shows good performance./accuracy compared to previous flexible RNNs with a reduction in the total number of updates. Finally, the impressive part of the paper is the real-world evaluation on Jetson Nano with a more complex workflow involving pose estimation from images for HAR-2D-Pose. 

Pros:
1. Simple and elegant idea. SkipWindow solution is in theory generalizable to multiple RNNs without much hassle. The abstraction could be thought of as an independent outer layer over RNNs similar to most other works in the space. 
2. Related work section is very thorough and the claims are grounded.
3. The architecture is easy to understand along with the aspects of training
4. The experimentation on both datasets reveals interesting insights and showcase the advantage of SkipW over other methods in both accuracy and computational budget. 
5. I highly commend the deployment experiments and evaluating it with complex workflow and showing how skipping updates and inputs can help compute latency and resources.
6. Plots are very clean and appendix and ablation are good. 

Weaknesses:
1. While I feel the idea works well and is elegant. It still is a combination of a couple of known techniques (mentioned in summary) which limits the novelty somewhat. But that doesn't stop the method from being useful.
2. Figure 2 is not required or needs redesigning as the equations were much clearer than the figure and the gates just made it hard to understand. 
3. I understand while K/L upper bound is guaranteed, the authors want to use thr for finer control, I just feel, it might not be required for super long sequences. - Just a comment.
4. My major issues are with experiments. While I like the work on HAR-2D-Pose and agree it is a good choice of dataset. I also want to see the success of Skip W on long term dependency tasks. I wouldn't count adding tasks as it can be trivially solved using good initialization (Henaff et al., ICML 2016). While I appreciate the efforts, I would like to see results on at least one or two (at least two preferred but I understand the time limitations, so one good dataset also works for the discussion phase) more real-world datasets to check the generalization. It can be Keyword spotting, phoneme detection, or even noisy-CIFAR. Something around 100 timesteps or more would be great

I am asking the authors to add these things during rebuttal or give reasoning corresponding to it. This is a key question to be addressed during the discussion. Note that I do not need deployment for these datasets but would like to see these numbers in the paper for more datasets to make the paper stronger. The reasoning for the long-range is because the gains would be more profound there than in 32 time steps. 

5. While the K/L based on importance makes sense per window. Sometimes, RNNs tend to identify signatures randomly. I suggest the authors add a comparison with random K choice instead of top K choice per window and have multiple variants of it like periodic sampling or all inputs being from start or end (these are deterministic for prediction time). This experiment will help us determine if the importance vector actually contributes to the decision making on skipping updates. The rationale behind this comes from the action recognition literature where people have shown that random sub-sampling works decently well compared to intelligent sub-sampling. Another thing to add here is because the importance vector per window is chosen based on the previous window it might not be optimal and maybe random selection might work fine. If random sampling (even not so random like periodic etc.,) works well, then I am not sure about using importance vectors anymore.

Without these baselines, it is hard to argue otherwise. I strongly suggest authors pursue this to make the claims more solid. This is another thing that needs to be addressed during the discussion.

Decision:
Even though the novelty is slightly limited, I like the idea for its ease, decoupled natured and potential generalizability along with the control on computational budgets. I appreciate the authors for ablation and on-device experiments which are very thorough. The only issue I found with the paper is the experimentation and baselines. I want to see at least one long term dependency task (real-world) and baselines that evaluate if the importance vector is even needed with the simple sampling strategies among each window. 

I am very much willing to increase the score based on discussion and the improvements on the experimentation front.
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Edit after rebuttal and discussion.

I thank the authors for extra experimentation to showcase the effectiveness of SkipW. While most reviewers here agree that the novelty is limited (that doesn't stop it from being useful), I strongly think the impact due to SkipW will be translated to the real-world. There has been some discussion on the datasets, which I agree are not extensive making the initial experimentation weak. However, the new experiments compensate to an extent and I would like to recommend a weak acceptance with a score of 6 (I am still between 6 and 7, waiting for other reviewers to pitch in).

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper introduces Skip-Window (SkipW), a lightweight wrapper for recurrent cells that enforces a strict upper bound on the number of updates per fixed-size window. By dividing an input sequence into windows of length L and computing a per-window importance vector from the previous window’s final hidden state, SkipW selects the top-K time steps for updates via straight-through binarization, guaranteeing at most K updates every L inputs. A secondary threshold term further refines the compute budget if desired. Experiments on HAR-2D-Pose, the Adding task, sequential MNIST, and IMDB demonstrate that SkipW matches or outperforms SkipRNN and ThrRNN in accuracy versus computation trade-offs. The authors also present real-world latency and energy measurements on edge hardware (Jetson Nano, TX2, Raspberry Pi) and showcase the ability to adapt budget parameters mid-sequence.

Strengths:
1. Simplicity and Generality: SkipW builds on well-understood skip-update mechanisms (SkipRNN, ThrRNN) and windowed processing (ShaRNN) into an outer abstraction that is cell-agnostic. Its modular design makes it straightforward to integrate with GRU, LSTM, or other RNN variants.
2. Strict Per-Window Budget Guarantees: Unlike prior methods that only control average update rates, SkipW enforces a hard budget of K updates per window, addressing both intra- and inter-sequence variability—a practical advantage for real-time, resource-constrained deployment.
3. Comprehensive Empirical Evaluation: The authors evaluate on four benchmarks, including a real-world HAR task and synthetic sequence tasks, providing accuracy-versus-update curves, ablations on window size L and thresholding, and comparisons to SkipRNN/ThrRNN. They further demonstrate dynamic adaptation of K and thr at inference time, a feature unique among skip-update RNNs.
4. Deployment Experiments: Real-device measurements on Jetson Nano, TX2, and Raspberry Pi clearly illustrate latency and energy savings. This on-device validation underscores SkipW’s practical impact for edge applications.
5. Clarity and Reproducibility Aids: The paper presents clean plots, a thorough related work survey, and ablation studies in the appendix. The architecture and training procedure are described clearly.

Weaknesses:
1. Incremental Novelty: As noted by multiple reviewers, the core idea—windowed top-K gating—is a logical extension of existing skip-update approaches. While the per-window budget guarantee is valuable, the conceptual contribution is modest.
2. Limited Task Scope: All evaluated datasets are relatively small or synthetic, with sequence lengths up to 100. There is no demonstration on large-scale or naturally long-range tasks such as speech recognition, machine translation, or long-form language modeling where compute savings would be even more critical.
3. Baseline Coverage: Comparisons are restricted to SkipRNN and ThrRNN. Recent hierarchical RNNs, early-exit sequence models, or summary-based architectures are omitted, leaving an incomplete picture of SkipW’s relative performance.
4. Hyperparameter Sensitivity: SkipW introduces two key hyperparameters—window size L and top-K—plus a threshold thr. Guidance on selecting these parameters is limited, and the authors do not analyze the sensitivity or provide heuristics for their choice.
5. Gating Overhead and Memory Cost: The paper lacks an analysis of the computational and memory overhead incurred by importance-vector computation, top-K selection, and gating. Profiling these costs would clarify the true end-to-end savings.
6. Importance vs. Random Sampling Baselines: The efficacy of the learned importance vector is not fully established. As suggested by prior work in action recognition, random or periodic sampling can sometimes match intelligent sampling. A comparison to random K selection or deterministic periodic sampling would strengthen claims about the contribution of the importance signals.
7. Figure 2 Clarity: The graphical depiction of the gating mechanism in Figure 2 adds complexity without clarity. The equations are more straightforward; the figure should be redesigned or omitted.

Actionable Suggestions:
1. Extend Evaluation to Long-Sequence Benchmark(s): Incorporate at least one larger real-world dataset (e.g., speech recognition, keyword spotting, machine translation, or noisy-CIFAR frames) with sequence lengths ≥100 to demonstrate generalization and stronger long-range dependency handling.
2. Broaden Baselines: Compare against hierarchical RNNs, early-exit methods, and other budget-aware sequence models to position SkipW within the latest landscape.
3. Hyperparameter Analysis: Provide ablation studies on window size L, top-K, and threshold thr across multiple tasks, and propose guidelines or simple heuristics for setting these values based on sequence statistics.
4. Profile Gating Overhead: Report the time and memory costs of importance-vector computation and top-K selection during training and inference to validate net savings.
5. Random Sampling Control: Add experiments with random, periodic, or start/end deterministic sampling strategies per window to assess whether learned importance outperforms simple baselines.
6. Figure Revision: Simplify or remove Figure 2, presenting the skip-update mechanism through clear equations and minimal diagrams.
7. Reproducibility: Release code, detailed hyperparameter settings, and training scripts to facilitate adoption and fair comparisons.

Decision:
I recommend a weak acceptance (rating 6). While the novelty is incremental and additional baselines and large-scale evaluations would strengthen the work, SkipW delivers a clear, practical advancement in enforceable compute budgets for RNNs. Its simplicity, cell-agnostic design, and on-device validation suggest high real-world impact for resource-constrained settings. The suggested extensions can be addressed during revision.

reviews.confidence: 2: The reviewer is willing to defend the evaluation, but it is quite likely that the reviewer did not understand central parts of the paper
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This work introduces Skip-Window (SkipW), an approach that allows RNNS to have improved computational efficiency at the cost of accuracy. SkipW adds a procedure to existing RNN cells that allows them to process fewer inputs while remaining in a strict computational budget. This work demonstrates the benefits of SkipW through experiments on multiple data sets.

This work proposes a structured procedure to process fewer inputs during inference while abiding by a computational budget. Each skip seems to be calculated over a window of inputs in a sequence, thereby minimizing inter and intra sequence variability in computation. This work does seem to have merit in a practical setting where the availability of computational resources can vary. The ability to manage dynamic budget, though not totally novel (ThrRNN also has the thr param), does provide an advantage with SkipW being perhaps more flexible with both K and thr usage.

This work empirically demonstrates the benefit of SkipW  over several baselines on two data sets. There are, however, some points that need to be addressed.





Figure 2 and equations 5-10 seem to be a bit unclear. Figure 1 and the first two paragraphs of section 3 seem to imply that  \hat{u}_{W} is calculated once as an L length vector at the beginning of an L sized window.   Here \hat{u}_{W}^{K}  are the selected K entries for the whole L sized window. Therefore what does \hat{u}_{W,t}^{K}  denote. 

In Figures 3 and 4 what is input processed % ? Is it K/L. For example, in Figure 3 and in the case of L=8, the setup K=1 implies K/L = 0.125 , but the K=1 point for SkipW is at less than 10% mark on the xaxis. Furthermore, how is it that K=[3-8] all have roughly the same input processed % ? Its the same case for K=[1-4] and K=[3-16]  


In Figure 4, the number of points for ThrRNN seem to be much more than SkipW. This seems to imply much more finer control over computational cost for ThrRNN as compared to SkipW (more coarser control). What are the authors' thoughts about this?


Figure 6 seems to imply that the inputs in p3 and p4 can be more easily dropped as compared to p1 and p2. What are the  authors' thoughts about the connection of this ease of dropping inputs to the various attention mechanisms that are available in literature. Furthermore, is it the case that later inputs will typically always have more tendency to be dropped in SkipW ?
 

Figure 7 seems to be a bit unclear. Is the accuracy being depicted the relative accuracy as compared to thr=0.4 and K=8? Furthermore, how is the accuracy being calculated as the thr,K are changing on the fly as time/sequences go on? This might imply that different thr,K values see different data? Is the same exact sample being used to calculate the accuracy? 


It seems that L-K denotes the minimum number of inputs that will be skipped (as there is a further 'binarize' that runs on the K inputs). Therefore, it seems that the system will under utilize the available computational capacity. This might not be desirable as any loss in accuracy might have been reduced if all available computational capacity was used. What are the authors' thoughts about this?


Perhaps a text processing task in NLP would have made the results stronger as in practical scenarios, this is one of the common modalities where RNNs are used.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper introduces Skip-Window (SkipW), a window-based wrapper for recurrent cells that enforces a strict upper bound on computation by selecting the top-K most important inputs in each fixed-size window of length L. At inference time the model processes at most K inputs per window, providing hard per-window guarantees that eliminate both inter- and intra-sequence variability in computational cost. SkipW is cell-agnostic and can adapt its budget (K or threshold thr) on the fly, making it attractive for resource-constrained settings. The authors empirically validate SkipW on multiple benchmarks, including HAR, sequential MNIST, IMDB sentiment, and the Adding Task, and report real-device latency and energy savings on Jetson and Raspberry Pi platforms.

Strengths  
• Novel budget enforcement: By shifting gating decisions from every time step to fixed windows and using a TopK selection, SkipW provides strict computational guarantees per interval—an improvement over prior average-based methods such as SkipRNN or ThrRNN.  
• Dynamic adaptation: The ability to change K or thr mid-sequence allows SkipW to flexibly trade off accuracy and cost under shifting resource constraints.  
• Empirical validation: Experiments cover four diverse tasks, ablations over window size L, thresholding, and gating variants, and real-hardware measurements that demonstrate meaningful latency and energy reductions.  
• Practical relevance: The wrapper is simple to integrate with existing RNN cells (LSTM/GRU) and addresses a real need for predictable runtimes on edge devices.

Weaknesses and Points for Clarification  
1. Clarity of notation and figures.  
   – Equations 5–10 and Figure 2 are hard to follow: the definition of \hat u_W (an L-dimensional importance vector) vs. \hat u_{W,t}^K (the selected entries for each time step) needs to be spelled out more clearly.  
   – In Figures 3 and 4 the “input processed %” axis is ambiguous: is this K/L? For instance, in the L=8 case, K=1 should correspond to 12.5% but is plotted below 10%. Additionally, why do K=[3–8] appear to have the same processed percentage?  
2. Comparative granularity.  
   – Figure 4 shows many more operating points for ThrRNN than for SkipW, suggesting ThrRNN can achieve finer control over cost. The paper should comment on this trade-off between budget granularity and strict guarantees.  
3. Hyperparameter sensitivity.  
   – SkipW introduces two new hyperparameters (window size L, top-K) plus thr. Yet no practical guidelines or theoretical insights are provided for setting these. An expanded ablation study on sensitivity to L, K, and thr (or λ) would help practitioners.  
4. Overhead of gating and TopK operations.  
   – The memory and runtime costs of computing the importance vector and performing TopK selection are not profiled. A complexity analysis, or at least empirical measurements of the gating overhead, would clarify the net computational gains.  
5. Limited task scope.  
   – All benchmarks are relatively small or synthetic. To demonstrate broader applicability, it would be valuable to evaluate SkipW on a larger real-world sequence task (e.g., language modeling, speech recognition, or machine translation).  
6. Gradient estimation and stability.  
   – The straight-through estimator for TopK binarization may introduce training instability or sensitivity to initialization. An analysis of gradient variance, convergence behavior, or alternative approximations would strengthen the paper.  
7. Capacity under-utilization.  
   – Since each window skips at least L–K inputs before binarization, SkipW may under-utilize its allocated budget. The authors should discuss whether this conservative behavior ever limits accuracy and how one might mitigate it.  
8. Interpretation of input dropping.  
   – Figure 6 suggests some input positions are more likely to be dropped. What drives this pattern? Is it related to known attention behaviors? Will later inputs always tend to be skipped more?  
9. Accuracy measurement under dynamic budgets.  
   – In Figure 7, it is unclear whether accuracy is measured relative to a fixed baseline (e.g., thr=0.4, K=8) or on the same data samples as budgets change. Clarify how per-window adjustments affect evaluation consistency.

Actionable Suggestions  
1. Revise notation and figure captions for \hat u_W, \hat u_{W,t}^K, and “input processed %” to ensure readers can precisely follow the method and results.  
2. Include a complexity and memory overhead analysis for the importance vector computation and TopK operation, or provide empirical profiling on real devices.  
3. Expand ablation studies on window size L, K, and thr (or λ), and offer heuristics or guidelines for selecting these hyperparameters based on sequence statistics.  
4. Evaluate SkipW on at least one large-scale, real-world sequence modeling task (e.g., a text classification or language modeling dataset) to demonstrate generality.  
5. Compare against additional budget-aware sequence models—such as hierarchical RNNs or early-exit methods—to contextualize SkipW’s performance and budget granularity.  
6. Analyze gradient stability through the TopK straight-through estimator, reporting any sensitivity to initialization or variance during training.  
7. Discuss the under-utilization of computation due to the L–K constraint and propose possible modifications (e.g., adaptive window overlaps) to make fuller use of the budget.  
8. Clarify how dynamic changes to K or thr are synchronized with evaluation to ensure that accuracy comparisons remain consistent across different budget settings.  
9. Consider releasing code, hyperparameter settings, and scripts to facilitate reproducibility and accelerate follow-on research.

Conclusion  
Skip-Window offers a practical and well-motivated advance in resource-aware RNNs by enforcing hard per-window computation budgets and supporting dynamic budget adjustments. While some aspects—particularly clarity of presentation, hyperparameter guidance, and broader empirical validation—require further development, the core idea and empirical results justify acceptance.

Rating  
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.original_text:
Review:
This work introduces Skip-Window (SkipW), an approach that allows RNNS to have improved computational efficiency at the cost of accuracy. SkipW adds a procedure to existing RNN cells that allows them to process fewer inputs while remaining in a strict computational budget. This work demonstrates the benefits of SkipW through experiments on multiple data sets.

This work proposes a structured procedure to process fewer inputs during inference while abiding by a computational budget. Each skip seems to be calculated over a window of inputs in a sequence, thereby minimizing inter and intra sequence variability in computation. This work does seem to have merit in a practical setting where the availability of computational resources can vary. The ability to manage dynamic budget, though not totally novel (ThrRNN also has the thr param), does provide an advantage with SkipW being perhaps more flexible with both K and thr usage.

This work empirically demonstrates the benefit of SkipW  over several baselines on two data sets. There are, however, some points that need to be addressed.





Figure 2 and equations 5-10 seem to be a bit unclear. Figure 1 and the first two paragraphs of section 3 seem to imply that  \hat{u}_{W} is calculated once as an L length vector at the beginning of an L sized window.   Here \hat{u}_{W}^{K}  are the selected K entries for the whole L sized window. Therefore what does \hat{u}_{W,t}^{K}  denote. 

In Figures 3 and 4 what is input processed % ? Is it K/L. For example, in Figure 3 and in the case of L=8, the setup K=1 implies K/L = 0.125 , but the K=1 point for SkipW is at less than 10% mark on the xaxis. Furthermore, how is it that K=[3-8] all have roughly the same input processed % ? Its the same case for K=[1-4] and K=[3-16]  


In Figure 4, the number of points for ThrRNN seem to be much more than SkipW. This seems to imply much more finer control over computational cost for ThrRNN as compared to SkipW (more coarser control). What are the authors' thoughts about this?


Figure 6 seems to imply that the inputs in p3 and p4 can be more easily dropped as compared to p1 and p2. What are the  authors' thoughts about the connection of this ease of dropping inputs to the various attention mechanisms that are available in literature. Furthermore, is it the case that later inputs will typically always have more tendency to be dropped in SkipW ?
 

Figure 7 seems to be a bit unclear. Is the accuracy being depicted the relative accuracy as compared to thr=0.4 and K=8? Furthermore, how is the accuracy being calculated as the thr,K are changing on the fly as time/sequences go on? This might imply that different thr,K values see different data? Is the same exact sample being used to calculate the accuracy? 


It seems that L-K denotes the minimum number of inputs that will be skipped (as there is a further 'binarize' that runs on the K inputs). Therefore, it seems that the system will under utilize the available computational capacity. This might not be desirable as any loss in accuracy might have been reduced if all available computational capacity was used. What are the authors' thoughts about this?


Perhaps a text processing task in NLP would have made the results stronger as in practical scenarios, this is one of the common modalities where RNNs are used.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold

reviews.confidence: 5: The reviewer is absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct and very familiar with the relevant literature
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This submission presents an extension of SkipRNN, Skip-Window, that splits input sequences into windows of length L from which only K samples can be used. This guarantees that the computational budget is never exceeded. Skip-Window implemented this inductive bias by predicting L updating probabilities in parallel at the beginning of each window. L needs to be set prior to training, whereas K can be modified at test time. The model is evaluated in two tasks, namely a synthetic adding task and human activity recognition. Authors report latency and energy consumption in small platforms, showing the impact of this research direction in real applications.

My main concern regarding the proposed architecture is that it limits the types of skipping patterns that can be discovered -- whereas the original SkipRNN can in principle learn any such pattern. For instance, Skip-Window cannot produce the skipping patterns shown in Figure 6 in Campos et al. (2018) unless K=L or L=1 (in both cases, Skip-Window reduces to SkipRNN). This can even be seen in the results for the adding task, where Skip-Window is actually *not* solving the task for most values of K (c.f. Figure 4). Recall that the output distribution has a variance of 0.166, and Campos et al. define solving the task as achieving an MSE two orders of magnitude below such variance. The reason for this is that Skip-Window with 5<K<L will miss the second marker in some sequences, as it needs to guess its position -- which is random within the second half of the sequence. For K<5, there’s a chance that Skip-Window will miss the first marker as well. As a sanity check, I suggest that authors report the percentage of first and second markers that are missed in the adding task as a function of K. Plotting the MSE of an LSTM or GRU that skips inputs randomly, and varying the fraction of skipped inputs as in Campos et al., would also provide context for the presented error rates.

Despite the imposed constraints in terms of skipping patterns, Skip-Window can be useful in tasks where the input signal can be downsampled more uniformly. After all, the adding task is a challenging problem for RNNs that skip input samples as missing one of the markers will massively increase the error rate. This potential is shown in the human activity recognition (HAR) results. However, I believe that more experimental evaluation is needed before this paper can be published at ICLR. First, the input sequences for HAR are extremely short (32 timesteps), which makes it difficult to draw strong conclusions. Second, since the Skip-Window architecture limits the types of skipping patterns that the model can discover, it is difficult to claim that Skip-Window is a generic RNN architecture unless experiments on more domains are reported (e.g. sequential MNIST, NLP).

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper introduces Skip-Window (SkipW), an extension of SkipRNN that enforces a strict upper bound on processed inputs over fixed-size windows. At the start of each window of length L, the model predicts L update probabilities in parallel and then selects the top K positions to actually update via a straight-through TopK gating. By design, K can be varied at test time, and the total number of updates per window never exceeds K, yielding predictable compute budgets—an attractive property for real-time, resource-constrained settings. The authors evaluate SkipW on a synthetic adding task and on human activity recognition (HAR), reporting both error rates and practical latency/energy savings on Jetson and Raspberry Pi platforms.

Overall Recommendation  
Rating: 5: Marginally below acceptance threshold  
While Skip-Window offers a clear practical advantage in enforcing per-window cost constraints and demonstrates real-device speedups, I find the novelty to be modest and the empirical evaluation insufficiently broad to justify publication in its current form.

Strengths  
1. Practical budget guarantees: Unlike SkipRNN or ThrRNN—which only bound average updates—SkipW hard-caps updates per window via TopK gating, directly addressing inter- and intra-sequence variability in compute.  
2. Generic wrapper: The approach is cell-agnostic and easily applied to LSTM or GRU units.  
3. Real-device evaluation: Reporting latency and energy measurements on Jetson Nano/TX2 and Raspberry Pi underscores the method’s applicability to edge hardware.  
4. Dynamic adaptation: Demonstration that K (or threshold) can be changed mid-sequence highlights SkipW’s flexibility for varying runtime budgets on the fly.

Weaknesses and Concerns  
1. Incremental novelty  
   – The core idea of windowed TopK gating builds directly on existing skip-update RNNs (SkipRNN, ThrRNN), with only a straightforward extension to enforce hard budgets.  
   – Similar budget-aware mechanisms (e.g., early-exit RNNs, hierarchical RNNs) are not compared, leaving the broader contribution unclear.

2. Limitation of skipping patterns  
   – As noted in Campos et al. (2018), SkipRNN can learn arbitrary skipping patterns, whereas SkipW restricts flexibility unless K = L or L = 1.  
   – This limitation is evident in the adding task results (Figure 4), where SkipW fails to solve the task for most K values. The model often misses one or both random markers, inflating MSE.

3. Narrow experimental scope  
   – Only very short sequences are tested in HAR (32 timesteps), making it hard to gauge performance on longer, more realistic signals.  
   – The adding task is too unforgiving for uniform downsampling approaches; missing a single marker catastrophically increases error.  
   – Key benchmarks present in alternative reviews—sequential MNIST and IMDB sentiment classification—are mentioned but not included in the main submission. The lack of any large-scale language or speech tasks (e.g., machine translation, language modeling) further limits claims of generality.

4. Missing analyses and comparisons  
   – There is no complexity or memory cost analysis for computing the importance vector and performing TopK selection.  
   – Hyperparameter sensitivity: choices of window size L, top-K, and threshold λ require per-task tuning; no systematic ablation or heuristic guideline is provided.  
   – Gradient estimation through the straight-through TopK is not analyzed; stability and variance of training remain unknown.  
   – Comparisons are restricted to SkipRNN and ThrRNN; recent hierarchical or summary-based RNNs and early-exit architectures are omitted.

Actionable Suggestions  
1. Deepen empirical evaluation  
   – Include at least one longer, real-world sequence benchmark (e.g., sequential MNIST, IMDB, speech recognition, or machine translation) to demonstrate SkipW’s generality beyond toy and very short tasks.  
   – Report the percentage of first and second markers missed in the adding task as a function of K, and compare MSE against an LSTM/GRU that randomly skips inputs at comparable rates (as in Campos et al.).

2. Expand ablations and analyses  
   – Profile the computational and memory overhead of the TopK gating and importance vector relative to the base RNN cell.  
   – Conduct sensitivity analyses for L, K, and threshold λ across multiple tasks, and provide practical guidelines or heuristic rules for setting them.  
   – Analyze the gradient signal through the straight-through approximation: report training variance, convergence curves, and any stability issues.

3. Broaden baseline comparisons  
   – Add comparisons to hierarchical RNNs, early-exit methods, and other budget-aware sequence models to position SkipW relative to the full landscape of adaptive computation approaches.

4. Improve reproducibility  
   – Release code, pretrained models, and detailed hyperparameter settings.  
   – If space is tight, move key implementation details from the appendix to the main paper to aid readers in reproducing results.

Conclusion  
Skip-Window addresses a practical need for strict per-window compute budgets in RNNs and demonstrates promising latency and energy gains on edge hardware. However, its conceptual contribution over prior skip-update methods is incremental, and the current experiments—limited in task diversity, sequence length, and baseline coverage—are insufficient to establish broad utility. I therefore recommend a “marginally below threshold” rating pending a more comprehensive empirical and analytical treatment.

title: Learning explanations that are hard to vary

reviews.confidence: 3: The reviewer is fairly confident that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This paper investigates one possible pitfall of current gradient descent method that averaging gradients over different examples failed to capture the invariance between different examples, through it can learn quickly by memorizing data. The authors claim that this is one important reason that machine learning with GD can't generalize well to out of domain dataset. To solve this issue, this paper focus on developing a method to learning the invariant explanations. The authors firstly formalize the a notion of consistency of the loss surface and also propose a practical algorithm, AND-mask. The authors also carried experiments and analysis on synthetic dataset to validate the effective the method. Overall, the work is important and the paper is well presented. I vote for accepting. 


##########################################################################
Pros 
	1. The paper studied the important problem of model generalization. The paper is well motivated and the knowledge presented can benefit the community. 
	2. The paper defines the invariance as the consistency for minima of the loss surface. The author presented case study examples as well as math formulations. The presentation is clear and easy to understand.
	3. The authors validate the value of capturing invariance through the implementation of a practical AND-mask, and run experiment on a set of tasks. The baselines are reasonably chosen. The proposed method greatly outperforms other baselines. 
	4. The synthetic tasks unveil the difference between ILC and gradient averaging. It is very helpful for the readers. 

##########################################################################
Cons
	1. The work claim that they run experiments on a set of real-world tasks. The authors introduce artificial label noisiness in CIFAR-10 by randomly shuffling the labels. However, recently work (e.g https://arxiv.org/pdf/2001.10528.pdf) found that noisiness exists in many popular datasets. It would be interesting to show that ILC can also handle such naturally introduced noises. 
2. What is the connection between invariance and model casual explanation?

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper addresses a timely and important problem in deep learning: why standard gradient descent—by averaging per-example gradients—can fail to capture invariant features and instead learn spurious “shortcuts,” thereby harming out-of-domain generalization. Building on the principle that “good explanations are hard to vary,” the authors formalize a consistency measure for minima (Invariant Learning Consistency, or ILC) and propose a simple, practical AND-mask algorithm that zeros gradient components whose signs disagree across examples. They provide a clear theoretical connection between ILC and the geometric versus arithmetic means of Hessians, along with convergence guarantees under diagonal‐Hessian and Gaussian‐gradient assumptions. Empirically, the method is validated on a suite of synthetic tasks—most notably a spiral‐versus‐shortcut dataset—plus CIFAR-10 with random and noisy labels, and a behavioral cloning task on CoinRun. The paper is well written, the presentation of the formalism is clear, and the experiments convincingly demonstrate that AND-mask suppresses memorization and improves OOD performance compared to standard baselines. I recommend acceptance (Rating: 7: Good paper, accept).

Strengths  
1. Clear Motivation and Formalism  
   - The authors identify a key pitfall of gradient averaging: it effectively implements a “logical OR” of environment‐specific gradients, which can favor spurious, environment‐specific mechanisms. By contrast, the AND-mask realizes a “logical AND,” preserving only invariant directions.  
   - The ILC metric is well motivated and neatly links to the geometric mean of Hessians, giving a solid theoretical grounding.  
   - Case studies and mathematical formulations are presented clearly, making the core ideas easy to follow.  

2. Theoretical Insights  
   - Under reasonable assumptions (diagonal Hessians, Gaussian gradients), the authors show that the AND-mask suppresses inconsistent gradient components at an exponential rate.  
   - These analyses help explain why standard GD can rapidly memorize noisy labels, whereas AND-mask naturally resists such spurious signals.  

3. Practical Algorithm and Lightweight Implementation  
   - The AND-mask is straightforward to implement: it only requires tracking per-example gradient signs and applying a threshold mask.  
   - The algorithm introduces minimal computational overhead compared to more complex invariance methods, and the authors provide code release to facilitate reproducibility.  

4. Empirical Evidence  
   - Synthetic Spiral Task: The controlled spiral‐vs‐shortcut benchmark cleanly demonstrates that AND‐mask recovers the true invariant mechanism where standard methods fail.  
   - CIFAR-10 Random Labels and Label Noise: AND-mask prevents overfitting to random labels and shows robustness to noisy annotations, addressing recent findings that real datasets contain inherent label noise.  
   - CoinRun Behavioral Cloning: The method yields consistent improvement in generalization to unseen levels, illustrating its applicability beyond vision tasks.  

Weaknesses and Suggestions  
1. Hyperparameter Sensitivity  
   - The AND-mask relies on an agreement threshold τ, whose choice is currently heuristic. An ablation on threshold sensitivity, along with practical guidelines for selecting τ, would strengthen the work (as suggested in Alternative Reviews 1 and 2).  

2. Limited Scope of Real-World Benchmarks  
   - While CIFAR-10 experiments are valuable, evaluation on established domain‐generalization datasets (e.g., PACS, VLCS, DomainBed, or WILDS) would better demonstrate broad applicability (noted also in Alternative Reviews 2 and 4).  
   - Comparison to additional invariance and domain‐generalization methods—such as REx, Fishr, Group DRO, or more recent IRM variants—would provide a richer empirical context.  

3. Theoretical Generality  
   - The convergence guarantees hinge on convex or diagonal‐Hessian settings. It remains unclear how these results extend to large, nonconvex deep networks. A discussion of this limitation (and potential avenues for extending analysis) would be helpful (Alternative Reviews 3 and 4).  

4. Computational and Implementation Costs  
   - Although the AND-mask is conceptually simple, per-example gradient computation can be resource intensive in current frameworks. Quantifying wall‐clock overhead and memory footprint on larger CNNs or transformer models would clarify practicality (raised in Alternative Review 3).  

5. Connection to Causal Explanations  
   - The paper alludes to invariance and causal reasoning, but the precise relationship between ILC and causal explanation frameworks could be more explicitly stated. Clarifying this link would situate the work within the broader causal‐machine‐learning literature.  

Minor Comments  
- Please include details on random seeds, optimizer settings, and hyperparameter search ranges in the appendix to ensure full reproducibility.  
- Reporting results across multiple random restarts and providing confidence intervals would strengthen claims of robustness.  

Conclusion  
Overall, this manuscript makes a significant contribution by formalizing an invariance metric for loss minima and introducing a lightweight, theoretically grounded AND-mask algorithm that demonstrably mitigates memorization and improves out-of-domain generalization. The paper is well motivated, clearly presented, and empirically convincing. Addressing the above concerns—especially regarding hyperparameter sensitivity, broader benchmarks, and computational cost—would further solidify its impact. I therefore recommend acceptance.

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
The authors introduce and formalize the concept of Invariant Learning Consistency (ICL), which is motivated by the idea that "good explanations are hard to vary" in the context of deep learning. Instead of using the arithmetic mean to pool gradients (logical OR), the authors propose to use the element-wise geometric mean of gradients with a logical AND masking. Experimental results on both synthetic and real-world data sets are reported under the setup of supervised learning and reinforcement learning. 

This paper is well written and clearly presented. The exploration of using geometric mean with a logical AND masking to pool gradients in deep learning is very interesting and novel. The proposed method learns invariances in gradient-based optimizations and can help with the memorization issue. This work has made good efforts towards a better understanding of learning, memorization and generalization of OOD. Experimental results on both synthetic and real-world data sets have demonstrated the effectiveness of the proposed AND-mask, comparing with commonly used regularizers and several baselines. 

As the authors point out, the AND-mask is one of multiple possible ways to improve consistency, and it is unlikely to be a practical algorithm for all applications. It would be great if the authors can provide further insights on what kind of data distributions (or applications) could potentially benefit from the proposed method versus using the arithmetic mean. In addition, it would be interesting to explore hybrid methods that combine the advantages of pooling gradients using arithmetic mean and geometric mean (i.e., AND-mask).

Rating:
9: Top 15% of accepted papers, strong accept
reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This paper introduces and formalizes the concept of Invariant Learning Consistency (ILC), motivated by the principle that “good explanations are hard to vary,” and shows that standard gradient averaging (the logical OR) can favor spurious “patchwork” solutions. To address this, the authors propose an AND-mask that zeros out gradient components whose signs disagree across environments, effectively pooling gradients via an element-wise geometric mean. A clear theoretical exposition links ILC to geometric vs. arithmetic means of Hessians, with convergence guarantees under diagonal Hessian and Gaussian gradient assumptions. Empirically, the method is evaluated on a synthetic spiral-vs-shortcut dataset, CIFAR-10 random-label memorization and label-noise settings, and behavioral cloning on CoinRun, demonstrating that the AND-mask can prevent memorization, resist label noise, and improve out-of-distribution generalization.

Strengths  
1. Novel Formalism and Theory: The ILC metric provides a precise, well-motivated measure of invariance grounded in causal principles. Connecting consistency to the geometric mean of Hessians and showing exponential suppression of inconsistent signals is both insightful and original.  
2. Simple, Efficient Algorithm: The AND-mask is lightweight to implement, requires no auxiliary networks, and enjoys theoretical convergence guarantees. It directly operationalizes “hard to vary” by filtering out conflicting gradient directions.  
3. Compelling Empirical Evidence: On the controlled synthetic spiral task—where invariant spirals must be recovered despite easy linear shortcuts—the AND-mask succeeds where baselines fail. On CIFAR-10 with random labels and label noise, it prevents overfitting without hurting standard accuracy. The CoinRun behavioral cloning experiment further shows improved generalization to unseen levels.  
4. Clarity and Reproducibility: The paper is well written, and the release of code and extensive appendices (gradient correlation analyses, proof details) enhance transparency.

Weaknesses and Suggestions  
1. Hyperparameter Sensitivity: The AND-mask introduces an agreement threshold τ that governs how strictly gradients must align. While initial experiments choose τ heuristically, its sensitivity and practical selection guidelines are not fully characterized. I recommend an ablation study over τ (and, more broadly, over batch size and learning rate) to show robustness and offer users concrete tuning advice.  
2. Limited Benchmark Diversity: Evaluation is currently confined to one synthetic dataset, CIFAR-10 variants, and a single RL environment. To strengthen claims of broad applicability, the authors should consider experiments on established domain-generalization benchmarks (e.g., Colored MNIST, PACS, VLCS, DomainBed or WILDS). Comparing against additional invariance methods such as REx, Fishr, Group DRO, or other gradient-agreement approaches would position the work more clearly relative to the state of the art.  
3. Theoretical Scope: Existing theoretical guarantees rely on diagonal Hessian and Gaussian assumptions, which may not hold in large, nonconvex deep models. A discussion of how these assumptions limit applicability—and, if possible, preliminary empirical insights into convergence behavior on nonconvex objectives—would help readers gauge when AND-mask is most effective.  
4. Computational Overhead: Per-example gradient computation and sign masking incur extra cost. A quantitative analysis of wall-clock training time, memory overhead, and integration strategies in common deep-learning frameworks would clarify the practicality of deploying the AND-mask at scale.  
5. Hybrid and Adaptive Extensions: As noted, the AND-mask may not be optimal for all data distributions. Exploring adaptive or learned aggregation strategies—potentially combining arithmetic mean and geometric mean pooling—or identifying data regimes where each approach excels would suggest fruitful directions for future work.

Overall Recommendation  
This work makes a significant conceptual and practical contribution by formalizing invariance via ILC and instantiating it with a novel AND-mask. Despite the need for broader benchmarks, deeper sensitivity analyses, and runtime profiling, the combination of clear theory, an elegant algorithm, and strong empirical results warrants acceptance. Rating: 9: Top 15% of accepted papers (strong accept).

reviews.original_rating: 9: Top 15% of accepted papers, strong accept

reviews.confidence: 3: The reviewer is fairly confident that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
this work posits that invariant mechanisms exist in a dataset. a machine learning algorithm that is trained using gradient descent usually averages gradients across examples. the thesis is that by averaging gradients, information is lost. the method posits that in a gradient descent algorithm, instead of an arithmetic average, a geometric (or karcher) mean can be used to preserve information about invariant mechanisms - while ignoring confounders. there are difficulties in a straightforward application of the geometric mean, so a simple heuristic algorithm is developed, involving masking gradients depending on whether the sign of the gradient agrees across a batch of examples (or, whether some agreement threshold is reached). this algorithm is tested on a synthetic dataset, a semi-synthetic task on CIFAR-10, and coinbase, an RL algorithm. 

recommend major revisions and baselines. it is interesting ideas and it would do the ideas justice to put a lot of effort into a rewrite so the ideas are properly understood by the ICLR community. clarity needs to be significantly improved in the introduction and conceptual framing (mainly in intro/methods sections)—the rest of the paper is largely well-written, the experiments are well-documented in the appendix, relationship to invariant risk minimization and causality is documented in appendix.

baselines: sum vs geometric mean is a well-known effect in products of experts vs sums of experts work. i think this should be the right baseline to compare to. train separate classifiers and use them as a product of experts, and you should get the same performance as in this paper. definitely need to discuss this related work and how learning consistency /geometric mean measures differ and warrant comparison.

the writing is more difficult to read than it ought to be. more work is needed to re-use existing concepts. for example, consistency is overloaded here (it might confuse some readers from a statistics background). the paper's title has the concepts of varying and explanations (variance and explanations do not appear in the method). small things like this abound. trying to find specific examples where i have a strong opinion to help:

* instead of consistency, it may be worth spending some time to think of another word that is less confusing. congruence? concordance? 'learning invariant mechanisms with geometric mean gradient descent' is an example of a title that would be easier to understand: (1) mechanisms relates to prior work such as the pearl work that is cited (indeed, in the appendix the authors state 'causality... is a key element informing our exposition'). a causal mechanism is a well-known concept to the ICLR community. (2) concordance is still overloaded (e.g. this is a major concept in biostatistics) but not to the degree that consistency is. similarly, if you choose congruence, this could have the nice geometric interpretation in the hessian example that is helpful for understanding the work in figure 3.

* the david deutsch quote distracts and confuses, remove it (i also know some researchers that might be immediately skeptical of a david deutsch citation in an ICLR paper). see above for ideas on title changes.

* the first example is helpful to understand the goals of the paper. however, figure 1 --  the symbol with the plus sign and arrow is confusing, all the axes tick labels need to be much larger

* the 'an example' chess example in the gray box on page 2 is unnecessary and distracting. instead, the discussion of the relationship to arjovsky et al could be inserted (with grass and cows), which is much more interesting and useful to a reader. 

* equation 1 engenders a double negative throughout the conceptual framing of the paper. i highly suggest considering renaming this to a 'consistency' score to reflect the name of the method; this will make it easier for readers to understand. 

* 'patchwork' may be another jargon word, try to find another one that is easier to understand. on page 3: 'low consistency of a classic patchwork solution' is loaded with jargon. first, a patchwork solution is not defined here or in previous work. second, a 'classic patchwork' solution is more confusing. removing adjectives such as 'classic' or even 'patchwork' throughout the paper may help readability and clarity. 

* i found the synthetic memorization dataset very difficult to understand. never start a sentence with math like p(y | x_{d_M}), it looked like it was connected to the previous with a \cdot. ideally write out the functional form of the mechanism. the axes in figure 5 are not labeled, so i have a hard time understanding what we are looking at: are the x and y axes d_S or d_M? how is environment A vs environment B defined? 

* re: wording -- ideally delete shortcuts. do not introduce more unnecessary lingo, because it leads to sentences like 'the shortcuts are not shared across environments, but provide a simple way to classify the data, even when pooling all the environments together'. this is asking a reader to do a lot of work: (1) what is pooling? this has a standard usage. (2) what are environments? this is used in RL (3) what are shortcuts, and how do they relate to environments? trying to rewrite this, i would want something with fewer novel concepts, that focuses on mechanisms that are invariant across datapoints or groups of datapoints (groups of data arising from causal graphs, e.g. in causal inference terminology).

* extremely minor, a few typos and inconsistent capitalization (section 3.1 is not capitalized, figure titles not capitalized, paragraph headings, figure legends not capitalized , good to pick one and stick to it)

Rating:
2: Strong rejection
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper posits that standard gradient descent—by averaging per‐example gradients arithmetically—loses information about invariant causal mechanisms and instead favors spurious “patchwork” solutions. To address this, the authors introduce an Invariant Learning Consistency (ILC) score linked to the geometric mean of Hessians and operationalize a simple heuristic—an AND‐mask that zeros gradient components whose signs disagree across a batch. Empirically, they test on a synthetic spirals‐plus‐shortcuts dataset, a semi-synthetic CIFAR-10 memorization/label‐noise task, and a behavioral-cloning CoinRun task.

Decision  
2: Strong rejection

Strengths  
• Novel formalization.  The ILC metric and its connection to arithmetic versus geometric gradient aggregation is an interesting theoretical contribution that builds on causal‐invariance principles (“good explanations are hard to vary”).  
• Simple, efficient algorithm.  The AND‐mask is lightweight, requires only per‐example gradient‐sign checks, and comes with intuitive theoretical guarantees (albeit under diagonal-Hessian/Gaussian assumptions).  
• Promising synthetic results.  On the spiral task the method indeed recovers the true invariant mechanism where standard pooling and IRM‐style baselines fail.  
• Empirical prevents memorization.  The AND-mask substantially mitigates CIFAR-10 random‐label memorization and resists label‐noise overfitting without degrading in-domain accuracy.  

Weaknesses and Required Revisions  
1. Clarity and Conceptual Framing  
   - The paper is difficult to read in places, especially the introduction and method framing. Key terms are overloaded (“consistency,” “patchwork,” “shortcuts,” “environments” and “pooling”) and may confuse readers from statistics or causality backgrounds.  For example, “patchwork solution” is never precisely defined, and the term “classic patchwork” adds little.  Renaming “consistency” to something like “concordance score” or “agreement score,” and choosing a clearer title (e.g. “Learning Invariant Mechanisms via Geometric‐Mean Gradient Aggregation”) would immediately improve readability.  
   - The David Deutsch quote in the introduction distracts rather than clarifies; we recommend removing it.  
   - The Section 3.1 heading, figure legends, and equation labels suffer from inconsistent capitalization and notation. Please standardize formatting and fix typos.  

2. Presentation of Examples and Figures  
   - Figure 1’s symbols and axes ticks are too small and the plus‐sign/arrow notation is confusing.  
   - The gray-boxed chess example on page 2 adds distraction; instead, a concise discussion of Arjovsky et al.’s grass‐and‐cow example would be more useful.  
   - In the synthetic memorization dataset description, equations begin a sentence with bare math (e.g. “p(y | x_{d_M})…”), leading to syntactic confusion.  Please rewrite in full functional form, clearly label axes in Figure 5, and explicitly define environment A vs. B.  

3. Missing Baselines and Related Work  
   - The effect of geometric versus arithmetic averaging is well known in “product of experts” versus “sum of experts.”  The authors should compare to a true product‐of‐experts baseline by training separate classifiers per environment and multiplying their outputs.  
   - Beyond IRM and DANN, recent invariant‐learning or robustness methods—REx, Fishr, Group DRO—should be included in the empirical comparison.  Evaluation on established domain‐generalization benchmarks (e.g. Colored MNIST, PACS, VLCS, WILDS or DomainBed) is also recommended to demonstrate broader applicability.  

4. Hyperparameter Sensitivity and Ablations  
   - The AND-mask introduces a threshold τ for agreement.  There is no ablation on its sensitivity or practical guidelines for choosing τ.  Please include a sweep over τ (and potentially batch size) to show how sensitive performance is.  
   - The paper does not report runtimes or memory overhead of computing per‐example gradients and masks, which can be substantial in large-scale deep learning frameworks.  Please quantify wall-clock and memory costs relative to standard training.  

5. Theoretical Limitations  
   - All convergence guarantees rely on diagonal Hessians and Gaussian gradient assumptions.  The paper should explicitly discuss how these assumptions break down in nonconvex deep nets and clarify the scope of the theory.  

Overall Evaluation  
The core idea—using geometric‐mean intuition to enforce invariant gradient signals—is compelling. However, the manuscript in its current form suffers from serious clarity issues, missing baselines, limited empirical scope, and insufficient hyperparameter and computational analysis. In light of these shortcomings, I recommend rejection. A major rewrite addressing the points above would be necessary before reconsideration at ICLR.
reviews.original_text:
Review:
this work posits that invariant mechanisms exist in a dataset. a machine learning algorithm that is trained using gradient descent usually averages gradients across examples. the thesis is that by averaging gradients, information is lost. the method posits that in a gradient descent algorithm, instead of an arithmetic average, a geometric (or karcher) mean can be used to preserve information about invariant mechanisms - while ignoring confounders. there are difficulties in a straightforward application of the geometric mean, so a simple heuristic algorithm is developed, involving masking gradients depending on whether the sign of the gradient agrees across a batch of examples (or, whether some agreement threshold is reached). this algorithm is tested on a synthetic dataset, a semi-synthetic task on CIFAR-10, and coinbase, an RL algorithm. 

recommend major revisions and baselines. it is interesting ideas and it would do the ideas justice to put a lot of effort into a rewrite so the ideas are properly understood by the ICLR community. clarity needs to be significantly improved in the introduction and conceptual framing (mainly in intro/methods sections)—the rest of the paper is largely well-written, the experiments are well-documented in the appendix, relationship to invariant risk minimization and causality is documented in appendix.

baselines: sum vs geometric mean is a well-known effect in products of experts vs sums of experts work. i think this should be the right baseline to compare to. train separate classifiers and use them as a product of experts, and you should get the same performance as in this paper. definitely need to discuss this related work and how learning consistency /geometric mean measures differ and warrant comparison.

the writing is more difficult to read than it ought to be. more work is needed to re-use existing concepts. for example, consistency is overloaded here (it might confuse some readers from a statistics background). the paper's title has the concepts of varying and explanations (variance and explanations do not appear in the method). small things like this abound. trying to find specific examples where i have a strong opinion to help:

'learning invariant mechanisms with geometric mean gradient descent' is an example of a title that would be easier to understand: (1) mechanisms relates to prior work such as the pearl work that is cited (indeed, in the appendix the authors state 'causality... is a key element informing our exposition'). a causal mechanism is a well-known concept to the ICLR community. (2) concordance is still overloaded (e.g. this is a major concept in biostatistics) but not to the degree that consistency is. similarly, if you choose congruence, this could have the nice geometric interpretation in the hessian example that is helpful for understanding the work in figure 3.

* equation 1 engenders a double negative throughout the conceptual framing of the paper. i highly suggest considering renaming this to a 'consistency' score to reflect the name of the method; this will make it easier for readers to understand.
