* i found the synthetic memorization dataset very difficult to understand. never start a sentence with math like p(y | x_{d_M}), it looked like it was connected to the previous with a \cdot. ideally write out the functional form of the mechanism. the axes in figure 5 are not labeled, so i have a hard time understanding what we are looking at: are the x and y axes d_S or d_M? how is environment A vs environment B defined? 

* re: wording -- ideally delete shortcuts. do not introduce more unnecessary lingo, because it leads to sentences like 'the shortcuts are not shared across environments, but provide a simple way to classify the data, even when pooling all the environments together'. this is asking a reader to do a lot of work: (1) what is pooling? this has a standard usage. (2) what are environments? this is used in RL (3) what are shortcuts, and how do they relate to environments? trying to rewrite this, i would want something with fewer novel concepts, that focuses on mechanisms that are invariant across datapoints or groups of datapoints (groups of data arising from causal graphs, e.g. in causal inference terminology).

* extremely minor, a few typos and inconsistent capitalization (section 3.1 is not capitalized, figure titles not capitalized, paragraph headings, figure legends not capitalized , good to pick one and stick to it)

Rating:
2: Strong rejection

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
The authors attempt to recover invariant solutions, defined as those with a similar loss neighbourhood across environments. This concept is interesting, especially since it can be incorporated with a clever understanding of loss gradients and hessians which underlies the generality of the proposed approach. 

The presentation is puzzling to me. The objective for all intents and purposes is to recover the causal solution from data in multiple environments, but this is never explicitly mentioned even though causality is said to be a "key element" in the Appendix. In this area, also precise conditions are needed on data distributions from different environments which seem to play no role in this paper. A more thorough description of what can be expected from invariant solutions and when we are expected to consistently recover them is needed in my opinion. The introduction is therefore also vague, what do we mean by generalizing out-of-sample? Is the objective to ensure good performance with arbitrary shifts in distribution while maintaining a causal graph? Is there an assumption that the invariant mechanism is unique among available environments?

Regarding the ILC, hessians and gradients I do see they are related but it is not lear to me how changing hessians would lead to optimizing for the ILC. The ILC is defined with respect to all local minima, I am not sure how such an objective would be attainable. More details here would be helpful.

In general, I found all experiments highly constructed and nothing like what you'd expect to see in reality. If the objective is improved performance out-of-sample, why not evaluate the method on domain generalization tasks (office, caltech datasets, etc.)? Invariant solutions may also be defined as minimizing a worst-case optimization problem across environments, yet the robust optimization literature is not mentioned.

Minor comments:
- I may be misunderstanding, but as described in the Appendix, does the synthetic experiment have shortcuts for both labels sampled from the same distribution?
- With unobserved confounding, it is known that the causal solution does not have zero loss gradient (for example in a linear model, least squares returns biased parameter estimates), what can we expect in that case?


In summary, I do believe the proposed approach to be interesting but the context in which it is expected to work, as well as its relationships with causality, robust optimization and domain generalization are not well described; this prevents me from raising my score at this moment.

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper introduces Invariant Learning Consistency (ILC) as a formal measure of “hard-to-vary” explanations across multiple training environments and proposes a practical AND-mask algorithm that zeros out per-example gradient components with inconsistent signs. The authors connect ILC to the geometric versus arithmetic mean of Hessians, provide theoretical analyses under diagonal‐Hessian and Gaussian gradient assumptions, and demonstrate empirically that the AND-mask recovers true invariant mechanisms on a synthetic spiral task, prevents memorization on CIFAR-10 with random or noisy labels, and yields modest improvements in generalization for a behavioral‐cloning task on CoinRun. While the core idea is novel and the lightweight mask is appealing, several conceptual and empirical gaps currently prevent me from recommending acceptance. I therefore maintain a rating of 5: marginally below the acceptance threshold.

Strengths  
• Formalization of invariance.  The ILC metric offers a clear, principled way to quantify invariance at minima, building on Deutsch’s “hard-to-vary” notion and linking consistency to Hessian geometry in a way that has not been previously explored.  
• Simple, efficient algorithm.  The AND-mask requires only sign comparisons of per-example gradients and is straightforward to implement as a “logical AND” across environments, with theoretical guarantees that inconsistent signals are suppressed exponentially fast under the paper’s assumptions.  
• Preventing spurious memorization.  On the synthetic spiral + shortcut task and random-label CIFAR-10, the AND-mask demonstrably avoids overfitting to easy, non-invariant features—an empirical property that conventional gradient averaging fails to ensure.  
• Broad task coverage.  Beyond vision, the paper includes a reinforcement-learning demonstration on CoinRun, illustrating potential applicability to behavioral-cloning settings.

Major Concerns  

1. Causal and distributional assumptions are underspecified  
   • Although the Appendix emphasizes causality as a “key element,” the main text never explicitly frames the objective as recovering a causal solution. Precise identifiability conditions—e.g., requirements on environment shifts, uniqueness of invariant mechanisms, or sufficiency of the data distributions—are absent. Without these, it is unclear when or why invariant minima correspond to the desired causal model.  
   • Related robust-optimization and domain-generalization literatures (e.g., Group DRO, REx, Fishr) are not adequately discussed. These works already formalize worst-case or distribution-shift objectives, and the connection (or difference) to ILC and AND-mask needs to be delineated.  

2. Theoretical limitations and clarity  
   • The analysis hinges on diagonal Hessian approximations and Gaussian gradient assumptions, yet modern deep nets are highly nonconvex and entail dense Hessians. How well do the theoretical guarantees migrate to practical scenarios?  
   • The definition of ILC relies on “all local minima,” but the paper does not clarify how one would optimize or even approximate this objective in nonconvex landscapes. Additional details or relaxed assumptions would strengthen the theoretical grounding.  
   • Sign conflicts and partial agreement in high-dimensional settings are noted in alternative reviews but not fully addressed. How should practitioners handle coordinates with borderline agreement or with small gradient magnitudes?

3. Experimental scope and reproducibility  
   • All experiments are highly constructed—synthetic spirals, CIFAR-10 memorization, and CoinRun. While illustrative, they do not reflect standard domain-generalization benchmarks (e.g., PACS, Colored MNIST, WILDS, DomainBed) where invariance methods are typically evaluated. Including one or more of these would demonstrate broader applicability.  
   • Hyperparameter sensitivity is underexplored. The AND-mask’s agreement threshold τ is critical, yet no ablation over τ, batch size, or learning rate is provided. Practitioners need guidelines on choosing τ and understanding its impact on convergence speed and generalization performance.  
   • Computational overhead and implementation details are omitted. Per-example gradients can be expensive in current deep-learning frameworks; quantifying wall-clock cost and memory requirements—especially on larger CNNs—would help assess practicality.  
   • Comparisons to recent baselines are limited. Beyond IRM and DANN, methods such as REx, Fishr, and Group DRO should be included to contextualize the AND-mask’s performance and efficiency trade-offs.

Minor Comments  
- In the synthetic experiment, do both spurious shortcuts sample labels from the same distribution? Clarifying the data-generation process would aid reproducibility.  
- Under unobserved confounding, causal solutions generally exhibit biased gradient estimates (e.g., least-squares under confounding does not zero out gradients). What behavior should one expect from the AND-mask or ILC in such cases?  
- Please specify random seeds, hyperparameter ranges, and early-stopping criteria in the Appendix for full reproducibility.

Conclusion  
The ILC formalism and AND-mask algorithm represent a promising new perspective on enforcing invariance via gradient aggregation. However, the current manuscript falls short in defining the underlying causal assumptions, extending theoretical guarantees beyond simplified settings, and validating on standard out-of-distribution benchmarks with robust comparisons and ablations. Addressing these issues could substantially strengthen the work, but in its present form I am not yet convinced it meets the bar for acceptance.

title: Graph Edit Networks

reviews.confidence: 1: The reviewer's evaluation is an educated guess
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
Graph Editing is a prominent research area which overlaps a variety of fields in computer science. As a typical example, a series of graphs - each obtained from its predecessor - can naturally represent the evolution of a system over time. From this viewpoint, it would be natural to obtain a means of predicting how such a series of graphs will evolve over time. The authors introduce a simple output layer (called "GEN") that can be used in graph neural networks to do precisely that.

On the theoretical side, the authors show that GENs have several useful properties, such as the ability to provide a constant-factor approximation of the Graph Edit Distance problem (assuming that a suitable teaching signal is provided). On the empirical side, the authors demonstrate the capabilities of GENs via several experimental evaluations, comparing the results to those of existing time series prediction approaches such as variational graph autoencoders. The experimental results are mostly encouraging: unlike existing systems, GENs can predict node insertions, and GENs also achieved higher accuracy for predicting edge operations.

The paper is well written. While the introduction and definitions are aimed mostly at researchers who have some knowledge on the topic (and hence I could not verify the details of the results), I believe that the paper would be of interest to the ICLR community. From what I have seen and understood, I think the contribution is also sufficient to warrant a presentation at the conference.

Questions:
-In Theorem 1, isn't it important to clarify (in the statement) that the translation can be carried out in polynomial time?
-Is there a specific reason for using the "Edit Cycles" and "Degree Rules" systems in the experiments? Both seem like rather arbitrary choices. Would it be possible to use GENs for systems where, e.g., we simply delete an edge incident to a highest-degree vertex in the graph? Or the highest-degree vertex?
-I'm curious whether GENs could be used to develop a neural network capable of learning to solve classical problems such as computing Treewidth... that problem can also be formulated as a graph editing task (via the elimination ordering characterization).

Minor remark:
Page 6: "GENs capability..." should maybe be "the capability of GENs..."; also, "Experiments are reported in three groups cover" should be "Experiments are reported in three groups and cover"

Post-Rebuttal Update:
I acknowledge having read the author's response and I also glanced over the new experiments.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper introduces Graph Edit Networks (GENs), a novel output layer for graph neural networks that directly predicts sparse edit scripts—node/edge insertions, deletions, and relabels—to transform an input graph into its successor. On the theoretical side, the authors prove that, given an optimal node mapping, GENs recover edit sequences within a constant additive factor of the graph edit distance (Theorem 1), and they argue that GEN inference runs in linear time on sparse graphs by making fully local decisions. Empirically, proof-of-concept experiments on synthetic graph dynamical systems (edit cycles, degree‐based rules, Game of Life), tree rewriting tasks, and a citation snapshot dataset demonstrate that GENs outperform variational graph autoencoders and kernel methods in edit‐precision and recall, while scaling linearly under practical edge filtering.

Strengths  
• Novel formulation for graph‐to‐graph prediction. By framing the output as sparse, sequential edits, GENs elegantly handle node set changes—an advantage over dense decoders or link‐prediction models that cannot insert or delete nodes.  
• Strong theoretical guarantees. The multi‐step teaching‐signal construction and the near‐optimality proof connecting GEN outputs to the graph edit distance extend and unify prior GED literature.  
• Computational efficiency and interpretability. GENs avoid costly pooling or quadratic decoders, operate in linear time for sparse inputs, and produce fully local edit decisions that are easy to interpret.  
• Empirical performance. On multiple synthetic benchmarks, GENs achieve near‐perfect accuracy on node/edge edits, substantially outperforming baselines (VGAE, VGRNN, kernel tree predictors), and exhibit promising scalability on large citation graphs.  
• Well-written and accessible. The exposition is clear, with precise definitions and motivating examples, making the paper of interest to ICLR’s audience.

Weaknesses and Suggestions  
1. Limited real‐world evaluation. All primary benchmarks are small or synthetic; real dynamic graph datasets (e.g., evolving social networks, traffic flows) are not included. Extending experiments to larger, real‐world graphs would validate generalization and scalability.  
2. Reliance on precomputed node mappings. Training requires an external GED or Hungarian‐based oracle, which may be expensive or infeasible on large graphs. An ablation study comparing optimal versus approximate mappings, or integrating a learned matching network, would clarify the impact of mapping quality on edit accuracy.  
3. Markovian assumption and temporal context. GENs are trained under a one‐step Markov model. Exploring non‐Markovian extensions—perhaps by combining GENs with recurrent GNNs—and evaluating on longer‐horizon predictions would broaden applicability.  
4. Ablation and sensitivity analyses. The paper currently lacks (a) comparison of multi-step teaching signals against a single-step variant to assess error accumulation, and (b) sensitivity studies on hyperparameters (hinge margins, edge‐filtering budget R). Including these would guide practitioners in setting and tuning GENs.  
5. Missing comparisons to dense graph generators. While VGAE and VGRNN are natural baselines, comparisons to recent graph‐to‐graph decoders (e.g., GraphRNN, conditional VAE/Transformer architectures) would strengthen claims about edit‐based decoders’ advantages.

Questions for the Authors  
– In Theorem 1, could you clarify in the statement whether the translation from mappings to teaching signals is guaranteed to run in polynomial time?  
– Why were the “Edit Cycles” and “Degree Rules” systems chosen for synthetic benchmarks? For instance, could GENs effectively learn a rule that always deletes an edge incident to the highest-degree vertex?  
– Have you considered using GENs to learn classical graph problems—such as computing treewidth via elimination orderings—since those too can be framed as editing tasks?

Minor Remarks  
– Page 6: change “GENs capability…” to “the capability of GENs…”.  
– Page 6: revise “Experiments are reported in three groups cover” to “Experiments are reported in three groups and cover”.

Recommendation  
7: Good paper, accept. Despite areas for further empirical validation and ablation, this work offers a compelling, theoretically grounded approach to sparse graph editing that is likely to spark valuable follow-up research.

reviews.confidence: 5: The reviewer is absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct and very familiar with the relevant literature
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
Summary
-------
This paper proposes a model that, given one graph, predicts a sequence of edits that transforms this graph to the next one in a sequence of evolving graphs. To this end, it proposes the graph edit network (GEN), which is a linear output layer that transforms node embeddings to a set of scores that are then used to deterministically select graph edits. The space of edits predictable by this model covers the full space of graph changes. The paper moreover shows that, given a graph matching and a pair of graphs, we can algorithmically find a near-optimal graph edit sequence for generating training data. Finally, the paper demonstrates the model's capabilities on a set of synthetic benchmarks.

Strong points
------------------

+ The paper is well written and properly embedded in the literature.
+ The approach of solving graph transformation by predicting a complete sequence of graph edits (i.e. including deletion) is novel and certainly very interesting.

Weak points
------------------

- I am not really convinced by the approach. There is no way for the proposed model to know if it is predicting step K+1 of the ongoing edit sequence or step 1 of a new sequence (put differently: the model has no memory/is stateless; unlike to e.g. existing recurrent graph generative models). There is also no signal telling the user that the edit sequence is finished. So it seems to me that neither the model nor the user can know which edit steps are actually predicted graphs and which are just intermediate states. 
- The experiments only use synthetic toy datasets and a single baseline each. There are no comparisons with recently published models using the common, but supposedly inferior method of generating the next graph from scratch.
- Theorem 1 does not "prove that graph edit networks can approximate the NP-hard graph edit distance" or show that "edits predicted by the GEN can be sharply bounded to be close to optimal", as (incorrectly) claimed in the introduction/abstract. The theorem merely shows that the space of possible edits is complete and that the edit sequence length of your training data can be bounded if you have an (NP-hard) optimal graph matching. There is no guarantee whatsoever on what the GEN might predict. Also, GEN doesn't use any mapping, so the statement "provided that an optimal mapping is used" is rather confusing.
- Theorem 1 merely transforms the graph edit problem into a graph matching problem. This is stated on page 5, but considering that graph matching is still NP-hard, the importance of Theorem 1 seems to be overstated throughout the rest of the paper. Especially since it is largely just a variant of Proposition 1 by Bougleux et al. 2017 (as cited).
- The hinge loss with margin is rather common in deep learning, see e.g. [1]. The novelty of the proposed loss function is thus significantly overstated. [Side note: Its description on page 6 also seems overly complicated. I would recommend that you simplify this.]
- The paper states that the model can achieve linear instead of quadratic runtime if the number of edge edits are limited. While this might be true, there is no measure on how such a constraint would affect model performance. The experiment described in the last paragraph of the experimental section only provides some data on scaling behavior, not on accuracy. Especially considering that this restriction limits the number of edits by the model and thus breaks its universality it can severely affect the outcome. [Side note: The number of non-negative edge filter scores actually does not need to be constant, it merely needs to scale with sqrt(N) instead of N. So you actually have more wiggle room than you allow yourself.]

Recommendation
--------------
I recommend rejection, mainly because of significantly lacking experiments and overstated theoretical results. The presented theoretical results are interesting but do not really give deep insights about proposed approach (specifically Theorem 1). The overall theoretical contribution is significantly smaller than the authors make them out to be.

Feedback for the authors
------------------------
I really appreciate the novel approach of predicting a complete instead of a limited edit sequence. I would recommend acceptance if you a) significantly extend the experiments to include real (ideally practically relevant) graph sequences and directly compare to multiple relevant methods, which you already mention in the related work, b) solve the stopping problem mentioned above (i.e. distinguishing between another and the first edit step), and c) make your statements more accurate and avoid overstating your results. Since especially points a) and b) are unlikely possible to be solved within 2 weeks I strongly encourage you to build on this work and submit a better version at the next conference.

[1] Junbo Zhao, Michael Mathieu, and Yann LeCun. Energy-based generative adversarial network. ICLR 2017


[Update after author responses]: The authors have addressed most of my concerns and I've updated the score accordingly.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary
-------
This paper proposes Graph Edit Networks (GENs), a GNN output layer that predicts a sparse sequence of node and edge edits—insertions, deletions, relabels—to transform an input graph into its successor. Building on a deterministic construction of near-optimal edit scripts from node mappings, the authors derive a teaching signal and train GEN end-to-end with a hinge-loss formulation. They report nearly perfect edit recall and precision on several synthetic graph dynamical systems, tree rewriting tasks, and demonstrate linear inference scaling on a citation snapshot via edge filtering.

Strengths
---------
• Novel edit-sequence formulation.  Rather than generating an entire graph in one shot, GEN directly models the sequence of atomic edits (covering the full space of graph changes), elegantly handling node-set changes that dense decoders or link-prediction models struggle with.  
• Solid theoretical grounding.  The paper connects node mappings, edit scripts, and graph edit distance, showing that under optimal mappings and expressive GNN encoders, GEN can recover edit sequences within an additive bound of the true distance. This extends prior GED literature in a clean, constructive way.  
• Efficient, local decisions.  By scoring edits per node or edge, GEN avoids quadratic pooling or decoding layers, yielding linear inference time on sparse graphs when the edit budget is limited.  
• Clear exposition and organization.  The manuscript is well written, situates itself appropriately in the literature, and the multi-step teaching protocol is described in sufficient detail.  
• Proof-of-concept validation.  On three synthetic dynamical systems, two tree-rewriting tasks, and a max-degree benchmark, GEN achieves perfect or near-perfect edit recovery, outperforming baseline VGAE/VGRNN and kernel-based methods.

Weaknesses
----------
1. Stateless / stopping criterion.  
   As noted in the main review, GEN is essentially Markovian: it has no internal memory or explicit signal for when the edit sequence should terminate. This makes it unclear how to distinguish “final” edits from intermediate states or how to chain predictions over longer horizons without an external stopping rule.  
2. Limited empirical scope.  
   All experiments are on synthetic or small semi-synthetic datasets. There is no evaluation on real-world dynamic-graph benchmarks (e.g. social, traffic, molecular evolution) or comparison to established dynamic GNNs (Dyngraph2vec, EvolveGCN) or graph-to-graph decoders (GraphRNN, GCPN, Transformer-based methods).  
3. Reliance on optimal node mappings.  
   Training requires computing or approximating a node correspondence via GED or Hungarian assignment—an NP-hard or expensive step in practice. The paper does not assess GEN’s robustness to imperfect mappings nor provide an avenue for mapping-free training.  
4. Overstated theoretical claims.  
   While Theorem 1 constructs a complete edit space and bounds training-data sequence length, it does not guarantee GEN’s predictions will be near-optimal in deployment. The notion of “sharp bounds” on GEN’s outputs is not supported, and the hinge loss novelty is modest given its prevalence in prior works.  
5. Missing ablations and sensitivity analyses.  
   There is no study of how edit accuracy degrades with imperfect or approximate mappings, nor comparison of multi-step versus single-step teaching signals. Hyperparameter sensitivity (margin thresholds, edge-filter budget R) and training stability are not explored, leaving practitioners without clear guidance.  
6. Unquantified trade-off for linear runtime.  
   The claimed linear complexity under a constrained number of edge edits is not tied to accuracy: we lack experiments showing how restricting R affects edit recovery in practice.

Actionable Suggestions
----------------------
• Broaden empirical evaluation to at least one real dynamic graph dataset (e.g. social network evolution, traffic flows, citation updates) and benchmark against both dynamic GNNs and dense graph generation models.  
• Assess robustness to mapping noise by comparing optimal versus approximate mappings, or by integrating a learned matching module to reduce dependence on oracles.  
• Provide ablations on multi-step vs. single-step teaching, and measure error accumulation when chaining predictions over multiple time steps.  
• Include a sensitivity analysis for key hyperparameters (hinge-loss margin, edge-filter budget) to guide future users.  
• Discuss or prototype integration with recurrent GNN layers (e.g. GRU-based memory) to address the statelessness and introduce an internal stopping mechanism for edit sequences.  
• Refine theoretical statements to clarify what Theorem 1 guarantees and simplify the hinge-loss description, removing any overstated claims about novelty or bound tightness.

Recommendation
--------------
I rate this paper a 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold. GEN presents an innovative and theoretically grounded approach to explicit graph editing, but its empirical validation is currently too narrow and some theoretical claims are overstated. Addressing these concerns—especially broader, real-world experiments and robustness analyses—would solidify GEN’s contributions and practical impact.
reviews.original_text:
Review:
Summary
-------
This paper proposes a model that, given one graph, predicts a sequence of edits that transforms this graph to the next one in a sequence of evolving graphs. To this end, it proposes the graph edit network (GEN), which is a linear output layer that transforms node embeddings to a set of scores that are then used to deterministically select graph edits. The space of edits predictable by this model covers the full space of graph changes. The paper moreover shows that, given a graph matching and a pair of graphs, we can algorithmically find a near-optimal graph edit sequence for generating training data. Finally, the paper demonstrates the model's capabilities on a set of synthetic benchmarks.

Strong points
------------------

+ The paper is well written and properly embedded in the literature.
+ The approach of solving graph transformation by predicting a complete sequence of graph edits (i.e. including deletion) is novel and certainly very interesting.

Weak points
------------------

- I am not really convinced by the approach. There is no way for the proposed model to know if it is predicting step K+1 of the ongoing edit sequence or step 1 of a new sequence (put differently: the model has no memory/is stateless; unlike to e.g. existing recurrent graph generative models). There is also no signal telling the user that the edit sequence is finished. So it seems to me that neither the model nor the user can know which edit steps are actually predicted graphs and which are just intermediate states. 
- The experiments only use synthetic toy datasets and a single baseline each. There are no comparisons with recently published models using the common, but supposedly inferior method of generating the next graph from scratch.
- Theorem 1 does not "prove that graph edit networks can approximate the NP-hard graph edit distance" or show that "edits predicted by the GEN can be sharply bounded to be close to optimal", as (incorrectly) claimed in the introduction/abstract. The theorem merely shows that the space of possible edits is complete and that the edit sequence length of your training data can be bounded if you have an (NP-hard) optimal graph matching. There is no guarantee whatsoever on what the GEN might predict. Also, GEN doesn't use any mapping, so the statement "provided that an optimal mapping is used" is rather confusing.
- Theorem 1 merely transforms the graph edit problem into a graph matching problem. This is stated on page 5, but considering that graph matching is still NP-hard, the importance of Theorem 1 seems to be overstated throughout the rest of the paper. Especially since it is largely just a variant of Proposition 1 by Bougleux et al. 2017 (as cited).
- The hinge loss with margin is rather common in deep learning, see e.g. [1]. The novelty of the proposed loss function is thus significantly overstated. [Side note: Its description on page 6 also seems overly complicated. I would recommend that you simplify this.]
- The paper states that the model can achieve linear instead of quadratic runtime if the number of edge edits are limited. While this might be true, there is no measure on how such a constraint would affect model performance. The experiment described in the last paragraph of the experimental section only provides some data on scaling behavior, not on accuracy. Especially considering that this restriction limits the number of edits by the model and thus breaks its universality it can severely affect the outcome. [Side note: The number of non-negative edge filter scores actually does not need to be constant, it merely needs to scale with sqrt(N) instead of N. So you actually have more wiggle room than you allow yourself.]

Recommendation
--------------
I recommend rejection, mainly because of significantly lacking experiments and overstated theoretical results. The presented theoretical results are interesting but do not really give deep insights about proposed approach (specifically Theorem 1). The overall theoretical contribution is significantly smaller than the authors make them out to be.

Feedback for the authors
------------------------
I really appreciate the novel approach of predicting a complete instead of a limited edit sequence. I would recommend acceptance if you a) significantly extend the experiments to include real (ideally practically relevant) graph sequences and directly compare to multiple relevant methods, which you already mention in the related work, b) solve the stopping problem mentioned above (i.e. distinguishing between another and the first edit step), and c) make your statements more accurate and avoid overstating your results. Since especially points a) and b) are unlikely possible to be solved within 2 weeks I strongly encourage you to build on this work and submit a better version at the next conference.

[1] Junbo Zhao, Michael Mathieu, and Yann LeCun. Energy-based generative adversarial network. ICLR 2017


[Update after author responses]: The authors have addressed most of my concerns and I've updated the score accordingly.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold

reviews.confidence: 2: The reviewer is willing to defend the evaluation, but it is quite likely that the reviewer did not understand central parts of the paper
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
The authors consider Graphical Neural Networks in the time series model: a sequence of "edits" (in its general form) describe the difference between two graphs. This approach generalizes over previous works where changes on node attributes or addition and deletion of lone edges were considered.
The advantages of this work are more pronounced in applications where the output of the neural network can be encoded as a number of graph edits (this number being much smaller than the size of the whole graph). This is well motivated.
The only weakness of this work is its technical novelty. I think all the ingredients of the work were already available. Though I like the combination as well as their theoretical analysis.
I also think the paper is relevant enough to be cited:
@inproceedings{li2019graph,
  title={Graph Matching Networks for Learning the Similarity of Graph Structured Objects},
  author={Li, Yujia and Gu, Chenjie and Dullien, Thomas and Vinyals, Oriol and Kohli, Pushmeet},
  booktitle={International Conference on Machine Learning},
  pages={3835--3845},
  year={2019}
}

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents Graph Edit Networks (GENs), a novel graph neural network output layer that predicts sparse sequences of graph edits—node insertions, deletions, relabels and edge modifications—to transform an input graph into a target graph. By framing graph‐to‐graph prediction as a sequence of local edit operations, the authors generalize beyond prior work that only handled node attribute changes or single‐edge updates. They provide a rigorous theoretical analysis showing that, under an optimal node mapping and with sufficiently expressive GNNs, GENs recover edit scripts whose total cost is within an additive 2(C–1) term of the true graph edit distance (C = number of connected components). Empirically, proof‐of‐concept experiments on three synthetic dynamical systems, two tree‐rewriting tasks and a citation network demonstrate near‐perfect edit precision/recall and linear‐time inference via edge filtering.

Strengths  
• Elegant, sparse output formulation: The edit‐based decoder naturally handles changes in both topology and node sets, overcoming the limitations of dense decoders or pure link‐prediction models.  
• Rigorous theoretical grounding: The connection between graph mappings, edit scripts and graph edit distance is clearly established, extending classical GED results to a learned GNN setting.  
• Efficiency and scalability: GENs make fully local decisions and avoid quadratic pooling layers. A scalability study on citation snapshots confirms near‐linear inference time when combined with edge filtering.  
• Strong synthetic performance: On a variety of synthetic benchmarks (including Game of Life, degree‐based and cycle‐based rules) and tree rewriting tasks, GENs achieve perfect or near‐perfect accuracy, outperforming variational graph autoencoders, kernel methods and other baselines.  
• Practical relevance: The ability to output concise edit scripts can be valuable in applications where graph size is large but edits are sparse—this motivation is well articulated.

Weaknesses and Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Limited real‐world evaluation  
   • All experiments are conducted on small or synthetic datasets, with only a single proof‐of‐concept citation snapshot. The paper would be significantly strengthened by evaluating GENs on real dynamic graph benchmarks—e.g. evolving social networks, traffic networks, or molecular reaction graphs—where node churn and richer temporal dependencies occur. Comparing to recent dynamic GNNs (e.g. Dyngraph2vec, EvolveGCN) or graph generative models (e.g. GraphRNN, GCPN) would provide a more comprehensive assessment.  

2. Reliance on external node mappings  
   • GEN training depends on precomputed graph mappings (oracle or approximate GED), which are NP‐hard in general and may be expensive or noisy in practice. An ablation study on mapping quality—comparing optimal mappings, Hungarian-based approximations and off-the-shelf GED heuristics—would clarify how sensitive GEN performance is to imperfect correspondences. Investigating a learned matching network or mapping‐free training scheme could broaden applicability.  

3. Markovian assumption and temporal context  
   • Currently, GENs assume a first‐order Markov process (one‐step edits). The discussion of extending to longer histories or integrating with recurrent GNNs is brief. Providing at least a toy experiment that combines GENs with sequence models (e.g. an RNN‐based GNN) would illustrate how to relax this limitation in practice.  

4. Missing ablations and hyperparameter sensitivity  
   • There is no analysis of the multi‐step teaching signal versus a single‐step or end‐to‐end loss, nor of how errors accumulate when chaining multiple GEN predictions over time. Including an ablation on teaching signal construction and measuring forecast quality over several steps would help practitioners understand stability and error propagation.  
   • Key hyperparameters—margin thresholds in the hinge loss, edge‐filtering budget R, and cost‐weighted edit loss weights—are not systematically explored. A sensitivity analysis would guide users in tuning GENs for new domains.  

5. Baseline comparisons  
   • While variational encoders and kernel methods serve as useful points of reference, comparison to dense graph‐to‐graph decoders or conditional generative models would strengthen claims about efficiency and accuracy trade‐offs.  

Minor Comments  
• Please cite and discuss related work on Graph Matching Networks (Li et al., ICML 2019) and recent conditional graph generation frameworks, to better position GENs within the literature.  
• Provide implementation details and hyperparameter settings in an appendix to facilitate reproducibility.  

Conclusion  
Overall, this paper offers a compelling and well‐motivated approach to sparse graph editing with solid theoretical guarantees and convincing synthetic results. Although the technical components—GNNs, GED approximations and teaching signals—are individually known, their combination into a unified, efficient edit‐based framework is novel and practically relevant. I recommend acceptance (Rating: 7: Good paper) subject to strengthening the empirical evaluation on real‐world dynamic graphs, ablations on mapping quality and hyperparameter sensitivity, and more extensive baselines.

reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This paper proposes an algorithm to learn dynamics on graphs: node scores are computed by a GNN and then used to predict the modification of the graph (node/edge insertion/deletion). 

The training of the GNN is unclear to me. In particular, the authors do not describe how they obtain the 'teaching signal'. Theorem 1 is of little help as 'graph mapping' are not defined.

I have a fundamental problem with this paper: it looks like the GNN is trained to mimic the teaching signal but as claimed by the authors on page 5 '...the key theoretical result of our paper yields a simple way to construct teaching signals...'. Hence what is the point of using a GNN if a simple algorithm gives a satisfactory answer?

I do not agree with the authors about the expressive power of GEN: for simplicity, consider a cycle graph, then for a GNN as the one described in (2), the node representation will all be the same. Hence the node scores will be the same for all nodes and the algorithm 1 will have the same output for all nodes and edges. What am I missing?

[After rebuttal]  Thanks for the clarification, the aim of the authors becomes clearer. However I still think the paper requires more work before publication. 
Your definition of graph mapping is still unclear. From your definition, it looks like the only dependence of $\Psi$ with respect to the graphs $G_t$ and $G_{t+1}$ are through their number of vertices N and M: " bijective mappingψ:{1,...,M+N} → {1,...,M+N}with  the  additional  restriction  that  for  the  setInsψ:={j≤N|ψ−1(j)> M}we  obtainψ−1(Insψ) ={M+ 1,...,M+|Insψ|}"
How can this mapping be related to the edit distance of $G_t$ and $G_{t+1}$.
After reading the other reviews, I think the authors should clarify if their gaph mapping is related to the standard graph matching pb see https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Graph_matching.

Rating:
3: Clear rejection
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper proposes Graph Edit Networks (GENs), an output layer for graph neural networks that predicts sparse, local edit scripts—node/edge insertions, deletions, and relabels—to transform an input graph into its successor. The model computes node scores via a GNN and uses these scores to guide a step-by-step graph editing procedure. A key theoretical claim (Theorem 1) is that, under an optimal node mapping, GEN recovers near‐optimal edits up to an additive term dependent on the number of connected components. Empirical results on synthetic graph dynamical systems and tree rewriting tasks report near‐perfect precision/recall, and a small scalability study suggests linear inference time with edge filtering.

Strengths  
- Novel formulation: Predicting explicit, sparse edit sequences addresses graph‐to‐graph tasks—including topology changes and node churn—in a more direct way than dense decoders or link‐prediction models.  
- Theoretical grounding: The paper offers rigorous connections between node mappings, teaching‐signal construction, and the graph edit distance, extending prior GED literature.  
- Efficient, local decisions: GEN operates in linear time for sparse graphs, avoiding costly quadratic decoders or global pooling layers.  
- Proof‐of‐concept performance: On several synthetic benchmarks (e.g., Game of Life, degree‐based rewrite rules) and tree transformations, GEN achieves high edit‐recovery accuracy, outperforming variational autoencoder baselines.

Weaknesses  
1. Unclear training signals and undefined mappings  
   - The manuscript does not clearly describe how teaching signals are obtained in practice. Theorem 1 invokes “graph mappings,” yet these are never formally defined beyond a size‐based bijection. It remains unclear how this mapping relates to the standard graph‐matching or graph‐edit-distance problems.  
   - If the theoretical result already yields a simple edit script construction, the necessity of a learned GNN layer is not justified.  
2. Expressive‐power concerns  
   - As formulated in Equation (2), the GNN produces identical node representations on regular structures (e.g., cycles), forcing all node scores—and thus all edit decisions—to be identical. This raises doubts about the model’s capacity to distinguish local edit contexts.  
3. Reliance on—oracle—node mappings  
   - All experiments assume access to ground‐truth or optimal node mappings, but computing such mappings (e.g., via the Hungarian algorithm or exact GED) is NP‐hard in general and may not scale to realistic graphs. The sensitivity of GEN to imperfect or approximate mappings is not assessed.  
4. Limited empirical evaluation  
   - Experimental validation is restricted to small, synthetic, or semi‐synthetic datasets. There is no evaluation on real‐world dynamic graphs (e.g., social networks, traffic data), nor comparison to state‐of‐the‐art dynamic GNNs (e.g., Dyngraph2vec, EvolveGCN) or dense graph‐to‐graph decoders (e.g., GraphRNN, GCPN).  
5. Missing ablations and sensitivity analyses  
   - The paper does not study the impact of mapping quality (optimal vs. approximate), multi‐step versus single‐step teaching signals, or hyperparameter choices (margin thresholds, edge‐filtering budget). Error accumulation over multiple prediction steps is not examined.  

Actionable Suggestions  
1. Clarify and formalize “graph mappings.”  Explicitly define the mapping notion, relate it to standard graph‐matching formalisms (e.g., the graph edit distance or maximum common subgraph), and detail how it is computed or approximated in practice.  
2. Justify the role of the GNN.  If a simple, non‐learned algorithm can generate near‐optimal edit scripts, explain what additional capacity the GNN provides. Consider experiments contrasting the learned GNN outputs against the base construction.  
3. Address expressive‐power issues.  Analyze whether more powerful aggregation or positional encodings can prevent the collapse of node representations on regular graphs (e.g., cycles).  
4. Evaluate on real dynamic‐graph benchmarks.  Include at least one real‐world dataset with node/edge churn, compare to dynamic‐GNN baselines, and report end‐to‐end graph similarity or downstream task performance.  
5. Perform ablation studies.  Investigate:  
   • The effect of approximate versus optimal node mappings on edit accuracy.  
   • Single‐step versus multi‐step teaching signals, measuring error propagation when predictions are chained.  
   • Sensitivity to margin parameters and edge‐filtering thresholds.  
6. Discuss complexity trade‐offs.  Provide empirical timing for both mapping computation and GNN training/inference, and analyze scalability as graph size grows.

Decision  
Rating: 3 (Clear rejection)  
While GENs present an interesting and theoretically grounded approach to sparse graph editing, critical aspects—namely, the precise definition and computation of graph mappings, the necessity of the GNN component, expressive‐power limitations, and a lack of real‐world validation—must be substantially addressed before the work can be considered for publication.

title: Sample-Efficient Automated Deep Reinforcement Learning

reviews.confidence: 5: The reviewer is absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct and very familiar with the relevant literature
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper addresses the sample‐efficiency and hyperparameter sensitivity challenges of deep reinforcement learning by introducing SEARL, a population‐based automated RL (AutoRL) framework that jointly evolves agent hyperparameters and neural architectures while sharing a global replay buffer. Instantiated with TD3 on MuJoCo (and extended to Rainbow DQN on Atari in the supplemental ablations), SEARL demonstrates up to a 10× reduction in environment interactions compared to baselines such as random search and a modified Population-Based Training (PBT) algorithm, making a compelling case for meta‐optimization in off‐policy settings.

Strengths  
• Novelty and Impact: The key innovation—shared experience replay across a population—addresses sample inefficiency head‐on and yields an order‐of‐magnitude improvement in meta‐optimization sample reuse. This component alone differentiates SEARL from prior PBT and evolutionary RL methods and underpins the most dramatic empirical gains reported.  
• Clarity and Reproducibility: The paper is exceptionally well written, with clear motivation, thorough related‐work discussion, detailed pseudocode, and open‐source code release. The evaluation protocol is transparent, accounting for total environment steps and decoupling meta- and actor-learner interactions.  
• Comprehensive Ablations: The ablation studies systematically peel apart the contributions of shared replay, hyperparameter scheduling, architecture adaptation, and Lamarckian weight transfer. These experiments convincingly attribute the majority of sample‐efficiency gains to shared replay, while also highlighting the (albeit smaller) role of dynamic hyperparameter and architecture mutations.  
• Robust Empirical Validation: Results on five MuJoCo tasks (and additional Atari tests) demonstrate consistent 5–10× speedups over strong baselines, with robustness checks across random seeds and held‐out hyperparameter settings.

Weaknesses  
• Baseline Scope: While random search and modified PBT are appropriate initial comparators, the manuscript omits recent gradient‐based hyperparameter optimization (e.g., metagradient methods or HOOF) and Bayesian HPO approaches. Including one or two of these would better contextualize SEARL’s advantages beyond evolutionary techniques.  
• Wall‐Clock and Compute Costs: The synchronous evolutionary loop may incur nontrivial wall‐clock overhead and resource usage. Reporting GPU/CPU hours or parallel efficiency would confirm that sample‐efficiency gains translate into practical speedups.  
• Architecture Adaptation Impact: As noted in the ablation (Figure 5), neural‐architecture mutations yield limited benefit on MuJoCo’s relatively simple tasks. Extending evaluations to more complex domains or richer search spaces (e.g., convolutional modules) could better showcase the value of online architecture search.  
• Statistical Rigor: Experiments rely on a modest number of random seeds (5–10), and confidence intervals on final returns are narrow. Additional seeds and statistical hypothesis testing would increase confidence in the reported gains.  
• Scope of Applicability: SEARL is currently restricted to off‐policy methods. A brief case study or discussion on extending shared replay to on‐policy algorithms (e.g., PPO or SAC) would broaden the framework’s appeal.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Add one or two additional baselines—such as a metagradient HPO method or Bayesian optimizer—to strengthen comparative claims.  
2. Report wall‐clock training time, compute usage, and, if feasible, an asynchronous variant to demonstrate scalability.  
3. Include sensitivity analyses over population size, mutation rates, and hyperparameter search spaces to guide practitioners.  
4. Increase seed counts and apply statistical tests to bolster the robustness of results.  
5. Discuss or preliminarily evaluate SEARL on an on‐policy algorithm or a more complex control suite to underscore generality.

Overall Assessment  
SEARL presents a clear, well‐motivated, and practically impactful advance in automated RL. By making shared replay across a hyperparameter‐evolving population its central contribution, the paper delivers strong empirical improvements in sample efficiency. While comparisons to recent HPO methods and additional runtime analyses would further strengthen the work, the core ideas and results merit acceptance.

Rating: 7 – Good paper, accept

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
Summary：
In this paper, the authors intend to propose an efficient automated reinforcement learning (RL) framework. To achieve this goal, they integrate three technologies, i.e., evolutionary RL for hyperparameter search, evolvable neural network for policy network design, and shared experience replay for improving data usage. The paper uses a case study on MuJoCo to demonstrate the claimed advantages over baselines.

Some pros:
1. The motivation is good. The automation of reinforcement learning is beneficial to the research community, especially for researchers who are not familiar with RL but in need of it.
2. The framework is general and friendly to users. This framework is general and can be regarded as a plug-in module for a series of reinforcement learning methods. Besides, as we all know, both of the autoML and RL have a heavy computational burden, the adaption of evolvable neural network and shared experience replay greatly alleviate this dilemma. 
3. The organization of this paper is easy to follow. We can follow the authors from why they want to deal with the problem, to how they are inspired by existing work, and then to how the algorithms are design based on the questions to be answered and the existing technologies. 

Some cons:
1. The experiment is far from enough. Actually, this paper only has a case study. First, the author claims that the framework can optimize arbitrary off-policy RL algorithms, why only try on TD3? From the perspective of robustness, the authors need to compare more off-policy algorithms with and without the proposed method. Second, the paper claims there is no directly comparable approach for efficient AutoRL, which I do not agree with. In its own related work, many AutoRL baselines are listed, e.g., H. L. Chiang et al.  'Learning navigation behaviors end-to-end with autorl', F. Runge et al. 'Learning to design RNA'. For these baselines, they can either take the same exploration steps and compare performance with the proposed method, or compare the performance/computational cost when reaching the same performance. Anyway, the readers expect to see more comparisons with more baselines from more perspectives. Third, the authors claim that to tackle the non-stationarity of the RL problem, existing studies can substantially increase the number of environment interactions, implying the proposed framework has advantages on non-stationarity RL environments, but still, no experimental results are given.
2. Some technical details are missing. The logic is clear, the solution is reasonable, but the details are ignored. It is a good idea to keep the writing compact, but the lack of details may harm the readability of the paper. For example, since this is a general framework, how should we design hyperparameter settings of SEARL in initialization for different algorithms? In the training part, why should individual be trained for as many steps as frames have been generated in the evaluation phase, and why the training time could be reduced by using only a fraction j of the steps?

6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper introduces SEARL, a population-based automated reinforcement learning (AutoRL) framework that jointly evolves hyperparameters and neural-network architectures for off-policy deep RL agents, while sharing a common experience replay buffer across the population to improve sample efficiency. The authors present a case study on MuJoCo using TD3, showing that SEARL can yield substantial reductions in environment interactions compared to vanilla TD3, random search, and a modified Population-Based Training (PBT). The paper is generally well organized, and the motivation—to lower the barrier of RL adoption via automated tuning—is clear.

Strengths  
1. Strong Motivation and Generality  
   • The automation of RL hyperparameter and architecture tuning is a pressing need, especially for practitioners without deep RL expertise. SEARL’s design as a plug-in module makes it broadly applicable to off-policy algorithms.  
2. Novel Integration of Techniques  
   • By combining evolutionary hyperparameter search, Lamarckian-style network mutations, and a shared replay buffer, the framework addresses both the non-stationarity of RL and the heavy sample demands of meta-optimization. This “three-way” integration, while building on existing ideas (e.g., ERL, PBT), is executed cleanly and demonstrates real gains.  
3. Clear Organization and Reproducibility  
   • The paper walks the reader through motivation, related work, algorithmic design, and case-study results in a logical progression. Pseudocode and the promise of open-source code further support reproducibility.  
4. Empirical Ablations  
   • Even within the limited MuJoCo study, the authors conduct component ablation to isolate the contributions of architecture evolution, hyperparameter adaptation, and shared replay, following a fair interaction-count evaluation protocol.

Weaknesses and Areas for Improvement  
1. Limited Experimentation Scope  
   • The evaluation is confined to TD3 on MuJoCo. Given the claim of generality, results on at least one additional off-policy method (e.g., Rainbow DQN as in recent AutoRL work) or an on-policy algorithm (e.g., SAC or PPO) would strengthen robustness claims. Demonstrations in more diverse settings—short-horizon tasks (Reacher), sparse-reward domains, or a real-robot control benchmark—could further validate applicability.  
2. Incomplete Baseline Comparisons  
   • The manuscript asserts a lack of directly comparable AutoRL methods, yet recent approaches such as metagradient-based HPO, bandit-based schemes (HOOF), and Bayesian optimization with early stopping are not included. Incorporating one or two of these baselines would contextualize SEARL’s benefits beyond random search and PBT.  
3. Missing Runtime and Scalability Analysis  
   • Sample-efficiency gains are compelling, but wall-clock compute cost, GPU/CPU usage, and scalability (synchronous vs. asynchronous operation) are not reported. Practitioners need to know whether fewer environment interactions translate to faster end-to-end turn-around.  
4. Statistical Rigor and Sensitivity  
   • Experiments are reported over a small number of seeds (5–10), without confidence intervals or significance testing. Moreover, the impact of key design choices—population size, mutation rates, hyperparameter search space bounds—remains unexamined. A sensitivity analysis would guide users in configuring SEARL for new tasks.  
5. Technical Detail Gaps  
   • As a general framework, SEARL requires more guidance on hyperparameter initialization (e.g., ranges for learning rates, discount factors) across different algorithms. The rationale for training individuals for “as many steps as frames generated during evaluation,” and for reducing training to a fraction j of those steps, needs clearer justification.  
6. Theoretical Insight  
   • While shared replay is shown empirically to improve sample reuse, the paper would benefit from a discussion—or even a sketch of analysis—explaining why this yields such gains under non-stationarity.

Actionable Suggestions  
• Extend experiments to at least one other off-policy baseline (e.g., Rainbow DQN) and consider a small on-policy case study.  
• Add comparisons to a gradient-based HPO method (metagradient, HOOF) or Bayesian optimizer with resource-aware early stopping.  
• Report wall-clock times, resource usage, and possibly an asynchronous variant to demonstrate practical speedups.  
• Increase the number of random seeds, include confidence intervals, and conduct basic statistical tests.  
• Perform sensitivity studies on population size, mutation rate, and search space definitions.  
• Provide detailed guidelines for setting SEARL’s algorithmic hyperparameters and clarify training/evaluation scheduling.  
• Discuss theoretical or intuitive reasons for the effectiveness of shared experience replay in non-stationary RL.

Overall Assessment  
SEARL introduces a coherent, well-motivated AutoRL framework with promising sample-efficiency improvements over simple baselines. However, the experimental validation is currently too narrow, and several comparisons and analyses are missing. I recommend a marginal accept (rating: 6), contingent on the authors addressing the evaluation breadth, baseline completeness, runtime reporting, and technical-detail clarifications outlined above.
reviews.original_text:
Review:
Summary：
In this paper, the authors intend to propose an efficient automated reinforcement learning (RL) framework. To achieve this goal, they integrate three technologies, i.e., evolutionary RL for hyperparameter search, evolvable neural network for policy network design, and shared experience replay for improving data usage. The paper uses a case study on MuJoCo to demonstrate the claimed advantages over baselines.

Some pros:
1. The motivation is good. The automation of reinforcement learning is beneficial to the research community, especially for researchers who are not familiar with RL but in need of it.
2. The framework is general and friendly to users. This framework is general and can be regarded as a plug-in module for a series of reinforcement learning methods. Besides, as we all know, both of the autoML and RL have a heavy computational burden, the adaption of evolvable neural network and shared experience replay greatly alleviate this dilemma. 
3. The organization of this paper is easy to follow. We can follow the authors from why they want to deal with the problem, to how they are inspired by existing work, and then to how the algorithms are design based on the questions to be answered and the existing technologies. 

Some cons:
1. The experiment is far from enough. Actually, this paper only has a case study. First, the author claims that the framework can optimize arbitrary off-policy RL algorithms, why only try on TD3? From the perspective of robustness, the authors need to compare more off-policy algorithms with and without the proposed method. Second, the paper claims there is no directly comparable approach for efficient AutoRL, which I do not agree with. In its own related work, many AutoRL baselines are listed, e.g., H. L. Chiang et al.  'Learning navigation behaviors end-to-end with autorl', F. Runge et al. 'Learning to design RNA'. For these baselines, they can either take the same exploration steps and compare performance with the proposed method, or compare the performance/computational cost when reaching the same performance. Anyway, the readers expect to see more comparisons with more baselines from more perspectives. Third, the authors claim that to tackle the non-stationarity of the RL problem, existing studies can substantially increase the number of environment interactions, implying the proposed framework has advantages on non-stationarity RL environments, but still, no experimental results are given.
2. Some technical details are missing. The logic is clear, the solution is reasonable, but the details are ignored. It is a good idea to keep the writing compact, but the lack of details may harm the readability of the paper. For example, since this is a general framework, how should we design hyperparameter settings of SEARL in initialization for different algorithms? In the training part, why should individual be trained for as many steps as frames have been generated in the evaluation phase, and why the training time could be reduced by using only a fraction j of the steps?

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold

reviews.confidence: 3: The reviewer is fairly confident that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
Summary: This paper propose a population-based AutoRL framework for hyperparameter optimization of off-policy RL algorithms. The framework optimizes both the hyperparameters and also the neural architecture in a one-shot manner, e.g., search and train at the same time. A shared experience replay buffer is used across the population, which as demonstrated in the experiments, substantially increase the sample efficiency compared to PBT and random search. 

Strengths:
- The idea is simple and intuitively makes sense. By sharing the experiences across the population each experience sample gets re-used more often during the training hence the increase in sample efficiency.

Weakness:
- Only apply to off-policy RL. 
- Random search is not a very compelling baseline. PBT is not optimized for sample efficiency. It seems that the shared replay buffer can also be applied to PBT? What about differentiable HPO methods? Those are often shown to be much more sample efficient compared to evolution based approaches. 
- There is little discussions on what the search results look like, e.g., the learning rate schedule or the neural architecture found by the search method. How are they compared to the SOTA learning rate schedule or neural architecture? Or maybe not even SOTA just compare to some simple heuristics that gradually decrease the learning rate or increase the network size. It would be of limited value if the use of AutoRL can only achieve marginally better performance compared to those simple heuristics.

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper introduces SEARL, a population-based AutoRL framework that jointly optimizes hyperparameters and neural architectures of off-policy agents in a one-shot, online fashion. Its key innovation is the use of a shared experience replay buffer across the population, which—as demonstrated on TD3 in MuJoCo and Rainbow DQN on Atari—yields up to a 5–10× reduction in environment interactions compared to random search and a modified Population-Based Training (PBT). The authors provide clear pseudocode, open-source code, and thorough ablation studies that isolate the impact of shared replay, architecture mutations, and dynamic hyperparameter scheduling.

Strengths  
- The shared replay buffer is a simple yet powerful idea. By pooling experiences across evolving agents, the method significantly improves sample reuse and meta-optimization efficiency.  
- The evaluation protocol is well designed: total interaction counts are reported, and ablations convincingly quantify each component’s contribution.  
- Demonstrated gains across two distinct domains (continuous control and Atari) show broad promise.  
- Open-source release and detailed pseudocode ensure reproducibility.  
- The paper addresses a pressing challenge in AutoRL—online adaptation to non-stationarity—through an elegant combination of evolutionary mutations, Lamarckian weight inheritance, and dynamic scheduling.

Weaknesses  
1. Limited Baselines for Sample-Efficient HPO  
   While comparisons to random search and PBT highlight the benefit of shared replay, established gradient-based or Bayesian HPO methods (e.g., metagradient tuning, HOOF, Bayesian optimization with early stopping) are conspicuously absent. These approaches often achieve higher sample efficiency than evolutionary strategies, and their inclusion is critical to contextualize SEARL’s empirical gains.  
2. Narrow Applicability and Novelty Concerns  
   SEARL is restricted to off-policy algorithms, and the discussion of extending to on-policy methods (e.g., PPO or SAC) remains cursory. Moreover, although combining PBT, evolutionary RL, and shared buffers yields practical improvements, the conceptual novelty over prior work such as ERL or metagradient HPO may be perceived as incremental without deeper theoretical insight.  
3. Insufficient Analysis of Search Outcomes  
   The manuscript provides little detail on the hyperparameter schedules and architectures discovered by SEARL. How do these evolve over time? Do they resemble known heuristics (e.g., monotonically decaying learning rates, progressive network widening)? Deeper analysis or comparison to simple hand-crafted schedules would clarify whether AutoRL yields qualitatively different solutions.  
4. Omitted Runtime and Resource Metrics  
   Sample efficiency gains do not automatically translate into wall-clock speedups. The synchronous evolutionary loop could incur nontrivial overheads, yet GPU/CPU usage and actual time-to-performance are not reported. Without this data, it is difficult to assess practical scalability or to compare SEARL’s real-world utility against asynchronous or gradient-based methods.  
5. Statistical Rigor and Scope of Evaluation  
   Experiments rely on a limited number of random seeds (5–10), and confidence intervals on final returns are narrow. More extensive statistical testing would strengthen claims. Furthermore, evaluation is confined to simulated benchmarks; demonstration on a real-world or higher-dimensional control task would bolster the framework’s generality.  

Suggestions for Improvement  
• Incorporate one or more sample-efficient HPO baselines—such as Bayesian optimization with early stopping, metagradient methods, or bandit-based tuning—to better contextualize gains over random/PBT.  
• Report wall-clock training times, compute resource usage, and parallel efficiency to validate practical speedups.  
• Provide a detailed analysis of the hyperparameter schedules and neural architectures discovered during search, including comparisons to simple heuristics.  
• Extend evaluation to an on-policy algorithm or a real-robot environment to demonstrate broader applicability.  
• Increase the number of random seeds and include statistical significance tests on final performance.  

Decision  
While SEARL offers an elegant integration of shared experience replay, evolutionary adaptation, and dynamic scheduling—with compelling empirical benefits—the limited baseline comparisons, lack of runtime analysis, and narrow applicability constrain its impact. I recommend marginal rejection.  

Rating: 5 (Marginally below acceptance threshold)

reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
# Summary

The authors in this paper propose to optimize the hyperparameters and also the neural architecture while simultaneously training the agent. They evaluate the proposed method with TD3 in the MuJoCo benchmark suite. 

Overall, the proposed method is well-motivated and well-written, and they provide enough experiment/implementation details to reproduce the results. More importantly, they provide the source code. 

# Strength

- Tuning architecture and hyperparameters with PTB for off-policy RL has not been studied before. Compared to baselines, the proposed method is much more sample-efficient.
- The evaluation on MuJoCo is comprehensive, especially the ablation study in Section 4.

# Weakness

- They only test on a single benchmark with one method, TD3. MuJoCo is arguably simple. Actually, the visual world in MuJoCo is quite limited, so the encoder of the RL agent does not have to be huge. It could be more convincing if the authors can test on another benchmark, e.g. ProcGen. 
- I think compared to computer vision tasks with huge neural networks, the search space for the architecture of RL models is much smaller, which can be observed in the ablation study. Without architecture adaption, there is no observable difference. It seems that combining the tuning of architecture and hyperparameters is not that useful.

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents SEARL, a population-based framework that jointly evolves neural architectures and algorithm hyperparameters of off-policy deep RL agents during training. By applying tournament-style evolutionary mutations and dynamic hyperparameter scheduling across a population of TD3 agents, and critically sharing a single replay buffer to maximize sample reuse, the authors demonstrate substantial improvements in meta-optimization sample efficiency on standard MuJoCo continuous-control benchmarks. The manuscript is generally well written, provides clear pseudocode and experiment details, and the authors have released their implementation to facilitate reproducibility.  

Strengths  
- Novel joint AutoRL formulation: SEARL integrates neural-architecture search (NAS) with hyperparameter optimization (HPO) in an online, population-based loop, addressing RL’s non-stationarity and sensitivity to hyperparameters.  
- Shared replay buffer: Borrowing from evolutionary RL, the shared experience pool is a simple yet effective mechanism that yields up to an order-of-magnitude fewer environment interactions for meta-optimization compared to random search or a PBT-style baseline.  
- Clear evaluation protocol: Experiments report total environment steps, include ablations disentangling the contributions of shared replay, architecture adaptation, and hyperparameter tuning, and track robustness across random seeds.  
- Reproducibility: Detailed implementation notes, pseudocode, and open-source code lower the barrier for follow-up work.  

Weaknesses and Suggestions  
1. Limited evaluation scope  
   • The experiments focus solely on TD3 in MuJoCo, which—while a standard testbed—does not fully stress richer visual encoders or diversified task distributions. As noted by several reviewers, MuJoCo’s relatively low-dimensional observations may understate the impact of neural-architecture adaptation. Extending evaluations to other benchmarks (e.g., ProcGen or Atari with Rainbow DQN) or at least one on-policy algorithm (e.g., SAC or PPO) would better demonstrate generality.  
   • Beyond simulated control, a small case study on a more challenging environment (e.g., a real-robot task or high-dimensional continuous control) would strengthen claims of practical applicability.  

2. Comparison to stronger baselines  
   • The current baselines are limited to random search and a modified population-based training. To contextualize SEARL’s gains, comparison to recent AutoRL or HPO methods—such as gradient-based metagradient tuning, Bayesian optimization with early stopping, bandit-based methods (HOOF), or Runge et al. (2019)—is necessary.  
   • Reporting performance versus off-policy gradient HPO baselines or recent meta-optimization approaches would solidify SEARL’s relative advantages.  

3. Novelty and theoretical insight  
   • While the combination of evolution, dynamic schedules, and shared replay is well motivated, key contributions over related work in evolutionary RL (ERL) or PBT appear incremental. More discussion or theoretical analysis of why shared replay yields such significant gains would enhance the paper’s conceptual contribution.  
   • A brief theoretical argument or empirical ablation isolating the conditions under which replay sharing is most beneficial (e.g., tasks with sparse rewards or short episodes) would provide deeper insight.  

4. Scalability and practical overhead  
   • SEARL employs a synchronous evolutionary loop; however, wall-clock runtime, compute requirements (GPU/CPU hours), and parallel efficiency are not reported. Without these details, it is unclear whether sample-efficiency gains translate into real-world speedups. Including a runtime analysis and resource usage comparison to baselines is recommended.  
   • Sensitivity to population size, mutation rates, and hyperparameter search‐space design is not fully explored. A brief sensitivity study would guide practitioners and clarify the robustness of SEARL’s performance.  

5. Statistical rigor  
   • The empirical results use a modest number of seeds (5–10); reporting confidence intervals and performing statistical tests on final performance would strengthen the validity of claimed improvements.  

Decision  
Rating: 5: Marginally below acceptance threshold  

SEARL tackles an important challenge in AutoRL with a technically sound and reproducible approach, achieving impressive sample-efficiency gains on MuJoCo. However, the limited evaluation scope, absence of stronger baselines, and lack of runtime analysis prevent a full assessment of its novelty and practical impact. With broader experimental validation, more rigorous comparisons, and additional insight into the underlying mechanisms, this work would be significantly strengthened. At present, it stands just below the bar for acceptance.
