title: Contemplating Real-World Object Classification

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
Real-world applications frequently provide challenges that are not seen in common computer vision datasets like Imagenet, where images are blurry, dark, corrupted, objects are highly occluded, test objects may be out of distribution due to natural distribution shifts, etc.  This phenomenon was investigated in 2018 by Beery et al, who similarly found categorization is easier with localization in challenging real-world scenes (ie classifying cropped boxes). I would recommend taking a look at that paper (citation below) and including it in the related work.

Beery, S., Van Horn, G., & Perona, P. (2018). Recognition in terra incognita. In Proceedings of the European Conference on Computer Vision (ECCV) (pp. 456-473).


Pros:

The authors provide many experiments digging into various aspects of what makes real-world object detection challenging. This is a useful reference point for future work.

Cons:
This paper re-analyzes generalization in the context of an existing dataset. There is not anything particularly novel about the analysis, and similar results have been shown on other real-world datasets.
The authors describe the image distortions they consider to be “natural”, but are applying them synthetically. It is not clear to me that applying a synthetic distortion of a type that can be seen in the real world is necessarily reflective of those realistic distortions in the wild.  It would be better to explicitly collect examples of these types of distortions in real data and compare against that.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper revisits the ObjectNet benchmark (Barbu et al.) by manually annotating 18,574 images across 113 overlapping ImageNet classes with bounding boxes and segmentation masks. The authors re-evaluate six popular ImageNet‐trained CNNs under full‐image, bounding‐box, and mask‐only inputs, reporting consistent 10–15% top-1 accuracy gains when models see only the object region. They further test robustness to 14 natural corruptions (noise, blur, etc.), geometric transformations, and white-box adversarial attacks (FGSM, PGD-5), finding that isolating the foreground improves both standard accuracy and robustness. A qualitative analysis compares human vs. model difficulty on challenging instances. Code and annotations are released for reproducibility.

Pros  
1. Comprehensive empirical study: The paper provides a clear, reproducible pipeline for annotating ObjectNet and rerunning experiments on six architectures, 14 corruption types, adversarial attacks, and geometric shifts.  
2. Segmentation insights: Extending the crop‐only analysis with COCO segmentation masks demonstrates further robustness gains with minimal drop in clean accuracy.  
3. Qualitative human–machine alignment: The authors highlight annotation challenges and show that isolating objects reduces the gap between machine errors and human‐judged difficulty.  
4. Actionable resource: All annotations and code are released, offering a valuable benchmark for future robustness and context‐ablation studies.

Cons  
1. Incremental novelty: The core idea—cropping to the object region improves classification and robustness—has precedent (e.g., Beery et al. 2018’s “Recognition in terra incognita”). The related work should discuss these prior findings to position the contribution.  
2. Synthetic vs. real distortions: While the paper labels its corruptions as “natural,” all distortions are synthetically applied. It remains unclear if these fully capture real‐world degradations; collecting actual distorted images would strengthen the ecological validity.  
3. Statistical rigor: Results lack confidence intervals or significance tests (e.g., paired t-tests, bootstrapped CIs). Without error bars or hypothesis testing, it is difficult to judge whether the reported gains are robust across runs or categories.  
4. Human performance baseline: Human accuracy is quoted from Barbu et al. rather than measured directly under the same experimental conditions (cropped vs. full images, time‐limited classification). A controlled psychophysics study would provide a solid baseline.  
5. Scalability and deployment: The manual annotations cover only 113 classes, and segmentation experiments rely on COCO masks, leaving open questions about generalization to the full 313‐class ObjectNet. Evaluating off-the-shelf detectors or segmenters would demonstrate real-world applicability.  
6. Minor formatting issues: There are broken footnotes, inconsistent figure labels, and variable image scales that should be cleaned up.

Suggestions for Improvement  
• Related Work: Add a discussion of Beery et al. (2018) and other localization‐based robustness studies to clearly delineate novelty.  
• Statistical Analysis: Include confidence intervals or perform significance testing for top-1 gains and robustness metrics.  
• Human Study: Conduct a small‐scale experiment measuring human accuracy on cropped vs. full ObjectNet images under matched conditions.  
• Real Data Collection: Supplement synthetic corruptions with a curated set of naturally distorted images (e.g., motion blur, poor lighting) to validate the “natural” distortion claim.  
• Automated Pipelines: Evaluate automatic bounding‐box/segmentation models on ObjectNet to assess how well the proposed cropping strategy scales in deployment.  
• Segmentation Scope: Extend mask‐based experiments beyond the COCO subset—either by expanding manual masks or fine-tuning a segmenter on more ObjectNet classes.  
• Formatting: Fix footnotes, unify figure/table captions, and ensure consistent reference formatting.

Rating  
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold  

Despite the incremental nature of the contribution, the paper’s thorough empirical evaluation, public release of data and code, and actionable insights into the role of context make it a valuable reference. Addressing statistical rigor, human baselines, and scalability concerns will further strengthen the work.

Beery, S., Van Horn, G., & Perona, P. (2018). Recognition in terra incognita. In Proceedings of the European Conference on Computer Vision (ECCV) (pp. 456-473).

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This paper revisits ObjectNet dataset closely and found applying classifiers on object bounding box significantly reduces the gap between ImageNet and ObjectNet. The authors further investigates the robustness of CNNs against image perturbations and adversarial attacks, and found limiting the object area to their segmentation mask significantly improves model accuracy (and robustness). Qualitative evaluation is also performed over confident and less-confident / incorrect model predictions and find it correlates with human perception.

Pros:
- More analyses like this paper does would help bridge the gap between ML/CV model performance in staged datasets and real-world scenarios
- The experimentation conducted in this paper is comprehensive, accompanied with many in-depth inspection over a large dataset. The insights drawn from this paper would be invaluable for researchers working in this field.

Cons:
- My major concern with this paper (and the main factor of rating it clear rejection) is the experimental setup used in section 3.3. From authors they "selected images from ten classes of the ObjectNet dataset ... manually annotated the object of interest in each image". Then "Models were trained on 70 images per category".  (also from Figure 39 "In total we annotated 1K images across ten categories of the ObjectNet dataset."). If interpreted correctly, the models are trained on part of ObjectNet images which clearly violates dataset license "ObjectNet may never be used to tune the parameters of any model." (https://objectnet.dev/download.html). 

- While appreciating the authors conducting study on model robustness, the conclusion drawn from several experiments seems to confuse "performance" v.s. "robustness", where the former indicates model have better accuracy, and the latter measures how model accuracy varies with increasing noise / perturbations. See more details below.

Some (minor) comments:

- Sec 3.1. 1) I share the concern from authors that "object detector" should be not confused with "object recognition" (or commonly used "image classifier"). Hopefully vision community could use more consistent terms across literatures.

- Sec 3.1 1) While detection dataset would surely have more scale variation (and truncation / occlusion due to many objects are not in the center), it is not entirely clear that object in detection datasets "vary more in parameters such as lighting, ..., and blur".

- Sec 3.1 1) It would be great to see more analysis on detection datasets (authors mentioned they will discuss in section 4, but only with very little analysis).

- Sec 3.1, 2) While ImageNet and ObjectNet have distinct characteristics, having some stats on object size / spatial placement might better illustrate the gaps between these datasets.

- Sec 3.1 3) Agree top-5 might make classifiers' life easy, but it is more of an eval metric rather than training loss (the model still need to predict top-1 class correct during training). Meanwhile, it is not very clear why multi-label annotation would bias against model that "is spatially more precise but misses some objects". That model should be evaluated against detection benchmarks, rather than object recognition (image classification) datasets.

- Sec 3.1 "bounding box annotation". For multiple objects nearby with the same category, the annotation would include all of them in one bounding box. This might leads to bad aspect ratio? (in general, bounding box would also vary more in aspect ratio than images, and feeding bounding box into a square CNN seems to be less ideal)

- Sec 3.1 "object recognition results". "AlexNet, VGG-19, and GoogLeNet have also been used", GoogLeNet should be "ResNet-152"? ObjectNet uses inception (GoogLeNet) v4 while authors use v3.

- Sec 3.2.1 "The higher the prediction accuracy, the higher the robustness against natural distortions". This is not necessarily true. Looking at Figure 3, it seems all models and both image / bounding box schemes would have decayed performance w.r.t distortion severity, and their slopes seem similar.

- Sec 3.3 "Despite a large body of literature on whether and how much visual context benefits CNNs, the majority being focused on model accuracy, the matter has not been settled yet". "the matter" means context for robustness? please clarify.

- Sec 3.3 " Around 35.5% and 12.6% of the image pixels fall inside the object bounding box and the foreground object, respectively. Around 58.5% of the bounding box pixels fall inside the object mask.". here foreground object and object mask should be the same thing? if so, the numbers seem not matching (would expect box-to-image ratio * mask-to-box-ratio = mask-to-image ratio, or these numbers are normalized differently)?

- Figure 5: it is clear that seg-mask actually isn't robust to adversarial attacks (accuracy dropped significantly), which contradicts with the claim from authors.

- Sec 3.3 "An attempt was made to tune the parameters to attain the best test accuracy in each case", could authors elaborate? 

- Sec 3.3.1. here the results seem to indicate segmask is more robust (less variations). Would the segmentation mask itself be indicative for object categories? It might be interesting to predict object classes directly from those masks as a baseline.


Final review:

The authors updated the manuscript and removed tuning experiment on ObjectNet. I am still a bit concerned about the definition of "robustness", but the paper overall does look good for ICLR publication.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This paper presents a thorough reanalysis of the ObjectNet benchmark by isolating objects via manually drawn bounding boxes and segmentation masks, and reevaluating six popular ImageNet‐trained CNNs on both classification accuracy and robustness measures. The authors annotate over 18K ObjectNet images across 113 overlapping classes, showing consistent 10–15% top‐1 accuracy gains when models are applied only to the object region. They further evaluate robustness under 14 natural corruptions, geometric transformations, and white‐box adversarial attacks (FGSM, PGD‐5), finding that segmentation‐mask inputs yield further resilience with minimal standard‐accuracy drop. A qualitative human–machine difficulty alignment study completes the analysis.

Strengths  
1. Comprehensive empirical scope: The paper evaluates six architectures, across hundreds of classes, multiple perturbation types, and both bounding‐box and segmentation‐mask schemes. This breadth of experimentation provides valuable insights into context’s role in modern CNN performance.  
2. Reproducible annotations and code: Manual bounding boxes on 18K ObjectNet images (and COCO‐based segmentation masks) fill a critical gap in the benchmark’s evaluation protocol and are shared publicly.  
3. Actionable robustness findings: The consistent accuracy and robustness gains when isolating the object region underscore a practical defense strategy—foreground extraction—that the community can adopt.  
4. Qualitative analysis: Correlating confident/erroneous model predictions with human judgments highlights annotation challenges and aligns machine difficulty with human perception.

Weaknesses  
1. Dataset‐license issue: The training setup in Section 3.3, which uses 70 ObjectNet images per category (1K images total), appears to violate ObjectNet’s license that prohibits tuning model parameters on its images. This raises concerns about the validity of fine‐tuning experiments and calls for clarification or alternative protocols.  
2. Incremental novelty: While the reanalysis is thorough, it does not propose new modeling techniques. The contribution primarily refines evaluation on an existing dataset rather than introduces algorithmic advances.  
3. Scalability of manual annotations: Relying on manually drawn masks and boxes limits applicability to the full 313‐class ObjectNet; there is no evaluation of off‐the‐shelf detectors or segmenters to automate this process.  
4. Robustness versus performance conflation: Some conclusions blur the distinction between higher baseline accuracy and genuine robustness to distortions. Figure 3 suggests similar degradation slopes across models and input schemes, so claims that segmentation inherently improves robustness need more statistical rigor (e.g., confidence intervals or paired significance tests).  
5. Missing human baselines: Human performance on isolated‐object crops is only quoted from prior work; the paper would benefit from a small psychophysics study matching the cropped‐image conditions to ground model gaps.  
6. Minor inconsistencies and clarifications:  
   – Terminology around “object detector” vs. “image classifier” could be standardized.  
   – Statistical details in Section 3.3 (parameter tuning, test‐time augmentations) are underspecified.  
   – Numbers on mask‐to‐box and box‐to‐image ratios appear mismatched and should be reconciled.  
   – Model naming (GoogLeNet v3 vs. inception‐v4) and figure labels need consistency fixes.

Suggestions for Improvement  
• Clarify the use of ObjectNet images in training and ensure compliance with licensing—alternatively, report results using only ImageNet‐trained weights.  
• Incorporate statistical significance testing (bootstrapped confidence intervals or paired t‐tests) for robustness metrics to support claims of improved resilience.  
• Conduct a small‐scale human study on cropped ObjectNet samples to benchmark human accuracy on isolated objects.  
• Evaluate automated object‐detection or segmentation pipelines (e.g., off‐the‐shelf detectors) to assess real‐world applicability and scalability beyond manual annotation.  
• Provide more dataset statistics—object size distributions, spatial placement, aspect‐ratio variation—to better illustrate differences between ImageNet, ObjectNet, and detection datasets.  
• Clean up minor formatting and terminology inconsistencies to improve clarity and reproducibility.

Decision and Rating  
I endorse the main review’s recommendation. Despite some concerns around licensing, novelty, and statistical rigor, this paper offers a rigorously executed empirical investigation with actionable insights into the role of context and foreground isolation for both accuracy and robustness. I recommend acceptance with minor revisions.  
Rating: 6 – Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.original_text:
Review:
This paper revisits ObjectNet dataset closely and found applying classifiers on object bounding box significantly reduces the gap between ImageNet and ObjectNet. The authors further investigates the robustness of CNNs against image perturbations and adversarial attacks, and found limiting the object area to their segmentation mask significantly improves model accuracy (and robustness). Qualitative evaluation is also performed over confident and less-confident / incorrect model predictions and find it correlates with human perception.

Pros:
- More analyses like this paper does would help bridge the gap between ML/CV model performance in staged datasets and real-world scenarios
- The experimentation conducted in this paper is comprehensive, accompanied with many in-depth inspection over a large dataset. The insights drawn from this paper would be invaluable for researchers working in this field.

Cons:
- My major concern with this paper (and the main factor of rating it clear rejection) is the experimental setup used in section 3.3. From authors they "selected images from ten classes of the ObjectNet dataset ... manually annotated the object of interest in each image". Then "Models were trained on 70 images per category".  (also from Figure 39 "In total we annotated 1K images across ten categories of the ObjectNet dataset."). If interpreted correctly, the models are trained on part of ObjectNet images which clearly violates dataset license "ObjectNet may never be used to tune the parameters of any model." (https://objectnet.dev/download.html). 

- While appreciating the authors conducting study on model robustness, the conclusion drawn from several experiments seems to confuse "performance" v.s. "robustness", where the former indicates model have better accuracy, and the latter measures how model accuracy varies with increasing noise / perturbations. See more details below.

Some (minor) comments:

- Sec 3.1. 1) I share the concern from authors that "object detector" should be not confused with "object recognition" (or commonly used "image classifier"). Hopefully vision community could use more consistent terms across literatures.

- Sec 3.1 1) While detection dataset would surely have more scale variation (and truncation / occlusion due to many objects are not in the center), it is not entirely clear that object in detection datasets "vary more in parameters such as lighting, ..., and blur".

- Sec 3.1 1) It would be great to see more analysis on detection datasets (authors mentioned they will discuss in section 4, but only with very little analysis).

- Sec 3.1, 2) While ImageNet and ObjectNet have distinct characteristics, having some stats on object size / spatial placement might better illustrate the gaps between these datasets.

- Sec 3.1 3) Agree top-5 might make classifiers' life easy, but it is more of an eval metric rather than training loss (the model still need to predict top-1 class correct during training). Meanwhile, it is not very clear why multi-label annotation would bias against model that "is spatially more precise but misses some objects". That model should be evaluated against detection benchmarks, rather than object recognition (image classification) datasets.

- Sec 3.1 "bounding box annotation". For multiple objects nearby with the same category, the annotation would include all of them in one bounding box. This might leads to bad aspect ratio? (in general, bounding box would also vary more in aspect ratio than images, and feeding bounding box into a square CNN seems to be less ideal)

- Sec 3.1 "object recognition results". "AlexNet, VGG-19, and GoogLeNet have also been used", GoogLeNet should be "ResNet-152"? ObjectNet uses inception (GoogLeNet) v4 while authors use v3.

- Sec 3.2.1 "The higher the prediction accuracy, the higher the robustness against natural distortions". This is not necessarily true. Looking at Figure 3, it seems all models and both image / bounding box schemes would have decayed performance w.r.t distortion severity, and their slopes seem similar.

- Sec 3.3 "Despite a large body of literature on whether and how much visual context benefits CNNs, the majority being focused on model accuracy, the matter has not been settled yet". "the matter" means context for robustness? please clarify.

- Sec 3.3 " Around 35.5% and 12.6% of the image pixels fall inside the object bounding box and the foreground object, respectively. Around 58.5% of the bounding box pixels fall inside the object mask.". here foreground object and object mask should be the same thing? if so, the numbers seem not matching (would expect box-to-image ratio * mask-to-box-ratio = mask-to-image ratio, or these numbers are normalized differently)?

- Figure 5: it is clear that seg-mask actually isn't robust to adversarial attacks (accuracy dropped significantly), which contradicts with the claim from authors.

- Sec 3.3 "An attempt was made to tune the parameters to attain the best test accuracy in each case", could authors elaborate? 

- Sec 3.3.1. here the results seem to indicate segmask is more robust (less variations). Would the segmentation mask itself be indicative for object categories? It might be interesting to predict object classes directly from those masks as a baseline.


Final review:

The authors updated the manuscript and removed tuning experiment on ObjectNet. I am still a bit concerned about the definition of "robustness", but the paper overall does look good for ICLR publication.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
I like the main ideas articulated in the paper, but find the writing lacks some clarity: 

Summary of paper: The paper  takes as a starting point the study from Barbu et al where the robustness of object recognition pipelines to be able to handle distribution shifts are studied by testing ImageNet trained architectures against ObjectNet.  The main point in the current paper is that the performance degradation seen in Barbu et al is due to the fact that the CNNs were processing the image with entire image as context  and when one only provides a sub-window around the objects of interest the resulting performance improves significantly.  The paper also describes experiments with various synthetic distorted data and finally examines details of ObjectNet dataset to illustrate that there are images that are hard to categorize even for humans. Thus, the paper concludes that object recognition on ObjectNet is still hard to solve.

It is clear that by using bounding boxes or even removing background from those bounding boxes,  the performance will be better (since the training was on ImageNet with single objects). So, in a way, they are kind-of recreating the training distribution in order to improve the performance.

Main significance of the paper (Pros): Detailed study of performance of object recognition and the empirical finding that figure-ground segmentation may improve recognition.  Analysis of properties of ObjectNet and its challenges.

Originality/Novelty:  The paper is largely empirical and has a good discussion of the relevant background literature analyzing object recognition systems.  

Cons:  It has incremental insights. 

Clarity of paper: The description of the experiments are at times unclear.    The structure of the paper could be simplified with a table or diagram that illustrates the logic behind the experimentation and conclusion.  There are multiple datasets used, the training scheme is sometimes on ImageNet and tested on ObjectNet and sometimes on selected categories of ObjectNet and test on the rest of ObjectNet.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This paper revisits the robustness gap identified by Barbu et al. on the ObjectNet benchmark, demonstrating that isolating objects via manually drawn bounding boxes—and further via segmentation masks—partially recovers the large accuracy drop observed when ImageNet‐trained CNNs are tested out‐of‐distribution. The authors annotate 18,574 ObjectNet images across 113 overlapping ImageNet classes, evaluate six popular architectures, and report consistent 10–15% top‐1 accuracy gains when models see only the cropped object region. They also extend the analysis to natural corruptions (14 types), geometric transformations, and white‐box adversarial attacks (FGSM, PGD‐5), showing that foreground extraction improves both classification accuracy and robustness. Finally, a qualitative human–machine difficulty study highlights annotation challenges and persistent “hard” instances even after cropping.

Strengths  
1. Comprehensive Empirical Study: The paper systematically evaluates multiple model families (e.g., ResNet, DenseNet, Vision Transformers) under a wide range of distribution shifts—natural corruptions, geometric distortions, and adversarial attacks—clearly demonstrating that isolating the object boosts performance across the board.  
2. Large‐Scale Annotation Effort: By manually annotating bounding boxes on over 18K ObjectNet images in 113 classes, the authors fill a critical gap in the benchmark’s evaluation protocol and provide reusable code and data for reproducibility.  
3. Segmentation‐Mask Experiments: The extension to segmentation masks—using COCO masks on overlapping categories—further substantiates that foreground subtraction can yield additional robustness benefits, with minimal drop on standard ImageNet accuracy.  
4. Qualitative Human–Machine Analysis: The paper highlights real‐world annotation difficulties and aligns challenging instances for humans and machines, underscoring the enduring hardness of ObjectNet even when context is removed.  
5. Actionable Insights: The finding that figure–ground segmentation meaningfully narrows the robustness gap suggests a promising direction for integrating segmentation into real‐world recognition pipelines.

Weaknesses  
1. Incremental Novelty: The work largely refines an existing dataset rather than introducing new modeling or algorithmic contributions. Although the empirical insights are valuable, the paper stops short of proposing end‐to‐end methods or novel defenses.  
2. Lack of Human Baseline on Crops: While the paper quotes human performance from Barbu et al., it does not include a controlled psychophysical study comparing human accuracy on cropped versus full scenes. This missing baseline makes it difficult to precisely locate the human–machine gap in the isolated‐object setting.  
3. Statistical Rigor: The reported gains would be more convincing with confidence intervals or paired significance tests (e.g., bootstrapped CIs or paired t‐tests) to quantify variability across runs and categories.  
4. Scalability of Manual Annotations: The current bounding‐box and mask annotations cover only 113 classes, leaving out the majority of ObjectNet’s categories. There is no evaluation of automated detection or segmentation pipelines, which limits the work’s applicability to large‐scale or real‐world settings.  
5. Inconsistent Protocols and Presentation: The experimental protocol—sometimes training on full ImageNet and testing on ObjectNet, other times training on selected crops—could be presented more clearly. The paper would benefit from a unifying diagram or table that outlines the dataset splits, training schemes, and evaluation settings. Minor formatting issues (e.g., misplaced footnotes, inconsistent figure labels) also detract from readability.

Actionable Suggestions  
1. Include Human Psychophysics: Conduct a small‐scale study measuring human accuracy on cropped ObjectNet images under rapid presentation, to establish a proper baseline for isolated‐object recognition.  
2. Report Statistical Significance: Provide confidence intervals or perform paired tests on the reported accuracy and robustness metrics to validate that the observed improvements are reliable.  
3. Explore Automated Segmentation: Evaluate off‐the‐shelf object detectors or segmentation models on ObjectNet to assess how much of the bounding‐box benefit persists when using imperfect masks.  
4. Investigate End‐to‐End Training: Fine‐tune or train models from scratch on cropped or segmented ObjectNet images to examine whether context‐restricted learning yields more generalizable feature representations.  
5. Clarify Experimental Design: Add a summary table or flowchart that clearly annotates the training and testing configurations, datasets used (ImageNet vs. ObjectNet vs. COCO), and perturbation settings. Clean up minor formatting inconsistencies to improve readability.  
6. Consider Multi‐Label and Detection Scenarios: Annotate multi‐object presence or evaluate a joint detection‐classification pipeline to better reflect real‐world complexity and context effects.

Overall Assessment  
This paper offers a detailed and reproducible empirical reanalysis of the ObjectNet benchmark, yielding valuable insights into the role of context and the potential of segmentation for improving robustness. While the contributions are largely incremental and certain methodological aspects could be strengthened (human baselines, statistical rigor, automation), the work nonetheless advances our understanding of distribution‐shift challenges in object recognition. I recommend marginal acceptance (Rating: 6), as the paper sits just above the threshold given its thoroughness, actionability, and relevance to the community.

6: Marginally above acceptance threshold

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
OVERVIEW:
The authors present a follow-up to the prior work of Barbu et al on the task of Object Recognition* (name confusion addressed in cons below). Barbu et al demonstrated that on a more realistic dataset like ObjectNet, models trained on a clean dataset like ImageNet suffer significant degradation. This work reduces the performance gap by cropping out the object using bounding box or mask information and running the recognition model on top of it. They do this for a variety of models (AlexNet, VGG-19, ResNet-152, Inception-v4, NASNet-A, PNASNet-5L) and transformations (image distortions, adversarial perturbations, context, geometric transformations). 

PROS:
- The paper is well-written and tackles an important topic of object recognition* in the wild. It tries to move away from the ImageNet driven approach that is currently present in the community to a more realistic scenario. 
- They build on the prior work of Barbu et al and are able to reduce performance gap demonstrated by Barbu et al by using bounding box or mask cropped images of the object of interest.
- They present a lot of experimental evaluation using a variety of models and transformations and demonstrate that their findings hold across all settings.

CONS:
- I agree with the authors that ImageNet with a single (or few) object present in the center of the image with clear foreground-background separation is unrealistic. ObjectNet is a better snapshot of the real world and models trained on ImageNet suffer in ObjectNet. However, the proposed approach to crop out the object using bounding box information or mask information is moving the data distribution from the real world setting of ObjectNet closer to the ideal setting of ImageNet. This then leads to an expected improvement in performance. I appreciate the thoroughness of the results and evaluation presented but it does not feel like a novel contribution in my opinion. 
- The authors present 4 future research directions in Section 4. I would be more willing to accept the paper if one of these research directions is incorporated as a contribution. For example, the last research direction of applying an object detection model trained with MS COCO on ObjectNet images instead of an image classification model trained with ImageNet is something that is doable. I would encourage the authors to even consider an object detection model trained on LVIS which has a larger number of object categories. This moves further away from object recognition* to object detection + classification but the latter is what we would typically encounter in a real-world scenario. Even here, I would need some contribution or novel analysis besides re-running current experiments with detection models.
- I strongly recommend a change of name to "Contemplating Real-World Object Classification" (no caps and classification instead of recognition). In my understanding, object recognition is a super-set of classification, detection, segmentation, etc. ImageNet leads to Image Classification models even if they are technically object classification. But sticking with the terminology of Object Recognition because it was used by Barbu et al is misleading. I would prefer Object Classification be used because that is the task of interest in this work.

REASON FOR RATING:
I think there is an interesting problem of real-world object classification that is of significant importance and this work moves a little closer to analyzing possible ways to reduce the performance gap from ImageNet to ObjectNet. However, their key contribution is somewhat expected (not novel) and needs some more work before being conference-paper ready.

UPDATE:
I have read the author feedback and the other reviews/discussions. I keep my original rating of 5. I think multiple authors raised the question of novelty relative to Barbu et al and the authors argue that they demonstrate the importance of context (whole image vs bounding box vs instance mask) for object recognition. Section 3.3 and Figure 5 is helpful in demonstrating it. However, the experimental setup is very limited (700 train + 300 test). COCO has 110K train and 5K val images and many more objects. If you argue that only 10 categories are common between COCO and ObjectNet, how many are common between LVIS and ObjectNet? I would strongly encourage the authors to leverage these pre-trained models and sharpen their message & contributions. I think they provide empirical justifications (important to the community) for expected results in moving from image to bounding box (same comment from multiple reviewers) but they need to de-emphasize that aspect and emphasize their results on context and robustness. A revision and resubmission to a different conference is encouraged.

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
OVERVIEW  
This paper provides a detailed re‐analysis of the ObjectNet benchmark by manually annotating 18.6K images across the 113 ImageNet‐overlap classes with bounding boxes and, for a subset, segmentation masks. The authors evaluate six popular CNN architectures (AlexNet, VGG‐19, ResNet‐152, Inception‐v4, NASNet‐A, PNASNet‐5L) on full images versus object‐cropped inputs under natural corruptions, geometric transformations, and white‐box adversarial attacks (FGSM, PGD‐5). They report consistent 10–30% top‐1 accuracy gains when models see only the object region, with further robustness improvements using segmentation masks. A qualitative human–machine difficulty analysis is also included, and all annotation code and data are released for reproducibility.

STRENGTHS  
1. Comprehensive empirical evaluation.  The study spans multiple model families, 14 corruption types, adversarial attacks, and geometric shifts, demonstrating that isolating the object boosts both accuracy and robustness.  
2. Reproducible dataset enrichment.  Manually drawn bounding boxes on ObjectNet fill a critical evaluation gap, and the segmentation‐mask extension on COCO classes further underscores the potential of foreground extraction.  
3. Qualitative insights.  The human–machine difficulty alignment offers useful qualitative evidence about which instances remain challenging even after foreground isolation.  
4. Clear action items for the community.  The paper convincingly argues that cropping or mask‐based preprocessing can serve as a practical defense against context‐induced failures.

WEAKNESSES  
1. Incremental novelty relative to prior work.  While the empirical scope is broad, the core idea—that cropping to the object yields better performance—is anticipated. The contribution refines rather than fundamentally advances the methodology introduced by Barbu et al.  
2. Reliance on perfect manual annotations.  The manual bounding boxes and ideal segmentation masks limit scalability and real‐world deployment. The absence of experiments with off‐the‐shelf object detectors or automated segmenters leaves open whether these robustness gains translate when masks are noisy.  
3. Limited generalization beyond 113 classes.  By focusing only on manually annotated overlap classes, it remains unclear if the findings hold across the full 313–class ObjectNet or in multi‐label scenarios common in natural images.  
4. Lack of statistical rigor.  The paper omits confidence intervals, hypothesis tests, and per‐category significance analyses, making it hard to assess the robustness of reported gains.  
5. Missing human baseline on cropped inputs.  The authors cite prior human accuracy figures, but no dedicated psychophysical study was conducted under the same rapid‐classification conditions, hindering a precise comparison of human versus machine performance on isolated objects.  
6. Experimental confounds and protocol inconsistencies.  Details on preprocessing steps, test‐time augmentations, and training‐from‐scratch versus fine‐tuning are insufficiently standardized, which may affect fair model comparisons.  
7. Presentation and formatting issues.  Minor errors—broken footnotes, inconsistent figure labels, and variable image scales—detract from the overall clarity.

RECOMMENDATIONS FOR REVISION  
• Integrate automated pipelines.  Evaluate off‐the‐shelf object detectors or segmentation models (e.g., Mask R‐CNN trained on COCO or LVIS) on ObjectNet images to assess whether robustness improvements persist with realistic masking.  
• Expand class coverage.  Extend the analysis to more ObjectNet categories—or include multi‐label annotations—to test generalization beyond the 113 overlap classes.  
• Statistical validation.  Report bootstrapped confidence intervals or conduct paired significance tests to confirm that accuracy and robustness gains are reliable.  
• Human psychophysics study.  Run a small‐scale human experiment on cropped versus full‐scene ObjectNet samples under matched timing constraints to establish a ground‐truth baseline.  
• End‐to‐end training experiments.  Fine‐tune or train models from scratch on isolated‐object crops (perhaps drawn from COCO or LVIS) to determine if the benefits of object isolation carry over to learned representations.  
• Clarify protocols and clean up formatting.  Provide full details on preprocessing, augmentations, and training regimes, and address minor editorial issues for improved readability.  
• Revisit terminology.  Rename the work to “Contemplating Real-World Object Classification” to avoid conflating “recognition” with the broader detection/classification task.

DECISION: MARGINAL  
While this paper presents valuable empirical insights into the role of context and robustness in object classification, its core contribution—foreground isolation via cropping or masks—is largely expected and remains untested under realistic, automated settings. Strengthening the work with automated detectors, statistical rigor, broader class coverage, and human baselines would elevate its impact. As it stands, I recommend a rating of 5: marginally below the conference threshold.

title: Provably robust classification of adversarial examples with detection

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
Summary: 
This paper deals with the problem of bounding the amount of errors that a model can make when attacked by an adversary limited to small perturbation of an input image. Similarly to previous paper, it proposes to extend the classifier with a detector to identify adversarial examples. As opposed to previous papers, the authors suggest to perform formal verification to prove that the samples are either robustly classified or abstained on (as opposed to testing the classifier + detector adversarially which would have left open the question of whether or not the attack against the pair was done in an adequate way). The method used for this is based on the simple IBP algorithm but the authors propose a proper encoding of the max(abstain_logit, gt_logit). They also follow the IBP strategy of using the bound resulting from verification as part of the training objective in order to encourage the network to behave properly.
Experimental results are reported on MNIST and CIFAR-10, and shows that there are scenarios in which the behaviour caused by this algorithm might be desirable.The experimental protocol for training network with the proposed method is described in significant amount of details and it should be possible to reproduce experimental results based on the description.

The crux of the paper lies in redefining the way accuracy should be computed in the case of an abstain option and adjusting verification and robust training methodologies for it. The accuracy is computed in the following way:
* For natural images, the prediction is considered correct only if the output is the correct class.
* For perturbed image, the prediction can be either the correct class OR the abstain class.
This might be sensible, but unless I'm mistaken, this could lead to degenerate cases where the network could achieve a verified error of 0 while having a standard error of 100% (see below). It's regrettable that we lose the property of the verified error rate being an upper bound on the standard error rate.

If I'm not mistaken, the results in Table 1 are hard to evaluate because the methods proposed by the authors is the only one that considers an abstain class, which gives it leeway to "cheat". A pathological example of this would be a network always outputting "abstain". If I understand correctly, it would be classified as having 0% verified error, while having 100% standard error. A moderate version of this is what can be observed for all the pairs (except CIFAR10-eps=16) : standard error goes up compared to IBP while verified error goes down. 
Figure 1. is however quite helpful in showing that there might be some benefit in the proposed approach if the goal of an application leans more towards "Never making bad predicitions" rather than "Always making good predicitions".

Comments:
- I am not entirely sure of the premise of the papers that "adversarial examples is something that we can defend against". I am not convinced that people are worried about an attacker crafting attacks to fool their classifier. The problem is that we want our classifiers to be robust to small perturbation, because that's a desirable property and adversarial examples is a sign that this property is not met. 

There might be some potential extension to this paper in the direction of OOD detection. Out of Distribution examples tend to make the classifier perform poorly. There is also a glut of methods that claims to be able to detect out of distribution examples without any formal guarantees so I would encourage the authors to look in that direction for extensions to their work.

Opinion: The paper is quite interesting to read, and as far as I can tell is the first one to apply verification methods to the detection of adversarial examples. This is a welcome contribution as opposed to all the methods that claim to detect attacks but can not provide any guarantees.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper tackles the problem of certifiably bounding a classifier’s error under small adversarial perturbations by augmenting a standard K-way network with an additional “abstain” label and applying Interval Bound Propagation (IBP) not only to classification but also to detection. Unlike prior work that only empirically tests classifier-plus-detector pairs, the authors propose a formal verification framework: they derive convex relaxations (Theorems 1–3) and an analytic dual formulation for the max(abstain_logit, ground-truth_logit) bound, integrate these certificates into the training objective, and demonstrate that the resulting network is “provably” either correct or abstaining on all ε-bounded perturbations. Experiments on MNIST and CIFAR-10 show that, compared to vanilla IBP, this joint classification-detection approach yields lower verified error—particularly at larger ε—and offers a clear trade-off curve between natural accuracy and robustness.

Strengths  
1. Novelty of certified detection. This is, to our knowledge, the first work to provide formal guarantees that perturbed inputs are either correctly classified or flagged, moving beyond heuristic detectors without provable bounds.  
2. Rigorous theoretical treatment. The convex relaxations and dual certificate derivations are clearly presented, and Algorithm 1 for efficient last-layer bound computation is both sound and implementable.  
3. Seamless integration into IBP training. By folding the verification bound into the loss, the method adds only modest computational overhead compared to standard IBP, and training details (schedulers, ramp-up of η/κ) are thoroughly documented for reproducibility.  
4. Empirical trade-off analysis. Figure 1’s Pareto-front visualization is particularly helpful, illustrating how abstention can be leveraged to drive down verified error at the cost of higher natural error—a desirable property for safety-critical applications that prefer “no prediction” over wrong decisions.

Weaknesses  
1. Evaluation scope is narrow. All experiments are confined to MNIST and CIFAR-10 using a single IBP-based architecture. It remains unclear how the approach generalizes to larger datasets (e.g., SVHN, TinyImageNet) or to stronger verifiers such as IBP–CROWN or SDP relaxations.  
2. Impact on natural accuracy and abstention rate. While verified error decreases, standard error often increases (Table 1), and the network may abstain excessively on clean inputs. A pathological “always abstain” model would trivially achieve 0% verified error but 100% standard error, raising concerns about practical utility. Detailed detection metrics—ROC curves, false-positive/false-negative rates, or cost-sensitive trade-offs—are not reported.  
3. High absolute robust error. On CIFAR-10 at ε=8/255, verified error remains above 60%, limiting real-world applicability.  
4. Missing comparisons and ablations. The paper compares only to vanilla IBP. It omits state-of-the-art verifiable defenses (e.g., CROWN, LP-based, or randomized smoothing methods) and lacks ablation studies on key hyperparameters (η, κ, λ schedules) or the tightness of the new bound relative to standard IBP.  
5. Adaptive attack evaluation. The empirical robustness relies on PGD attacks targeting classification, but stronger adaptive or black-box attacks specifically crafted to bypass the detection class are not considered.

Detailed Comments and Suggestions  
– Clarify the verification metric and its relation to standard error. The definition of “verified error” with abstention is sensible but can be misleading without bounding the standard error. You might discuss conditions under which the verified error remains an upper bound on true error.  
– Report detection performance curves (ROC or precision­/recall) to quantify trade-offs between abstaining on adversarial versus clean images. This will illustrate practical operating points rather than single-point summaries.  
– Extend experiments to at least one additional benchmark (e.g., SVHN or TinyImageNet) and, if possible, a larger architecture to assess scalability and generality.  
– Compare against other detection or certification baselines, such as Laidlaw & Feizi’s provable detection, IBP–CROWN, or LP-based verifiers, to contextualize improvements.  
– Provide ablation studies on the schedules and weights for natural, robust, and abstain losses (η, κ, λ). Quantify how sensitive the results are to these choices and offer guidelines for tuning.  
– Discuss practical deployment considerations: How should one set an abstention budget? What are the implications for user experience when abstaining on clean inputs?  
– Ensure code release for reproducibility and consider summarizing the main algorithmic steps in the body for clarity.

Minor Comments  
1. A brief step-by-step pseudocode in the main text (beyond Algorithm 1) would help readers grasp the training pipeline at a glance.  
2. Typographical fixes: check consistency of ε notation (sometimes “eps” vs. “ε”) and ensure all acronyms (e.g., IBP) are defined at first use.

Conclusion  
Overall, this paper makes a meaningful contribution by introducing the first certifiable framework for adversarial detection via an abstain class, backed by clear theory and promising empirical trade-off curves. While the evaluation is currently limited in scope and further ablation and baseline comparisons are needed, the core idea is sound and of interest to the community. I recommend acceptance.

Rating: 7: Good paper, accept
reviews.original_text:
Review:
Summary: 
This paper deals with the problem of bounding the amount of errors that a model can make when attacked by an adversary limited to small perturbation of an input image. Similarly to previous paper, it proposes to extend the classifier with a detector to identify adversarial examples. As opposed to previous papers, the authors suggest to perform formal verification to prove that the samples are either robustly classified or abstained on (as opposed to testing the classifier + detector adversarially which would have left open the question of whether or not the attack against the pair was done in an adequate way). The method used for this is based on the simple IBP algorithm but the authors propose a proper encoding of the max(abstain_logit, gt_logit). They also follow the IBP strategy of using the bound resulting from verification as part of the training objective in order to encourage the network to behave properly.
Experimental results are reported on MNIST and CIFAR-10, and shows that there are scenarios in which the behaviour caused by this algorithm might be desirable.The experimental protocol for training network with the proposed method is described in significant amount of details and it should be possible to reproduce experimental results based on the description.

The crux of the paper lies in redefining the way accuracy should be computed in the case of an abstain option and adjusting verification and robust training methodologies for it. The accuracy is computed in the following way:
* For natural images, the prediction is considered correct only if the output is the correct class.
* For perturbed image, the prediction can be either the correct class OR the abstain class.
This might be sensible, but unless I'm mistaken, this could lead to degenerate cases where the network could achieve a verified error of 0 while having a standard error of 100% (see below). It's regrettable that we lose the property of the verified error rate being an upper bound on the standard error rate.

If I'm not mistaken, the results in Table 1 are hard to evaluate because the methods proposed by the authors is the only one that considers an abstain class, which gives it leeway to "cheat". A pathological example of this would be a network always outputting "abstain". If I understand correctly, it would be classified as having 0% verified error, while having 100% standard error. A moderate version of this is what can be observed for all the pairs (except CIFAR10-eps=16) : standard error goes up compared to IBP while verified error goes down. 
Figure 1. is however quite helpful in showing that there might be some benefit in the proposed approach if the goal of an application leans more towards "Never making bad predicitions" rather than "Always making good predicitions".

Comments:
- I am not entirely sure of the premise of the papers that "adversarial examples is something that we can defend against". I am not convinced that people are worried about an attacker crafting attacks to fool their classifier. The problem is that we want our classifiers to be robust to small perturbation, because that's a desirable property and adversarial examples is a sign that this property is not met. 

There might be some potential extension to this paper in the direction of OOD detection. Out of Distribution examples tend to make the classifier perform poorly. There is also a glut of methods that claims to be able to detect out of distribution examples without any formal guarantees so I would encourage the authors to look in that direction for extensions to their work.

Opinion: The paper is quite interesting to read, and as far as I can tell is the first one to apply verification methods to the detection of adversarial examples. This is a welcome contribution as opposed to all the methods that claim to detect attacks but can not provide any guarantees.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

reviews.confidence: 5: The reviewer is absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct and very familiar with the relevant literature
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This paper proposes a new defense method based on adding a reject class to a classifier trained for robust classification. The paper has merits, it is formally sound and it improves the SOA. However, my rating is not so high for the following reasons:

- Only a short paragraph at the end is devoted to the natural-vs-robust error trade-off and the comparison is done only with IBP (another defense method of 2018). In my opinion this important trade-off is being neglected. What is the point of developing classifiers that are robust to AEs when they are not robust to other normal images (like those found in the test set)?
- Adding a reject class may not seem fair. Particularly, the paper states (p.4) "the classification/detection tasks is considered successful if the input is classified either as the correct class y or as the abstain class a; as both cases prevent misclassification of the adversarially perturbed input as a wrong class". I do not agree with that; it should be considered successful only if the input is classified as the correct class y, period. The "abstain" class is just a reject option, which is not the same as the correct classification. One can always resort to rejection to improve the error rate in the non-rejected part of the dataset. When there is a reject option in the system the figure of merit should be the ratio between error and rejection rate, while in your case you are using only error as the figure of merit. What would be the error-reject trade-off curve of the base classifier in these same datasets? 

Also, I could not understand the relative weights lambda 1 and 2 in Eq. 14. Are they correct? shouldn't there be a weight also for the first term Lrobust? Likewise in Eq. 18 you state that for lambda1=0 the training reduces to that of Gowal but the weight terms are different in Eqs. 18 and 7.

Minor: 
- When referencing an equation you should capitalise the first letter E. 
- "plotted in 2"
- "network can increase robust"

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper proposes augmenting a K‐class classifier with an additional “abstain” class and extending Interval Bound Propagation (IBP) to jointly certify robust classification and adversarial detection. The authors derive convex relaxations (Theorems 1–3), present an analytic dual solver (Algorithm 1), and integrate these certificates into training with minimal overhead. Empirical results on MNIST and CIFAR-10 demonstrate reduced verified error, particularly at larger perturbation radii, compared to standard IBP.

Strengths  
- Novel integration of a reject/abstain option into a certifiable defense framework, marking the first joint training and certification of classification plus detection.  
- Rigorous theoretical derivations, including convex relaxations and an efficient O(n log n) dual computation algorithm, that are clearly presented and reproducible.  
- Modest computational overhead over vanilla IBP training, with empirical gains in verified robust error on CIFAR-10 at larger ε values.  
- Well‐documented training schedules and parameter ramping that yield stable convergence.

Weaknesses  
1. Natural‐vs. robust trade-off is underexplored. Only a brief paragraph compares to IBP, neglecting how natural accuracy degrades when the model abstains on clean inputs. The paper treats abstentions as “successful” defenses, but a more meaningful metric is the error-reject trade-off or the ratio of misclassification to rejection. What is the baseline classifier’s error-reject curve on these datasets?  
2. Fairness of the reject class. Counting an abstain as success risks trivial solutions: one can abstain on every input to minimize error. The evaluation should report the fraction of natural examples on which the model abstains and define a clear figure of merit (e.g., ROC curves or cost-sensitive metrics) rather than raw error.  
3. Limited empirical scope. Experiments are confined to MNIST and CIFAR-10 with a single IBP baseline. There is no comparison to tighter relaxations (e.g., CROWN, LP‐ or SDP‐based methods) or recent detection schemes (e.g., Laidlaw & Feizi 2019). Scalability to larger datasets (SVHN, TinyImageNet) and deeper architectures remains unclear.  
4. Robust error rates remain high. Even with abstention, the absolute certified error on CIFAR-10 at ε = 8/255 exceeds 60%. The practical utility of such error rates, especially when clean‐input abstention is nonnegligible, warrants further discussion.  
5. Hyperparameter and formulation clarity. The relative weights λ₁ and λ₂ in Eq. 14 lack justification, and it is unclear why the robust loss term has no explicit weight. Moreover, the claim that setting λ₁ = 0 recovers Gowal et al.’s training (Eq. 18 vs. Eq. 7) appears inconsistent and needs clarification.  
6. Attack evaluation. The adaptive attacks are limited to PGD; stronger or black-box attacks, as well as targeted evaluations against the detection mechanism, are not assessed.  

Suggestions for Improvement  
• Thoroughly analyze the error-reject trade-off: report ROC curves, precision/recall, and the fraction of abstentions on clean vs. adversarial inputs. Compare these curves to those of the baseline classifier without robustness training.  
• Provide ablation studies on hyperparameters (λ₁, λ₂, η schedules) and quantify sensitivity. Offer guidance on how to tune these parameters in practice.  
• Extend experiments to additional benchmarks (SVHN, TinyImageNet) and evaluate on larger, modern architectures to demonstrate generality and scalability.  
• Compare against stronger certifiable defenses (IBP-CROWN, LP/SDP verifiers) and existing detection methods under adaptive threat models.  
• Clarify the derivation and role of each loss term, correct equation references (capitalize “Eq.”), and fix minor typos (e.g., “plotted in 2”, “network can increase robust”).  
• Discuss practical deployment considerations: how to set an abstention budget, handle user feedback upon abstention, and integrate cost‐sensitive decision thresholds.  

Conclusion  
This work makes a meaningful conceptual and theoretical advance by integrating an abstain class into certified IBP defenses. The proposed method is formally sound and shows empirical improvements, yet the evaluation remains narrow and key trade-offs are only superficially addressed. With deeper analysis of the natural vs. robust trade-off, broader empirical validation, and clearer hyperparameter justification, this approach could have stronger practical impact. Rating: 6 (Marginally above acceptance threshold).

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold

reviews.confidence: 3: The reviewer is fairly confident that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
##########################################################################

Summary: 

In this paper, the authors propose an additional "abstain/detection" loss term into training, so that the classifier can either robustly classify or detect an adversarial attack. They extend the interval bound propagation method for certified robustness under L_infity perturbations (a simple bounding in changes of the weight after each NN layer application, using a box constraint). Hyperparameters in the objective trade-off between clean and adversarial accuracy. 

##########################################################################

Reasons for score: 
 
Overall, I think the paper is borderline. I like the idea of adding an "abstain" error term in the loss during training, and I like that the authors have derived some (simple) bounds on the min-max formulations. They show some benefit of addition of abstain/detection in the training. However, my major concern is that if these results could also be obtained without adding these losses in the objective, but by simply comparing "closeness" or confusion in the class labels from the classifier, and abstaining from classification as a post-processing step instead. What is the benefit of adding this to the training? 
 
##########################################################################

Pros: 

The authors are trying to break the loop of adversarial training and subsequent adaptive attacks, by developing "provably robust" methods.

The idea of incorporating an "abstain" loss term at the training stage, and trading it off with the true error in classification is interesting. They further modify the loss slightly to exclude true and abstain classes (perhaps, driven by experiments). 

They obtain (simple) convex upper bounds on the loss functions and minimize these. 

##########################################################################

Cons: 

1. Choice of the loss functions: why the specific choice? Loss for misusing abstain class or mislabeling the true label could be something more general. 

2. Choice of hyperparameters: How does one select \lamba_1 and \lambda_2 in practice? The authors mention a few practical ways (also used by authors in related works), e.g., ramp down of \kappa etc. Why are these good choices?

3. Clarity in the writing: The paper is unclear at many places- for example, it is nowhere mentioned that the maximum weighted class is finally chosen, and the interval bounds are not propagated at this last layer. Algorithm 1 is explained nowhere in the paper, except in a psuedocode. 

4. Can similar benefits in adversarial robustness be obtained by post-processing an adversarially trained model?
 
##########################################################################

Questions during rebuttal period: 

 
Please address and clarify the cons above 

 
#########################################################################

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:  
This paper introduces a novel extension to certified adversarial defenses by augmenting a standard K-class neural network with an additional “abstain” class, thereby enabling classifiers that either robustly predict the correct label or detect (and abstain on) adversarial inputs. Building on Interval Bound Propagation (IBP), the authors derive convex upper and lower bounds (Theorems 1–3) for this joint classification/detection objective, present an efficient dual formulation and an O(n log n) algorithm (Algorithm 1) for bound computation, and integrate these certificates into the training loss via three terms balancing natural accuracy, robust classification, and abstention-aware robustness. Empirical results on MNIST and CIFAR-10 demonstrate modest reductions in verified error at larger ε values compared to vanilla IBP, and Pareto‐front analyses illustrate the trade-off between clean accuracy and robust detection.

Strengths:  
1. Novelty and Theoretical Clarity  
   • This is, to my knowledge, the first work to provide provable certification for both classification and detection by embedding an abstain option directly into the training loss, rather than as a post-hoc filter.  
   • The convex relaxations and dual certificates are derived rigorously, and the analytic solver for the final layer bound is transparent and implementable.  
2. Efficiency and Practical Overhead  
   • The proposed extension incurs only modest computational overhead over standard IBP training and retains the simplicity of box constraints.  
   • Algorithm 1’s sorting-based dual computation runs in O(n log n), making it scalable for moderate network sizes.  
3. Empirical Demonstration of Trade-offs  
   • Experiments on MNIST and CIFAR-10 show that, by allowing the model to abstain on high-uncertainty points, one can achieve lower verified robust error at larger perturbation budgets (e.g., ε = 8/255).  
   • Pareto curves effectively visualize how tuning the abstention weight shifts the balance between clean accuracy and certified robustness.

Weaknesses and Points for Improvement:  
1. Motivation for In-Training Abstention vs. Post-Processing  
   • The paper does not fully clarify why incorporating an abstain loss during training is strictly better than applying a post-hoc decision rule (e.g., thresholding softmax confidences) on a standard adversarially trained model. A direct comparison or ablation would strengthen this claim.  
2. Limited Evaluation Scope  
   • All experiments are confined to MNIST and CIFAR-10 with a single, relatively small architecture. It remains unclear whether the approach scales to larger datasets (e.g., SVHN, TinyImageNet) or more complex networks, or how it compares to tighter relaxations (CROWN, IBP-CROWN) and other verifiable defenses (LP-based, SDP).  
   • Certified error rates, especially on CIFAR-10 at ε = 8/255, remain high (>60% error), and natural accuracy drops substantially when abstaining.  
3. Detection Metrics and Adaptive Attacks  
   • While robust error is reported, there is no ROC-style analysis showing true positive vs. false positive abstention rates on clean and adversarial inputs, nor cost-sensitive metrics to illustrate practical deployment trade-offs.  
   • The evaluation uses only PGD-based adaptive attacks; stronger white-box or black-box adaptive methods targeting both classification and detection would provide a more complete picture.  
4. Hyperparameter Sensitivity and Ablations  
   • Key hyperparameters (λ₁, λ₂, η, κ schedules) are selected heuristically with limited justification. An ablation study on these parameters and their impact on both robustness and abstention rate is necessary to guide practitioners.  
5. Clarity and Reproducibility  
   • Algorithm 1 is presented only as pseudocode, with little discussion of its implementation details in the text. The final decision rule (selecting the maximum weighted class) and how bounds propagate to the last layer should be described more clearly.  
   • Releasing code and hyperparameter schedules would greatly enhance reproducibility.

Decision:  
While the paper offers a creative and theoretically sound approach to certifiable detection by integrating an abstain option into IBP training, its empirical validation remains narrow, key comparisons and ablations are missing, and the practical benefits over simpler post-processing strategies are not convincingly demonstrated. For these reasons, I recommend a marginal rejection (Rating: 5 – below acceptance threshold), with encouragement to address the above concerns in a revision.

reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This paper aims to train networks that can map a possibly $\ell_\infty$-perturbed input to its class provably or map this input to the “abstain class” provably. This is achieved by training on the IBP output boxes together with a new loss function. The method diverts from the classical setting in which the classifier needs to be robust, by allowing the classifier to abstain. While this is an interesting idea, the paper seems to be rushed and not carefully written. The notation could be improved and presentation could be simplified. It seems as if the results from related work (Balunovic et. al. [3] and Mirman et. al. [4]) are missing in Table 1 and thus are not compared against. Further, some questions remain open after reading the paper:

Questions:
- Would the idea to have an explicit “abstain” together with the loss function work also for randomized smoothing [1]? How would the results look like?
- Have you tried to use certification methods using tighter relaxations like k-ReLU[2]?
- In Equation 8: Is this here for all $i$?
- How would your method perform when using COLT [3]?

Comments:
- in equation 3, it seems to me as if the second ‘+’ for $\underline{z}_l$ should be a ‘-’, similar for $\overline{z}_l$
- in equation 6: $\ell_{\text{xent}}$ should be defined before used

The comparison in the evaluation is not complete, questions remain open after reading the paper and evaluation is missing, hence this is a reject for me. 


[1] Cohen et. al.: 'Certified Adversarial Robustness via Randomized Smoothing'. http://proceedings.mlr.press/v97/cohen19c/cohen19c.pdf

[2] Singh et. al.: 'Beyond the Single Neuron Convex Barrier for Neural Network Certification'. http://papers.nips.cc/paper/9646-beyond-the-single-neuron-convex-barrier-for-neural-network-certification.pdf

[3] Balunovic et. al.: 'Adversarial Training and Provable Defenses: Bridging the Gap'. https://openreview.net/pdf?id=SJxSDxrKDr

[4] Mirman et. al.: 'A Provable Defense for Deep Residual Networks'. https://arxiv.org/pdf/1903.12519.pdf

######################

After reading the author's response: While i still think that extending the experimental evaluation along the axes described above would improve this work, i decided to raise my score.
