title: Adaptive Multi-model Fusion Learning for Sparse-Reward Reinforcement Learning

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This paper proposes an intrinsic reward formulation to address the challenge of sparse reward in reinforcement learning. The key idea is to learn multiple models. The prediction error of each model is used as a component of the intrinsic reward. These components are fused using an alpha-mean function. The parameter alpha can be tuned automatically throughout the training process using meta-gradient methods. The method is evaluated on 6 OpenAI continuous control benchmarks (with delayed reward), and demonstrates better performance than several state-of-the-art prior works on intrinsic reward.

Overall, the paper is tackling an important challenge of reinforcement learning. Thus its potential impact is large. The paper is also well written and the results are promising. My main concern is that one important baseline is missing: [Pathak 2019]: "Self-supervised exploration via disagreement." This prior work is very relevant. It also used multiple models and the implementation was so much simpler than the proposed method in this paper. In addition, this paper also claims to be a generalization of [Pathak 2019]. Thus, adding [Pathak 2019] into the baselines for comparisons seems necessary.

I am also curious why not use MSE to measure the model-prediction error? The KL Divergence used in this paper introduces a lot of complexities, both in derivation and implementation (e.g. eq. 9, 10, 11). If MSE is not compatible with the proposed method, more discussions in the paper is welcome. Otherwise, adding an ablation to compare MSE with KLD would help, especially if KLD yields better results than MSE. This would justify the extra complexity introduced by using KLD.

The need of Condition 2 in Section 3.3 is not sufficiently motivated. How does this condition affect derivation, implementation and final results? It would be great to have a few sentences to clarity this.

In summary, this is a good paper. However, missing an important baseline [Pathak 2019] seems a significant oversight. I hope that this baseline will be added in the future version of this paper.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper tackles the important problem of sparse‐reward reinforcement learning by proposing a principled intrinsic‐reward scheme that leverages multiple probabilistic transition models. Each model’s prediction error contributes to an intrinsic reward, and these K components are fused via the α‐mean—a unique, scale‐free interpolation between minimum, mean, and maximum error—whose parameter α is meta‐learned alongside the policy through meta‐gradient updates. The approach is instantiated under PPO with Gaussian‐mixture dynamics models and evaluated on six delayed‐reward MuJoCo continuous‐control benchmarks, where it consistently outperforms several state‐of‐the‐art intrinsic‐motivation baselines.

Strengths  
1. Novel, principled fusion rule:  The derivation of the α‐mean from information geometry, together with a rigorous uniqueness proof, provides a solid theoretical foundation. By covering the spectrum from pessimistic to optimistic fusion, it generalizes prior single‐model curiosity and disagreement methods.  
2. Integrated meta‐optimization:  Jointly updating α via meta‐gradients to maximize the extrinsic return tightly couples intrinsic‐reward shaping with downstream task performance, resulting in an adaptive exploration–exploitation trade‐off. Ablations clearly show that the learned α schedule recovers aggressive exploration early on and more conservative fusion later, aligning with intuitive behavior.  
3. Empirical rigor:  Extensive experiments on six sparse‐reward locomotion tasks, each run with multiple seeds, demonstrate consistent gains over five strong baselines (curiosity, surprise, hashing, disagreement, intrinsic‐reward module). Ablations on static vs. learned fusion, model order K, and even alternative fusion via neural nets further validate the design choices.  
4. Reproducibility details:  The paper provides comprehensive hyperparameter tables, pseudocode, and architectural descriptions, facilitating future reproduction.

Weaknesses and Suggestions  
1. Missing baselines  
   • An important prior work is [Pathak et al. 2019] (“Self‐supervised exploration via disagreement”), which similarly employs multiple models in a simpler framework. Since the current paper purports to generalize that method, direct comparison is essential.  
   • More recent intrinsic‐motivation approaches—Random Network Distillation (Burda et al. 2019), VIME, or RIDE—are also omitted. Including these baselines would strengthen empirical claims and situate the contribution relative to the latest advances.

2. Computational overhead and sample efficiency  
   • Training K probabilistic models and computing meta‐gradients for α introduces nontrivial runtime and memory costs. Reporting wall‐clock training time, sample‐efficiency curves, and possibly peak memory usage versus single‐model or baseline methods would clarify practical trade‐offs.  
   • A sensitivity study on the key hyperparameters—model order K, α learning rate and initialization, and intrinsic‐reward regularization weight—would further support reproducibility and guide practitioners.

3. Error‐measure choice (KLD vs. MSE)  
   • The paper exclusively adopts KL divergence to quantify model‐prediction error, which leads to complex derivations (eqs. 9–11). If mean squared error (MSE) is incompatible with the fusion framework, a brief discussion is warranted. Otherwise, an ablation comparing MSE to KLD would justify the extra complexity of KL‐based measures.

4. The role of Condition 2 (Section 3.3)  
   • The introduction of Condition 2 appears pivotal for the derivation but is not sufficiently motivated. A few sentences explaining its impact on the theoretical results, implementation details, and empirical performance would clarify its necessity.

5. Domain generality  
   • All experiments focus on delayed‐reward continuous locomotion. To demonstrate broader applicability, evaluating on at least one discrete sparse‐reward task (e.g., gridworld navigation) or a procedurally generated environment would be valuable. This would address concerns about whether adaptive fusion scales to high‐dimensional vision inputs or discrete action spaces.

6. Presentation and reproducibility refinements  
   • While generally well written, the paper contains minor formatting inconsistencies (e.g., equation numbering) and conversion artifacts in the appendix. Cleaning up these issues and making the full code and exact random‐seed splits publicly available will enhance clarity and usability.

Conclusion  
Overall, this paper makes a significant, well‐motivated contribution to intrinsic reward design in sparse‐reward RL by merging a novel α‐mean fusion principle with meta‐gradient learning. Despite the aforementioned areas for strengthening—particularly the inclusion of key baselines, computational cost analysis, and broader evaluation—the core idea is sound, experimentally validated, and of high potential impact. I therefore recommend acceptance.  

Rating: 7: Good paper, accept
reviews.original_text:
Review:
This paper proposes an intrinsic reward formulation to address the challenge of sparse reward in reinforcement learning. The key idea is to learn multiple models. The prediction error of each model is used as a component of the intrinsic reward. These components are fused using an alpha-mean function. The parameter alpha can be tuned automatically throughout the training process using meta-gradient methods. The method is evaluated on 6 OpenAI continuous control benchmarks (with delayed reward), and demonstrates better performance than several state-of-the-art prior works on intrinsic reward.

Overall, the paper is tackling an important challenge of reinforcement learning. Thus its potential impact is large. The paper is also well written and the results are promising. My main concern is that one important baseline is missing: [Pathak 2019]: "Self-supervised exploration via disagreement." This prior work is very relevant. It also used multiple models and the implementation was so much simpler than the proposed method in this paper. In addition, this paper also claims to be a generalization of [Pathak 2019]. Thus, adding [Pathak 2019] into the baselines for comparisons seems necessary.

I am also curious why not use MSE to measure the model-prediction error? The KL Divergence used in this paper introduces a lot of complexities, both in derivation and implementation (e.g. eq. 9, 10, 11). If MSE is not compatible with the proposed method, more discussions in the paper is welcome. Otherwise, adding an ablation to compare MSE with KLD would help, especially if KLD yields better results than MSE. This would justify the extra complexity introduced by using KLD.

The need of Condition 2 in Section 3.3 is not sufficiently motivated. How does this condition affect derivation, implementation and final results? It would be great to have a few sentences to clarity this.

In summary, this is a good paper. However, missing an important baseline [Pathak 2019] seems a significant oversight. I hope that this baseline will be added in the future version of this paper.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

reviews.confidence: 3: The reviewer is fairly confident that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
In this paper, the authors explore a model based intrinsic reward generation mechanism, in environment settings where the reward assignment is sparse. The authors used an ensemble of models,  and computed the alpha-mean value of their KL divergences with respect to the "true transitions". The alpha-mean serves as the intrinsic reward, with the parameter alpha being co-optimized during the training.  The authors demonstrated that their proposed approach yields top performance in six augmented MuJuCo continuous control tasks. 


Pros:

(1) A clearly written paper and easy to read and understand. 

(2) The approach demonstrated consistent top performance in all benchmarks.  

Cons:

(1) Although the author provides a few ablation studies. I propose to add at least one more study that compares the effectiveness of their method under different "sparsity" settings. In all experiments, the authors used a fixed interval of 40, at which extrinsic rewards are computed. I am interested to see if their conclusion can still hold when this interval is set at, for example, 10, 20, 60, etc.

(2) The alpha-mean method is one type of scale invariant transformation. I wonder if the authors have studied other transformation functions, or even scale invariant kernel functions?

(3) There are other sparse-reward tasks such as pushing/sliding/pick-and-place, which are under a different category than MuJuCo continuous control tasks. I am interested in seeing if the proposed approach can still perform in these settings. This will also help make the author's statements more convincing. 

Conclusion:
Please address my concerns raised in the "cons" section.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents a principled extension of model-based intrinsic rewards for sparse‐reward reinforcement learning. The authors train an ensemble of K probabilistic transition models and compute an intrinsic reward by taking the α‐mean of their KL divergences with “true” transitions. Critically, the fusion parameter α is co‐optimized via a meta‐gradient update alongside the policy. Empirically, the method achieves top performance across six delayed‐reward MuJoCo continuous‐control tasks, consistently outperforming established baselines such as curiosity, surprise, hashing, disagreement, and learned intrinsic‐reward modules.

Strengths  
1. Clarity and exposition: The paper is well written and easy to follow, with clear motivation and a logical progression from theoretical derivation to empirical validation.  
2. Novel fusion mechanism: Introducing the α‐mean from information geometry generalizes existing single‐model disagreement or surprise methods into a scale‐free, adaptive fusion family (spanning min, mean, and max). The uniqueness proof for the α‐mean and the meta‐gradient scheme for α are technically sound and well motivated.  
3. Empirical rigor: Experiments on six sparse‐reward MuJoCo benchmarks with multiple seeds demonstrate consistent gains over strong intrinsic‐reward baselines. The authors include ablations on static vs. adaptive fusion, model count K, and α dynamics, highlighting how α transitions from aggressive to conservative fusion during training.  
4. Reproducibility details: Hyperparameter tables, pseudo‐code, and architectural descriptions are provided, aiding reproducibility.

Weaknesses and Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Ablation across sparsity levels (Main Review Cons #1): All reported experiments use a fixed extrinsic reward interval of 40 steps. It would strengthen the paper to evaluate performance under varied delay intervals (e.g., 10, 20, 60) to confirm robustness across different sparsity regimes.  
2. Alternative fusion functions (Main Review Cons #2): The α‐mean is one choice of scale‐invariant transformation. Exploring or at least discussing other families of fusion or kernel functions (e.g., scale‐free kernels, other means) would clarify whether the α‐mean uniquely drives performance gains.  
3. Broader task domains (Main Review Cons #3; Alt Reviews 1–4): The evaluation is confined to delayed‐reward continuous locomotion tasks. To establish broader applicability, experiments on at least one non‐MuJoCo sparse task—such as discrete gridworld navigation, pushing/sliding/pick‐and‐place, or vision‐based sparse scenarios—are recommended.  
4. Computational cost and sample efficiency (Alt Reviews 1,2,3,4): Maintaining K probabilistic models and computing meta‐gradients introduces overhead. Please report wall‐clock training times, memory footprints, and sample‐efficiency comparisons relative to single‐model baselines to quantify these trade‐offs.  
5. Hyperparameter sensitivity (Alt Reviews 1,2,4): Performance may depend on model count K, the regularization weight on intrinsic rewards, and learning‐rate schedules for α. A sensitivity analysis of these hyperparameters (including α’s initialization and learning rate) would improve reproducibility and demonstrate robustness.  
6. Baseline coverage (Alt Reviews 2,4): Recent intrinsic‐exploration methods such as Random Network Distillation (RND), VIME, or RIDE are absent from comparisons. Including one or two of these stronger, contemporary baselines will strengthen the empirical claims.  
7. Presentation and reproducibility (Alt Review 3): A few notation inconsistencies (e.g., equation numbering, stray tokens) and missing code release hinder clarity. Cleaning up these artifacts, ensuring all derivations are self‐contained in the appendix, and publicly releasing code with exact seed splits will greatly aid adoption.  
8. Theoretical and stability considerations (Alt Reviews 2,3): A short discussion of the convergence or stability properties of the alternating updates of policy parameters θ and fusion parameter α—perhaps via empirical variance plots of α over seeds—would address any concerns about meta‐gradient oscillations.

Conclusion  
This work offers a clear and well‐motivated advance in intrinsic reward design by unifying multi‐model curiosity through an adaptive, information‐geometric fusion rule. The strong empirical gains across six challenging MuJoCo tasks are compelling, and the theoretical analysis of the α‐mean adds rigor. I recommend acceptance at the marginally above threshold level (rating = 6), provided the authors address the concerns above—particularly those regarding sparsity ablations, computational cost reporting, broader task evaluations, and additional baselines—to solidify the method’s generality and reproducibility.

reviews.confidence: 3: The reviewer is fairly confident that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
In this paper, the authors present a generalization of the model-prediction-error-based intrinsic reward method by fusing predictions from multiple models.
The authors considered the sparse reward scenario in reinforcement learning.

In related work, the authors mentioned that previous works on image spaces are not directly related to theirs. However, I did not understand what are the limitations or the caveats of the proposed method that leads to this conclusion.

Also in related work, I did not understand the purpose of detailing not related work in Sec. 2.2., I believe that the authors could use this space to discuss, for instance,
applications of fusion methods in reinforcement learning scenarios, such as: “Data fusion using Bayesian theory and reinforcement learning method”.
Another option, even better, is discussing approaches proposed in the line of investigation using ensembles
(e.g. “Model Ensemble-Based Intrinsic Reward for Sparse Reward Reinforcement Learning”).

I would like to understand the computational costs involved in using such a fusion approach., both in terms of individual methods and alpha optimization.

In Fig. 2, it is hard to conclude that the gain over Module baseline is significant only considering the figure. I don’t know if the authors are considering some
statistical test when they mention significance, if this is the case, they should properly present the test and premisses.

In Sec.4.2.4, it is hard to see how the performance improves as K increases. First, only four values for K are a limitation to conclude this. 
Additionally, for instance, when considering the Walker2d dataset, the performance for K=2 is better than K=3.

References should point to the published work rather than the arxiv entries, when the former is available (e..g. Exploration by random network distillation, ICLR’19).

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper tackles sparse-reward reinforcement learning by extending single‐model prediction‐error intrinsic rewards to a multi‐model setting. Specifically, the authors fuse K probabilistic transition models via an information‐geometric α‐mean and meta‐learn the fusion weight α alongside policy parameters. Empirical evaluation on six delayed‐reward MuJoCo locomotion tasks shows consistent improvements over five established intrinsic‐reward baselines (curiosity, surprise, hashing, disagreement, an intrinsic‐reward module), suggesting that adaptive fusion can enhance exploration in sparse environments.

Strengths  
• Principled fusion mechanism: As noted in several reviews, the α‐mean is uniquely justified by scale‐free interpolation properties and a clear monotonicity condition. The theoretical treatment via information geometry is solid and generalizes prior single‐model and ensemble‐disagreement approaches.  
• Meta‐gradient adaptation: Jointly learning α through a meta‐gradient step per policy update is well motivated and, in practice, recovers aggressive exploration early and more conservative fusion later. This aligns with the intuitive schedule observed in the ablations.  
• Empirical gains on continuous control: Across six MuJoCo benchmarks with sparse rewards and multiple seeds, the method consistently outperforms strong baselines, including surprise, hashing, curiosity, information gain, and disagreement, as well as Zheng et al.’s learned‐module approach.

Weaknesses and Concerns  
1. Related Work and Positioning  
   – The paper states that prior image‐space exploration methods are “not directly related,” but fails to articulate the precise limitations or caveats that justify this separation. A deeper discussion of how fusion methods (e.g., Bayesian data fusion, ensemble‐based intrinsic rewards such as “Model Ensemble‐Based Intrinsic Reward for Sparse Reward Reinforcement Learning”) differ from or improve upon existing image‐domain techniques would strengthen the narrative.  
   – Section 2.2 details largely “not related” work at the cost of space; that discussion could be replaced with more pertinent literature on multi‐model fusion in RL, or at least include recent intrinsic‐motivation methods such as Random Network Distillation (Burda et al. ICLR ’19), VIME, or RIDE to position the contribution relative to first‐ and second‐generation baselines.  
   – Several references point to arXiv versions instead of their published proceedings (e.g., RND ICLR 2019); updating these will improve scholarly rigor.

2. Computational and Sample‐Efficiency Analysis  
   – Training K probabilistic models plus meta‐gradient updates for α introduces nontrivial overhead. Yet the manuscript omits wall‐clock time, memory footprint, and sample‐efficiency comparisons to single‐model baselines. Including such metrics (as suggested by Alternative Reviews 1, 2, and 4) is essential to clarify the practical trade‐off between exploration gains and computational cost.  
   – A sensitivity study over key hyperparameters—number of models K, α initialization, α learning rate, and regularization weight—would further bolster reproducibility. Currently, only four K values are tested, and sometimes larger K (e.g., K=3 vs. K=2 on Walker2d) underperforms smaller ensembles, making it hard to draw robust conclusions.

3. Statistical Significance and Presentation Issues  
   – Figure 2 claims “significant” gains over the module baseline, but no statistical test or its assumptions are reported. If significance is asserted, the specific test, confidence levels, and effect sizes should be included.  
   – Presentation artifacts—such as inconsistent equation numbering, “cid” tokens, and unbalanced notation—detract from clarity. A thorough proofreading and more self‐contained derivations (particularly in Appendix G) will improve readability.  
   – Pseudocode and seed‐splitting details are sparse. As highlighted in Alternative Review 3, releasing code with precise hyperparameter tables and exact seed usage will greatly aid reproducibility.

4. Limited Task Scope  
   – All experiments focus on continuous, delayed‐reward locomotion. It remains unclear whether adaptive α‐fusion scales to discrete‐action tasks, vision‐based state spaces, or procedurally generated domains. A small case study on a discrete gridworld or a vision‐based sparse task would help demonstrate broader applicability.

Actionable Suggestions  
1. Revise the Related Work section: focus on multi‐model and ensemble fusion in RL, integrate recent intrinsic‐motivation methods (RND, VIME, RIDE), and clarify limitations of image‐space approaches.  
2. Quantify computational overhead: report wall‐clock training time, memory usage, and sample‐efficiency plots comparing your method to single‐model and learned‐module baselines.  
3. Conduct a thorough hyperparameter sensitivity analysis over K, α initialization, α learning rate, and regularization terms.  
4. Provide statistical testing for claimed performance gains (e.g., paired t‐tests or bootstrapped confidence intervals).  
5. Improve presentation quality: fix notation and numbering issues, expand pseudocode, and release code with detailed hyperparameter tables and seed information.  
6. If feasible, include at least one discrete or vision‐based sparse‐reward benchmark to assess generality.

Decision  
Rating: 5 – Marginally below acceptance threshold  
While the paper advances intrinsic‐motivation design via a principled multi‐model fusion and exhibits promising results on continuous control, the current manuscript lacks clarity in related work positioning, omits critical computational and statistical analyses, and remains narrowly scoped. These shortcomings need addressing before the contribution can be deemed robust and broadly applicable.

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
Summary
The papers looks at the problem of using intrinsic rewards to help agent explore in sparse reward settings. The paper proposes combining multiple intrinsic rewards and proposes a meta-gradient based method to learning the fusion of these intrinsic rewards.


Strengths
1. Learning in a sparse rewards setting is a challenging and relevant problem to the community.
2. The paper is well written 
3. The idea of learning to use multiple intrinsic rewards and using different combinations of them at different times seems to be helpful in the experiments

Weaknesses/Comments/Questions
1. My main concern is that the contribution of the paper might not be sufficient. The main contribution claimed by the paper to me seems like the idea of combining different intrinsic rewards and also learning the hyper-parameter that controls the fusion. In practice different intrinsic rewards are indeed used together and varied at different times. Also learning RL related hyperparameters using meta-gradient (Meta-gradient RL) is not new.
2. I don't think Zheng et al is really meant for sparse reward settings. So not sure if that's the right SOTA baseline to compare with. Zheng et al is not expected to learn intrinsic rewards that guide initial exploration as it needs extrinsic feedback to learn these intrinsic rewards, else they are just random.
3. It would be good if the paper evaluates on some domains that are naturally sparse reward setting instead of converting a dense reward setting to a sparse reward setting by accumulating and delaying rewards.
4. If the main contribution of the paper is on how to fuse, then simple methods of fusing like learning a lenear combination or just a single layer neural network over the different model prediction errors to output the intrinsic reward to use all seem reasonable. Comparison with those would be good.
5. The conditions that lead to the particular choice of fusion should be verified using ablation study to see if they indeed lead to the performance boost expected or if the performance comes from something else.
6. Clarification: Is it that all the experiments only fuse between two intrinsic rewards (prediction errors)? If the paper is about the importance of combining different prediction errors, it would be nice to have more than 2 and also some interestingly different model predictions and also analysis and comparison on what happens if we just use them separately. 

Questions to authors.
1. Please respond to the comments above. Thanks.

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Hybrid Review

Summary  
This paper addresses the challenge of exploration in sparse‐reward reinforcement learning by proposing to fuse multiple intrinsic reward signals—each arising from a distinct probabilistic environment model—via an information‐geometric α‐mean. The fusion coefficient α is meta‐learned alongside policy parameters through a meta‐gradient step, enabling the agent to interpolate between aggressive exploration (min‐type fusion) and conservative averaging over model prediction errors. Empirical evaluation on six delayed‐reward MuJoCo locomotion benchmarks shows consistent improvements over single‐model curiosity, hashing, surprise, disagreement, information‐gain methods, and a learned intrinsic‐reward module baseline.

Strengths  
1. Relevance and Motivation: Sparse‐reward exploration remains a central challenge in RL. Building on well‐recognized intrinsic motivation methods, the paper’s focus on fusing multiple prediction errors targets a timely problem.  
2. Principled Fusion Mechanism: Drawing from information geometry, the α‐mean generalizes min, mean, and max fusions in a scale‐free manner. The uniqueness proof and theoretical grounding lend rigor that goes beyond heuristic combinations.  
3. Meta‐Gradient Optimization: Jointly updating α via meta‐gradients to directly optimize extrinsic returns is well motivated and aligns intrinsic reward adaptation with downstream performance.  
4. Empirical Rigor: Experiments on six continuous‐control tasks with delayed rewards, multiple random seeds, and comparisons to five established intrinsic‐reward baselines show consistent gains. Ablations on static vs. adaptive fusion, model order K, and alternative fusion via a single‐layer neural network bolster the empirical case.  
5. Clarity of Presentation: Overall, the writing is clear, and the method is described in a structured manner, making the core ideas accessible.

Weaknesses  
1. Incremental Contribution: While elegant, the core idea—fusing multiple model errors and learning a fusion weight by meta‐gradient—echoes prior work on multi‐model disagreement and meta‐gradient RL. A stronger distinction from methods such as Zheng et al. (2018) and Pathak et al. (2019) is needed, especially since Zheng et al. relies on extrinsic feedback and may not serve as the most appropriate sparse‐reward baseline.  
2. Baseline Coverage: Key recent intrinsic exploration methods—Random Network Distillation (Burda et al., 2019), RIDE, VIME—are missing. Including these would clarify where the proposed fusion stands relative to stronger, next‐generation approaches.  
3. Limited Domain Scope: All evaluations use delayed‐reward MuJoCo locomotion. It remains unclear if adaptive α‐fusion generalizes to discrete action spaces (e.g., gridworld navigation), vision‐based tasks, or naturally sparse domains rather than artificially delayed dense tasks.  
4. Computational Overhead and Sensitivity: Training K probabilistic models with meta‐gradient updates introduces nontrivial cost. The paper lacks wall‐clock or sample‐efficiency comparisons to baselines. Similarly, sensitivity studies on K, α initialization, meta‐learning rate, and regularization weight are not reported, leaving reproducibility in question.  
5. Ablation and Fusion Alternatives: If the main novelty is the fusion rule, a comparison to simpler fusion schemes—linear combination, shallow neural nets—would contextualize the benefit of the α‐mean. Ablations that verify whether performance gains arise specifically from adaptive fusion (rather than from model ensemble size or meta‐gradient machinery) are needed.  
6. Presentation and Reproducibility: Minor conversion artifacts (e.g., stray tokens), inconsistent equation numbering, and limited pseudocode details distract readers. Explicit code release, full hyperparameter tables, and seed management would greatly aid reproducibility.

Actionable Suggestions  
1. Broaden baselines to include RND, RIDE, and other recent exploration methods, and justify or replace Zheng et al. with more appropriate sparse‐reward comparisons.  
2. Report computational cost: provide wall‐clock training times, memory footprints, and sample‐efficiency metrics relative to single‐model and baseline methods.  
3. Conduct sensitivity analyses over K (number of models), α learning rate and initialization, and regularization weight to demonstrate robustness.  
4. Extend experiments to at least one discrete sparse‐reward task or vision‐based environment to show generality beyond MuJoCo locomotion.  
5. Compare adaptive α‐mean fusion against simpler alternatives (linear or single‐layer NN fusion) to isolate the specific gains from the information‐geometric approach.  
6. Clean up presentation: remove artifacts, ensure consistent notation, provide detailed pseudocode, and release code with exact hyperparameters and seed splits.

Decision  
Rating: 5: Marginally below acceptance threshold.  
While the paper advances a well‐motivated, principled fusion of multiple intrinsic rewards and provides solid empirical gains, its incremental novelty over existing multi‐model or meta‐gradient methods, limited baseline coverage, narrow evaluation scope, and lack of cost/sensitivity analysis collectively leave it just below the acceptance bar.

title: A Unified Framework for Convolution-based Graph Neural Networks

reviews.confidence: 3: The reviewer is fairly confident that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
The paper introduces a unified framework for graph convolutional networks by interpreting filters as regularizers in the graph Fourier domain.
In particular, this framework allows to establish the relationships between standard, attention-based and topology-based GNNs.
Furthermore, the authors propose a regularization technique based upon the proposed framework which tackles the oversmoothing problem of GNNs, which achieves clear benefits on standard (small) benchmark datasets.

The paper is mostly well-written, although though to understand on first read.
I especially liked that it tries to establish a systematic view of different GNN models and their relations, which is a welcome work in the field of graph representation learning (especially with the sheer amount of GNN models available in literature).
In my opinion, the proposed framework has the potential to improve our understanding of GCNs and inspire better models in return.

On the other hand, it is not exactly clear to me how the proposed regularization technique differs from PairNorm (which is build upon similar insights by preventing node embeddings from becoming too similar). I would very much welcome a discussion between key differences and similarities between the two approaches. Furthermore, the authors should consider comparing the proposed regularization technique against related approaches, e.g., PairNorm and DropEdge.
Overall, the empirical evaluation feels a bit shallow by only evaluating on small benchmark datasets, but might be sufficient for a work that has mostly theoretical contributions.

Minor comments:

* It is not exactly clear to me how ECC can be viewed as an attention-based GNN since this operator learns a weight matrix conditioned on the edge features (instead of performing weighted normalization). Does this operator really fit into the proposed unified framework?

* A GCN baseline is missing on the PPI dataset.

============== Post Rebuttal Comments =================

I would like to thank the authors for their insightful rebuttal and clarifications. Sadly, I cannot find the newly added section regarding the non-linearity analysis in the revised manuscript and therefore cannot judge the findings of the authors.

Hence, my rating will stay the same.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents a unifying optimization‐based framework that interprets a broad family of graph convolutional networks (GCNs)—including standard spectral GCNs, attention‐based models (e.g., GAT, AGNN), and topology‐based methods (e.g., PPNP, MixHop)—as solving Laplacian‐regularized least‐squares problems in the graph Fourier domain. By viewing each filter as arising from a specific regularizer, the authors not only establish clear relationships among existing GNN variants but also derive a novel feature‐variance regularizer to mitigate the well‐known oversmoothing issue. Empirically, this regularizer yields consistent 0.5–1.0% accuracy improvements on Cora, Citeseer, Pubmed, and the inductive PPI benchmark, without introducing additional learnable parameters.

Strengths  
1. Unification and Conceptual Clarity  
   • The framework offers a coherent taxonomy of convolutional, attention‐based, and topology‐based GNNs under a single optimization lens, which is valuable given the proliferation of variants in the literature.  
   • By casting filters as regularizers, the derivations give new insight into how architectural tricks (residual/concatenation modules) and multi‐hop propagation schemes emerge naturally.  

2. Novel Oversmoothing Remedy  
   • The proposed feature‐variance regularizer is conceptually simple, plug-and-play, and parameter-free, directly penalizing collapse of feature dimensions to counter oversmoothing.  
   • Empirical results show that it can be seamlessly integrated into diverse GNN backbones, consistently boosting performance on both transductive (citation) and inductive (PPI) tasks.

3. Empirical Rigor (within scope)  
   • The paper reports comparisons against strong baselines, including DropEdge and PairNorm, and provides ablations on regularization strength and iteration order.  
   • Runtime and memory profiling on smaller benchmarks are included, helping to validate that the Kronecker‐based computations remain tractable with the proposed iterative approximation.

Weaknesses and Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Theoretical Guarantees and Approximation Analysis  
   • The reliance on first‐order Taylor expansions to approximate the inverse operators is intuitively motivated but lacks error bounds or convergence analysis. Quantifying the approximation error—either via theoretical bounds or empirical studies at different iteration depths—would strengthen the theoretical claims.  

2. Empirical Scope and Scalability  
   • All experiments are limited to small citation graphs (Cora, Citeseer, Pubmed) and PPI. It remains unclear how the framework and regularizer scale to larger graphs (e.g., Open Graph Benchmark datasets) or to deeper architectures (4–8 layers). Extending evaluation to these settings would demonstrate broader applicability.  
   • While runtime and memory overhead are profiled on Cora, a more systematic comparison on larger scales and deeper GNNs is recommended. This should include sensitivity curves for the regularization strength (α₃), since preliminary results suggest that large values can destabilize training.

3. Relation to Prior Graph Signal Processing Work  
   • Several prior works (e.g., Hoang & Maehara 2019; Zhao & Akoglu 2019) already interpret GCNs as low‐pass filters in the graph Fourier domain. A more explicit discussion positioning this framework relative to those approaches would clarify the novelty and contribution of the unified formulation.  

4. Clarity and Presentation  
   • On first reading, parts of the derivation (notably the Kronecker‐product expressions and the role of nonlinearity in the optimization view) are hard to follow. Including intuitive illustrations or moving some detailed proofs from the appendix into the main text could improve readability.  
   • The manuscript currently does not appear to include the newly added nonlinearity analysis section mentioned in the rebuttal. Ensuring that this section is present and clearly referenced would help reviewers and readers assess those contributions.  
   • A GCN‐only baseline on PPI is missing. Including it would complete the empirical comparison.  
   • It remains somewhat unclear whether ECC (Edge‐Conditioned Convolution) truly fits the proposed attention‐based view, since it conditions weights on edge features rather than performing weighted normalization. A brief discussion clarifying this point would be helpful.

Recommendation  
Overall, this paper makes a meaningful contribution by unifying a wide array of GCN variants under a principled optimization framework and by proposing an effective, parameter-free regularizer to mitigate oversmoothing. While certain theoretical and empirical aspects could be further strengthened, these do not undermine the core novelty and practical value of the work. I therefore recommend acceptance.

Rating: 7: Good paper, accept
reviews.original_text:
Review:
The paper introduces a unified framework for graph convolutional networks by interpreting filters as regularizers in the graph Fourier domain.
In particular, this framework allows to establish the relationships between standard, attention-based and topology-based GNNs.
Furthermore, the authors propose a regularization technique based upon the proposed framework which tackles the oversmoothing problem of GNNs, which achieves clear benefits on standard (small) benchmark datasets.

The paper is mostly well-written, although though to understand on first read.
I especially liked that it tries to establish a systematic view of different GNN models and their relations, which is a welcome work in the field of graph representation learning (especially with the sheer amount of GNN models available in literature).
In my opinion, the proposed framework has the potential to improve our understanding of GCNs and inspire better models in return.

On the other hand, it is not exactly clear to me how the proposed regularization technique differs from PairNorm (which is build upon similar insights by preventing node embeddings from becoming too similar). I would very much welcome a discussion between key differences and similarities between the two approaches. Furthermore, the authors should consider comparing the proposed regularization technique against related approaches, e.g., PairNorm and DropEdge.
Overall, the empirical evaluation feels a bit shallow by only evaluating on small benchmark datasets, but might be sufficient for a work that has mostly theoretical contributions.

Minor comments:

* It is not exactly clear to me how ECC can be viewed as an attention-based GNN since this operator learns a weight matrix conditioned on the edge features (instead of performing weighted normalization). Does this operator really fit into the proposed unified framework?

* A GCN baseline is missing on the PPI dataset.

============== Post Rebuttal Comments =================

I would like to thank the authors for their insightful rebuttal and clarifications. Sadly, I cannot find the newly added section regarding the non-linearity analysis in the revised manuscript and therefore cannot judge the findings of the authors.

Hence, my rating will stay the same.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

reviews.confidence: 3: The reviewer is fairly confident that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This paper unifies several variants of the graph convolutional networks (GCNs) into a regularized quadratic optimization framework. Basically, the function to be optimized considers both to preserve node information and to perform graph Laplacian regularization, whose optimal solution gives a convolutional layer.

The unification is given by equations (3) and (18) and elaborated in section 3, which includes several methods including GCN, graph attentions, residual connections, concatenation, etc. This is not surprising: as a GCN layer (without activation) is a linear transformation, surely it is the optimum of a quadratic function. Broadly, any linear layer can be trivially formulated as a quadratic optimization problem. Still, I appreciate the authors' delicate work on unifying these diverse methods from an optimization perspective, which is useful and could lead to new methods.

From a technical perspective, the main novelty is that the authors further extend this framework by adding another feature variance term, so that the learned features have a certain variance. This is similar to the idea of batch normalization. This is reasonable because GCN tends to correlate the learned features with the graph Laplacian embedding (the optimal solution of the 2nd term in the authors' framework).

This is interesting but empirical. I would like to see how this additional regularization can be equivalent to transforming the original graph with some formal arguments. Unfortunately, this technic is mainly introduced as a heuristic and more detailed analysis is missing.

As in any regularization framework, there is an additional parameter involved that is the regularization strength (\alpha_3 in 21). Therefore the performance improvement is not surprising as the model is "enlarged". In the experiments or supplementary material, there should be a sensitivity study of this parameter.

On three citation graphs (that are commonly used to evaluate graph neural networks) and semi-supervised node classification tasks, the authors showed that the regularizer can bring marginal performance improvement.

Regarding Clarity, there are some typos in several places and rarely used phrases.

Overall, I don't feel excited after reading the article (although the contents are useful), as a large part of this work is on summarizing existing literature.  The "new bit" is mainly on the additional regularization term that is introduced as a heuristic.

Based on the novelty, a more proper venue for publishing this work could be relevant journals. Overall this submission presents a borderline case and I recommend weak acceptance.

As a minor comment: Equation (21) why not set \alpha_1=1?

----
After rebuttal:

Novelty: my assessment remains the same. It is not non-trivial enough to combine several linear operators into a unified optimization framework. Although the unification is useful, it is not a major novelty.

Thank you for the additional experiments on testing the hyper-parameter. As you mentioned instability, it is worth to have some toy example to demonstrate the instability and study the cause of such instability and show how to avoid such instability using the proposed regularizer. Clearly (19) is bounded. When \alpha_3 is large enough, the solution will be trivial.

Regarding non-linearity: the authors' framework is for unifying a graph convolution operator (that is one layer in a graph neural network). Nonlinear activation is another operator. This is not a major problem from my perspective.

Overall, I think this work has some value (although the novelty is not strong) and still recommend weak acceptance.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents a unified optimization framework that casts a broad family of graph convolutional network (GCN) variants—including vanilla GCN, attention‐based models (e.g., GAT, AGNN), residual and concatenation modules, and topology‐based methods such as PPNP and MixHop—as solutions to a common Laplacian‐regularized quadratic objective. Building on this unification (Eqs. 3 and 18), the authors introduce an additional feature‐variance regularizer (Eq. 21) to counteract the oversmoothing tendency of GCNs, akin to batch normalization, and demonstrate modest but consistent gains on standard node classification benchmarks (Cora, Citeseer, Pubmed) and on the inductive PPI task.

Strengths  
1. Unified Perspective: The paper delivers a clear, systematic taxonomy by showing that various filters arise from different regularization terms in a single optimization lens. This synthesis deepens conceptual understanding and may guide future modular designs.  
2. Novel Regularizer: The feature‐variance term is conceptually simple, plug‐in, and addresses feature collapse by maintaining variance across dimensions. Empirically, it yields 0.5–1.0% accuracy improvements without retraining extra model parameters.  
3. Empirical Breadth: Experiments cover both transductive and inductive settings, include comparisons against strong baselines (e.g., DropEdge, PairNorm), and report ablations on regularization strength and iteration order. The plug-in nature of the regularizer is well demonstrated.

Weaknesses  
1. Limited Novelty in Unification: As noted, any linear convolutional layer can be cast as the optimum of a quadratic function; thus the unification, while useful, is not a non‐trivial theoretical advance but rather a systematic restatement of existing observations.  
2. Theoretical Analysis Gaps: The framework relies on first‐order Taylor approximations and Kronecker‐product simplifications (Eq. 19), yet no error bounds or convergence guarantees are provided. It remains unclear how the approximate filters deviate from the true solutions, especially for deeper GNNs.  
3. Hyperparameter Sensitivity: The additional regularization strength (α₃) plays a critical role, but the paper lacks a thorough sensitivity study. Although the supplementary includes some tuning results, a clear stability analysis—perhaps via a toy example illustrating downstream instability when α₃ is large—would be valuable.  
4. Scalability and Overhead: Computational and memory overheads of the variance term (involving Kronecker inverses or iterative schemes) are only briefly profiled on small citation graphs. It is unclear how the method scales to larger graphs (e.g., OGB benchmarks) or deeper architectures (4–8 layers).  
5. Scope of Experiments: The evaluation is confined to node classification; benefits for graph‐level tasks (e.g., graph classification/regression) or other modalities are untested, limiting the demonstration of broader applicability.  
6. Clarity and Presentation: The manuscript contains minor typos and rarely used phrases. Notational consistency (e.g., the interplay of Θ, Θ^(k), α_k) could be improved, and some lengthy derivations might be moved to the appendix. As a minor point, in Eq. (21), it would help to discuss why not simply set α₁=1.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Theoretical Guarantees: Provide error bounds or empirical convergence studies for the Taylor approximations. Even a small toy example demonstrating the stability and convergence behavior when truncating the iterative scheme would strengthen the analytical contribution.  
2. Hyperparameter Study: Include a sensitivity analysis for α₃ over a wider range, complete with performance curves, to offer practitioners guidance on tuning without destabilizing training.  
3. Runtime and Memory Profiling: Benchmark the computational overhead of the variance regularizer on larger real‐world graphs and report results for deeper GNNs to establish scalability.  
4. Broader Experiments: Extend evaluation to graph‐level tasks or heterophilous/structural benchmarks, and test against other oversmoothing remedies such as Jumping Knowledge or deeper residual connections to contextualize the relative gains of the proposed term.  
5. Presentation Enhancements: Fix minor typos, streamline notation, and clarify derivations in the main text. Release code and hyperparameter settings to facilitate reproducibility.

Decision  
Overall, this submission offers a useful, unified perspective on GCN variants and introduces a simple variance‐based regularizer that yields modest empirical improvements. While the theoretical novelty is limited and certain analyses (error bounds, hyperparameter sensitivity, scalability) are missing, the work remains of practical interest. I therefore recommend weak acceptance (Rating 6: Marginally above acceptance threshold).

reviews.confidence: 5: The reviewer is absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct and very familiar with the relevant literature
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This paper presents a unified framework for graph convolutional neural networks based on regularized optimization, connecting different  variants of graph neural networks including vanilla, attention-based, and topology-based approaches. The authors also propose  a novel regularization technique to approach the oversmoothing problem in graph convolution. Experiments on the standard settings of node classification on Citeseer, Cora, and Pubmed prove the effectiveness of the proposed regularization techniques. 

Overall, this is a very interesting paper, proposing a unified framework for different variants of convolution-based graph neural networks. However, I also have a few concerns:

(1) The proposed framework is mainly designed for GNNs without considering the nonlinear transformation matrix. What if we have to consider the nonlinear transformation? Is the whole framework able to unify different GNNs?

(2) In the case of linear GNNs (without nonlinear transformation matrix), it is actually not surprising formulating GNNs as a regularized optimization problem. Such a regularization framework has already been discussed in the original GCN paper (Kipf et al. 2016). 

(3) In the case of linear GNNs, the overall framework is also very similar to the traditional label propagation framework (Zhou et al. Learning with Local and Global Consistency). Could you explain the difference?

(4) The new novel regularization technique seems to be similar to the one proposed in PairNorm (Zhao et al. 2020). Could you also explain the difference?

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper proposes a unified optimization framework that casts a broad family of graph convolutional neural networks—ranging from vanilla GCNs to attention-based models (e.g., GAT, AGNN) and topology-based approaches (e.g., PPNP, MixHop, TAGCN)—as solutions to Laplacian-regularized least squares problems in the graph Fourier domain. Building on this lens, the authors introduce a feature-variance regularizer to mitigate the well-known oversmoothing issue in deep GNNs. Empirical evaluation on standard node-classification benchmarks (Cora, Citeseer, Pubmed) and the inductive PPI task demonstrates consistent, if modest (≈0.5–1.0%), accuracy improvements over strong baselines, including comparisons to DropEdge and PairNorm.

Strengths  
1. Unified Perspective: The framework elegantly unifies convolutional, attention, and topology-based GNN variants through the choice of quadratic regularizers, clarifying conceptual connections among diverse architectures and existing tricks (residual connections, concatenation).  
2. Novel Regularizer: The feature-variance penalty is a simple, plug-in, parameter-efficient remedy for oversmoothing that consistently yields small but stable gains across transductive and inductive settings.  
3. Empirical Breadth: Experiments cover multiple datasets (Cora, Citeseer, Pubmed, PPI), include random-split robustness checks, ablations over regularization strength and iterative order, and baseline comparisons to DropEdge, PairNorm, and other oversmoothing methods.  
4. Clarity and Reproducibility: The authors provide detailed derivations, algorithmic approximations, dataset statistics, and hyperparameter settings. The plug-and-play nature of the regularizer is clearly explained, and code release would further enhance adoption.

Weaknesses and Suggestions  
1. Theoretical Guarantees of Approximations  
   • Across several sections, the core derivations rely on first-order Taylor expansions and truncated iterative schemes. Yet no error bounds or convergence analyses are provided to quantify how these approximations deviate from true filter solutions (as similarly noted in PairNorm and Taylor-approximation critiques).  
   • Suggestion: Include theoretical or empirical studies of approximation error at various truncation depths, and discuss how this impacts filter fidelity—especially in deeper GNN stacks.  

2. Novelty and Positioning  
   • The unified regularization interpretation echoes prior observations in graph signal processing (e.g., low-pass filtering interpretations in Hoang & Maehara, Zhao & Akoglu) and the original GCN regularization discussion (Kipf et al., 2016).  
   • Suggestion: Clearly delineate how this work extends beyond assembling prior formulations—perhaps by highlighting unique aspects of the taxonomy or by comparing to closely related signal-processing frameworks.  

3. Relation to Classical Label Propagation  
   • In the linear GNN setting, the proposed framework strongly resembles traditional label propagation (Zhou et al., 2004). The paper should articulate the precise differences and advantages of the optimization perspective over established graph-based semi-supervised learning methods.  

4. Comparison to Existing Oversmoothing Remedies  
   • While the feature-variance regularizer is validated against PairNorm and DropEdge, deeper comparisons to residual/Jumper architectures (e.g., Jumping Knowledge, APPNP variants) are missing. It remains unclear whether the proposed penalty offers distinct benefits over architectural solutions.  
   • Suggestion: Empirically benchmark the variance penalty against a broader set of oversmoothing treatments on deeper networks (4–8 layers) to demonstrate relative strengths.  

5. Hyperparameter Sensitivity and Computational Overhead  
   • The regularizer’s strength parameter (α₃) is reported to be sensitive—large values can destabilize training—yet no systematic sensitivity analysis is provided.  
   • The Kronecker-based formulation and iterative inversion incurs extra computation and memory costs, but only a brief runtime profile on Cora is shown. Scalability to larger graphs (e.g., OGB benchmarks) and deeper GNNs is untested.  
   • Suggestion: Provide sensitivity curves for α₃, report runtime/memory overhead on larger datasets, and offer guidelines for choosing hyperparameters in practice.  

6. Limited Task and Dataset Scope  
   • Experiments focus exclusively on node-classification in small to medium graphs. The applicability of the unified framework and variance penalty to graph-level tasks (classification, regression) or heterophilous structures remains unexplored.  
   • Suggestion: Extend empirical evaluation to additional tasks and larger real-world datasets to demonstrate generality and robustness.

7. Consideration of Nonlinear Transformations  
   • The current formulation is tailored to linear GNNs without learnable nonlinear transformation matrices. It is unclear how (or whether) the framework can incorporate non-linearities and parameter matrices common in standard architectures.  
   • Suggestion: Discuss extensions that integrate nonlinear transforms into the regularized optimization view or clarify limitations if such integration is not straightforward.

Decision  
Rating: 5: Marginally below acceptance threshold  

While this paper offers a compelling unified view of convolutional GNNs and a conceptually simple oversmoothing fix, it falls short in theoretical rigor, novelty positioning, scalability analysis, and breadth of empirical validation. Addressing the above weaknesses—particularly providing approximation error bounds, clearer differentiation from prior work, deeper oversmoothing comparisons, and broader experiments—would substantially strengthen the contribution.

reviews.confidence: 3: The reviewer is fairly confident that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
Summary: The paper shows that several graph networks (GCN, attention GCN, PPNP, residual) can be unified under a common framework of Laplacian-regularised optimisation. Subsequently, different types of regularisation are combined to propose a new method for graph transduction, which is then empirically evaluated.

Significance: Laplacian regularisation is a classical approach for formulating/justifying graph transduction algorithms (multiple papers by Mikhail Belkin and Xiaojin Zhu around 2004-06). It is interesting to see that so many graph networks can also be unified in the same framework. A unified framework does aid in both theoretical analysis and implementation of GCNs.
However, the claims and derivation do not seem to account for the non-linear activation in the networks, and hence, significance of the work seems limited.

Quality: As noted above, non-linearity is not considered which makes the derivation significantly simpler. Moreover, the first-order approximation is quite misleading since even the proof do not seem to consider non-linear activation. 
Since the proposed method combines multiple types of regularisation, it is expected to perform better than other networks. However, it is not clear if the training time increases due to the complex regularisation.

Clarity and orginality: The paper is otherwise well written / organised, and the theoretical contributions (although technically straightforward) seem original and somewhat interesting.

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper develops a unified optimization framework showing that a wide family of graph convolutional networks—including vanilla GCNs, attention‐based GAT/AGNN, PPNP, MixHop, residual/concatenation modules, and other spectral filters—can all be viewed as solving variants of a Laplacian‐regularized least‐squares problem in the graph Fourier domain. Building on this insight, the authors propose a novel feature‐variance regularizer to mitigate oversmoothing, and demonstrate consistent 0.5–1.0% accuracy gains on standard node‐classification benchmarks (Cora, Citeseer, Pubmed) and the inductive PPI task.

Strengths  
• Unified Perspective: The paper provides a clear, coherent taxonomy that unifies many popular GNN variants under a single optimization lens. This synthesis deepens conceptual understanding and could guide future module design.  
• Simple, Plug‐in Regularizer: The feature‐variance penalty is conceptually straightforward, parameter‐efficient, and can be applied to diverse architectures without introducing extra learnable parameters.  
• Empirical Gains: Experiments show modest but consistent improvements over strong baselines, including comparisons to DropEdge and PairNorm, under both transductive and inductive settings with random‐split robustness checks.  
• Clarity and Organization: The manuscript is well written, with detailed derivations, ablation studies on regularization strength and iteration order, and clear explanation of the plug‐in nature of the proposed module.

Weaknesses  
• Neglect of Non‐linear Activations: The core derivation ignores non‐linear activation functions (e.g., ReLU), which substantially simplifies the analysis but limits the theoretical justification for standard GCN architectures.  
• Lack of Approximation Error Analysis: The reliance on first‐order Taylor expansions and iterative truncations is not accompanied by theoretical error bounds or convergence guarantees. As a result, it is unclear how closely the approximated filters match true solutions, especially in deeper models.  
• Limited Novelty Relative to Prior GSP Work: While the unified formulation is nicely presented, related graph signal processing literature (e.g., interpretations of GCNs as low‐pass filters) already explores similar Laplacian‐regularized views. The paper’s main novelty, therefore, lies primarily in assembling existing observations rather than introducing fundamentally new theory.  
• Computational Overhead and Hyperparameter Sensitivity: The Kronecker‐based variance regularizer incurs additional runtime and memory costs, but only minimal profiling is provided (e.g., on Cora). The regularization strength hyperparameter (α₃) also appears sensitive, yet no systematic sweep or practical guidelines are offered.  
• Narrow Empirical Scope: All experiments are limited to small citation graphs and the PPI task. It remains unclear how the framework and regularizer scale to larger benchmarks (e.g., OGB), deeper GNNs (4–8 layers), or other tasks such as graph classification and link prediction.  
• Incomplete Comparison to Oversmoothing Remedies: Although DropEdge and PairNorm are included, other contemporary techniques (e.g., Jumping Knowledge, residual depth strategies) are not fully compared or ablated, making it difficult to assess the unique benefits of the feature‐variance penalty.

Actionable Suggestions  
1. Provide theoretical or at least empirical bounds on the approximation error introduced by the Taylor expansions, possibly as a function of iteration depth or network width.  
2. Profile the runtime and memory overhead of the variance regularizer on larger graph datasets (e.g., OGB) and report concrete comparisons to baseline GCNs.  
3. Conduct a more extensive hyperparameter sensitivity analysis for α₃, including visualizing performance vs. regularization strength to guide practitioners.  
4. Extend experiments to deeper architectures (4–8 layers) and to graph‐level tasks (e.g., classification/regression benchmarks) to demonstrate the framework’s scalability and generality.  
5. Compare the proposed feature‐variance regularizer against other oversmoothing countermeasures—such as Jumping Knowledge, residual/skip connections, and advanced normalizations—to quantify relative advantages.  
6. Clarify notation consistency (e.g., Θ vs. Θ^(k), α_k) and consider moving key derivations from the appendix into the main text for readability. Releasing code and hyperparameter configurations would also improve reproducibility.

Decision  
Rating: 5 – Marginally below acceptance threshold  
While the paper offers a neat unifying view and a simple oversmoothing remedy with consistent empirical gains, the theoretical contributions are limited by neglecting non‐linear activations and lacking error analyses. Moreover, the scope of experiments and the assessment of computational trade‐offs are too narrow to fully establish the method’s utility. I therefore recommend a borderline rejection, pending a revision that addresses the theoretical rigor, scalability, and empirical breadth concerns outlined above.

title: Practical Marginalized Importance Sampling with the Successor Representation

reviews.confidence: 3: The reviewer is fairly confident that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
***Summary***
The paper proposes an approach to employ successor representation combined with marginalized importance sampling. The basic idea exploited in the paper consists of expressing the occupancies in terms of the successor representation and to model it via a linear combination of some features. This allows handling, although approximately, continuous state-action spaces. After having derived the objective function, an experimental evaluation on both Mujoco and Atari domains is presented, including an ablation study.

***Major***
- (About the linearity of the weight) Linear representations expressed in terms of a feature function are common in RL as the reward function can be often seen as a trade-off of different objectives encoded in the features. However, the choice of the linear representation in Equation (7) is based on the assumption that the marginalized weight is linear in the feature function. This assumption seems to me less justified compared to the one for the reward function. Clearly, a suitable feature design could overcome this limitation. Can the authors explain how the features \phi are selected or learned? 
- (Experimental evaluation) The results presented in the experimental evaluation are partially unsatisfactory, as also the authors acknowledge. It seems that there is no clear benefit in employing the marginalized importances sampling (both the baselines and the proposed approach) compared to standard deep temporal difference approaches. The authors suggest that this phenomenon can be ascribed to the fact that the quality of the marginalized weights is affected by the successor representation learned. I don't think this is the main weakness of the paper, but a reflection of the usefulness of the method in complex scenarios is necessary. Alternatively, it would be interesting to compare the proposed approach with DualDICE and GradientDICE on simpler tasks (maybe toy ones) in which DualDICE and GradientDICE work well.


***Minor***
- The related work section should be moved later in the paper, maybe after Section 4
- Pag 2, two lines above Equation (2): the transition model is here employed as a distribution over the next state s' and the reward r, but the reward function is considered separately in the definition of MDP presented before
- Figures 2, 3, and 4: the plots are not readable when printing the paper in grayscale. I suggest using different linestyles and/or markers

***Typos***
- Pag 2: isn't -> is not
- Pag 2: doesn't -> does not
- Pag 8: the the -> the

***Overall***
The paper can be considered incremental compared to DualDICE. I did not find any fault, but I feel that the significance of contribution is currently insufficient for publication at ICLR. In particular, for a paper that proposes a practical variation of a theoretically sound algorithm, the experimental evaluation is essential. I think that the results are currently unable to clearly show the advantages of the proposed method.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper introduces SR-DICE, a novel approach to marginalized importance sampling (MIS) for off-policy evaluation (OPE) that leverages the successor representation (SR) of a target policy to sidestep unstable minimax objectives. By expressing the occupancy ratios in terms of a linear combination of successor features and recasting ratio estimation as a convex least-squares problem, SR-DICE decouples dynamics learning from density-ratio estimation. The authors derive clear theoretical connections to existing methods—showing, for instance, that DualDICE can be viewed as a form of Bellman‐residual minimization—and demonstrate SR-DICE on both MuJoCo continuous-control benchmarks and Atari domains, including an ablation study on data size, discount factor, and behavior noise.

Major Strengths  
1. Novel Insight and Theoretical Clarity  
   - Reframing MIS as reward-function optimization over successor features provides fresh intuition and avoids adversarial training.  
   - The case study recasting DualDICE in a Bellman-residual framework is insightful, highlighting why minimax methods can be unstable.  
2. Scalability and Simplicity  
   - After pretraining an encoder and deep SR via standard TD-like methods, the density-ratio estimation reduces to a single convex regression.  
   - The convexity of the final loss sidesteps the need for double-sampling or saddle-point optimization.  
3. Empirical Breadth and Reproducibility  
   - Evaluation spans seven MuJoCo tasks (from Pendulum to Humanoid) and multiple Atari games, with ablations on representation size, behavior policy noise, and data volume.  
   - Code and hyperparameters are provided to facilitate reproducibility.

Major Concerns and Suggestions  
1. Linearity Assumption on Marginalized Weights  
   The core assumption in Equation (7) is that the marginalized density ratio can be expressed as a linear function of features φ(s,a). While linear representations are commonplace for reward functions, this assumption for density ratios is less obviously justified.  
   • Can the authors clarify how the feature map φ is chosen or learned? If φ is pretrained, what criteria guide its design?  
   • It would strengthen the contribution to provide theoretical bounds or at least empirical characterization of how SR approximation error propagates to w(s,a). Prior work on error propagation in SR (e.g., as noted in Alternative Reviews 2 and 3) suggests that small errors in φ or ψ could substantially bias the density-ratio estimate.  
2. Experimental Evaluation and Practical Impact  
   While SR-DICE consistently outperforms DualDICE and GradientDICE in terms of stability and lower MSE, the proposed method still does not surpass direct deep TD or successor-representation–based estimators in OPE error. This raises questions about its practical benefit.  
   • Comparing SR-DICE against a broader set of OPE baselines—such as Fitted Q Evaluation (FQE), doubly-robust estimators, MAGIC, or weighted importance sampling—would more clearly position its advantages and limitations.  
   • The authors might consider simple toy domains or settings where DualDICE and GradientDICE are known to excel, to isolate when SR-DICE’s convex formulation truly pays off.  
   • Empirical studies on hyperparameter sensitivity (learning rates, network architectures, target-network update frequency, φ-loss weighting) and wall-clock runtime comparisons would help assess robustness and computational overhead.  
   • As noted in Alternative Review 3, demonstrating SR-DICE’s utility in a downstream task—such as off-policy policy gradient or imitation learning—could illustrate practical gains of accurate density ratios beyond OPE.  
   • Exploring end-to-end joint training of the successor representation and weight estimator, as suggested in Alternative Review 4, might reduce pretraining overhead and improve overall performance.

Minor Comments  
- The related work section might flow better if moved after Section 4.  
- On page 2, the transition model p(s',r|s,a) and the separate reward function definition could be harmonized for clarity.  
- Figures 2, 3, and 4 become difficult to distinguish when printed in grayscale; consider using varied linestyles or markers.  
- Some notation (e.g., the use of dπ/dD) is not explicitly defined before use; a concise symbol table or more careful definitions would aid readability.

Typographical Corrections  
- Page 2: “isn’t” → “is not”; “doesn’t” → “does not”  
- Page 8: “the the” → “the”  
- Address minor formatting inconsistencies throughout, as noted in Alternative Review 3.

Overall Assessment  
SR-DICE presents a thoughtful, technically sound advance in off-policy ratio estimation by uniting successor representations with MIS. The theoretical derivations are clear and the algorithm is elegant in reducing to convex regression. However, its empirical gains over both existing MIS methods and direct deep TD approaches are mixed, and several aspects—feature selection, error propagation, baseline comparisons, and downstream validation—warrant deeper exploration. Given its incremental but meaningful contributions and the solidity of its analysis, I assign a rating of 6: Marginally above acceptance threshold. Enhancements along the suggested lines would further strengthen the paper’s significance for the community.
reviews.original_text:
Review:
***Summary***
The paper proposes an approach to employ successor representation combined with marginalized importance sampling. The basic idea exploited in the paper consists of expressing the occupancies in terms of the successor representation and to model it via a linear combination of some features. This allows handling, although approximately, continuous state-action spaces. After having derived the objective function, an experimental evaluation on both Mujoco and Atari domains is presented, including an ablation study.

***Major***
- (About the linearity of the weight) Linear representations expressed in terms of a feature function are common in RL as the reward function can be often seen as a trade-off of different objectives encoded in the features. However, the choice of the linear representation in Equation (7) is based on the assumption that the marginalized weight is linear in the feature function. This assumption seems to me less justified compared to the one for the reward function. Clearly, a suitable feature design could overcome this limitation. Can the authors explain how the features \phi are selected or learned? 
- (Experimental evaluation) The results presented in the experimental evaluation are partially unsatisfactory, as also the authors acknowledge. It seems that there is no clear benefit in employing the marginalized importances sampling (both the baselines and the proposed approach) compared to standard deep temporal difference approaches. The authors suggest that this phenomenon can be ascribed to the fact that the quality of the marginalized weights is affected by the successor representation learned. I don't think this is the main weakness of the paper, but a reflection of the usefulness of the method in complex scenarios is necessary. Alternatively, it would be interesting to compare the proposed approach with DualDICE and GradientDICE on simpler tasks (maybe toy ones) in which DualDICE and GradientDICE work well.


***Minor***
- The related work section should be moved later in the paper, maybe after Section 4
- Pag 2, two lines above Equation (2): the transition model is here employed as a distribution over the next state s' and the reward r, but the reward function is considered separately in the definition of MDP presented before
- Figures 2, 3, and 4: the plots are not readable when printing the paper in grayscale. I suggest using different linestyles and/or markers

***Typos***
- Pag 2: isn't -> is not
- Pag 2: doesn't -> does not
- Pag 8: the the -> the

***Overall***
The paper can be considered incremental compared to DualDICE. I did not find any fault, but I feel that the significance of contribution is currently insufficient for publication at ICLR. In particular, for a paper that proposes a practical variation of a theoretically sound algorithm, the experimental evaluation is essential. I think that the results are currently unable to clearly show the advantages of the proposed method.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
