reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
*** Summary ***

The paper investigates emergent gesture-based communication in Embodied Multi-Agent Populations. A noticeable feature of the paper is that it investigates emergent communication in the case of non-uniform distribution of intents and costly communication (i.e. agents are penalized for effort). The authors find that in certain scenarios, these conditions may lead to communication generalization of learned communication strategies to new, previously unseen agents.

*** Relevance, Originality, Novelty *** 

I believe that the paper meets ICLR conference standards when it comes to the importance of the problem considered, the originality of the approach and the novelty of the proposed ideas.

*** Clarity ***

The paper is, in general, clearly written and is a pleasure to read. Figure 4 is deeply confusing, however. As mentioned in the appendix, the actual number of timesteps is equal to 5 and is upsampled during visualization. I believe that it is important to mention it right away (i.e. in the image description).
Otherwise, the reader can easily misunderstand the setup after seeing this picture.

*** Quality ***

The quality of the experimental support is, unfortunately, the weakest part of the contribution.

The authors introduce a number of exciting ideas, all of which make the communication learning setting more realistic. Specifically, the authors are studying communication in an embodied setting, Zipfian intent distribution, and the idea of effort-based action cost.

At the same time, while the ideas sound very promising in theory, in practice, the complications introduced by the embodied setting complicate the interpretation of the obtained results when it comes to the effects of Zipfian intent distribution and effort-based action costs.

In order to mitigate the difficulties, authors propose additional measures: providing explicit feature information to the outside observer (i.e. the observer directly gets the action effort); using "torque pretraining" to make the model more familiar with the reward landscape, dramatically reducing the number of actions.
These measures, in my opinion, are significantly detrimental to the potential impact of the paper. Providing effort information directly they makes the setting less realistic, defeating the purpose of the embodied approach.  Torque curriculum makes the results very specific to the considered setting.

There are other significant limitations of the experimental results that are probably indirectly induced by the embodied setting. Specifically, figure 2 and table 1 report no variability measures for the obtained result, and it's not clear, how many runs were made to obtain these results. Moreover, the agent population considered in these experiments is 10 (mentioned in the appendix). With such a small population, it is not surprising that the observer can simply memorize the individual patterns, and is not pressured to infer the underlying structure.  It seems very natural, therefore, to increase the number of agents in the simulation.
I suspect that these limitations are a consequence of the fact that training in the multidimentional setting is very costly and time-consuming.

Moreover, some of the limitations I describe in the "technical soundness" section could have been controlled for in a simpler setting.

Overall, all things considered, it's not clear what are the insights gained from the embodied approach.

*** Technical soundness ***

There are certain technical problems with the contribution:

Firstly, the justification for why Zipfian intents, together with effort-based penalty should lead to generalization is not rigorous (paragraph 1, section 4.1), and not completely convincing.

One of the sentences in the intuitive justification raises particularly serious concerns. The authors say "Coupled, [Zipfian intents and Energy regularization] incentivize an inverse relationship between energy exertion and intent frequency, assigning minimum energy trajectories to maximally occurring intents.". This assumes that energy levels are "occupied" by a limited number of actions. Why that would be true is entirely unclear to me.

For example, for any action with a certain energy level, there is a mirror action (with all joint torques multiplied by -1) with exactly the same energy consumption. Even if for some reason such specific pair of distinct actions with exactly the same effort 
is impossible in a given setting, given how high the degree of freedom of the system is, it is still easy to imagine that every energy level offers a plethora of actions to choose from.

This raises a question of why then we see good performance in 2-action scenario. There is an alternative explanation that was not explored: there is a unique action with zero effort: doing nothing.
I suspect that it may be the only special case, where energy level is mapped uniquely onto an action (i.e. there is no different action with the same (0) energy penalty).

Another alternative explanation that must be addressed is the exposure bias. Rare intents are sampled less frequently, therefore the model has less time to optimize its actions for that intent. This may explain some of the differences in energy costs between the rare and frequent actions.

Lastly, the architecture choice and problem setting formalization also raise certain concerns. Specifically, the sender only receives joint positions and angles at time t, with no history. This does not seem to be an appropriate state specification. Coupled with the fact that the network is a simple feedforward architecture (no recurrence), this 
limits the number of possible jestures that can be generated.

*** Conclusion ***

The paper addresses a highly relevant problem and proposes a number of interesting ideas. Unfortunately, in my opinion, 
some of the proposed ideas (studying communication in an embodied setting, Zipfian intent distribution, costly actions), when introduced together, are not interacting well and are hindering result interpretation.

As authors mention, using an embodied setting with multidimentional continuous action and observation spaces makes the problem extremely challenging. Therefore, when we see only partial success or no success in learning, it becomes difficult to identify the true source of the problem. That is, it is unclear if we are hitting a fundamental limit of what can be achieved by the combination of the Zipfian intent + costly actions, or is whether it a just a limitation of the architectures/algorithms that the authors used, when those algorithms are applied in a challenging multidimentional setting.

The latter concern is exacerbated by some technical questions. In particular, the choice of a feedforward architecture seems extremely limiting, when combined with the state description that the authors used. The state space for the policy (sender) agent only includes the current joint configuration, without the history. This renders a large number of actions impossible (i.e. the "pendulum-like" wave of a hand becomes impossible, since when the hand returns to the middle after the first half-swing, the policy would act as if the action just started).

Lastly, the logic behind the main hypothesis is not fully theoretically supported, and the alternative explanations are not addressed.

Overall, I believe that the contribution is extremely promising, but it still requires some polishing and potential restructuring in order to be up to the standards of the ICLR conference. I do deeply hope to see this paper improved and published in the future, as I believe that after some improvements, it will be of interest to a large portion of the community.

*** Suggestions ***

I believe that the paper can be made much stronger if the authors take a step back and start with a simplified communication game setting. E.g. instead of actually producing an action trajectory, agents could pick among a number of actions with pre-defined effort levels, or they can separately pick the gesture and the amount of effort to allocate to that gesture (i.e. a subtle hand-wave vs flailing one's arms in the air).
This would allow to explore the benefits and limitations of the proposed Zipfian intent+Costly actions approach. In that case, demonstrating that the approach also works in an emboddied setting would be a beautiful cherry on top. Alternatively, it is possible to "boost" the embodiment part of the paper, 
e.g. by pre-training the agents on some other tasks, thus making certain actions more natural, etc. Currently, it just seems that embodiment itself is not adding additional insights, but complicating the experiments.

Adding noize (mentioned in the appendix) is a very good idea as intuitively, it seems that it is the only reason why the agents don't converge on an array of extremely subtle actions with near-zero effort. I believe that it was not discussed enough in the paper and maybe its importance is somewhat overlooked.

*** Questions *** 

- "Furthermore, with an increase in task complexity to 10 concepts, the external observer never performs better than this baseline strategy" I thought the baseline accuracy was 0.34, so it seems that with curriculum the external observer does outperform the baseline.
- Apart from the zero-effort action, why can't the agents converge on minimally distinguishable actions with the same effort level? 

*** Typos and other minor suggestions ***

"execution decentralized" -> "decentralized execution"
"Agent reward" verbally described as a "function of state and actions taken", but the formula is written as if that it is a function of observation and actions taken.

Table 1 is slightly confusing to read, it may be better to make it broader so that the phrase "Train Input" does not break into two lines.

Conflicting notation: N is used as the number of agents on page 3, but later N refers to the number of concepts. 10 agents - not enough to infer the latent variable, especially if no randomness is added to the agents.

I personally believe that the use of the term "zero-shot" in this setting is not ideal. The observer is trained on a sample of communication protocols and is then tested on a held-out set. 
In my view, it's analogous to "generalization performance", not "zero-shot" performance. I understand that the authors are referring to another study that did use "zero-shot", so this consideration did not affect my score.
Before the name for this problem setting is thoroughly established, it may be possible to use "out of sample coordination" or some other option.

*** Update after rebuttal ***

I have read the rebuttal and I deeply appreciate the detailed response by the authors. I especially appreciate the introduction of a number of experiments on a simple domain that help to illustrate the main point of the paper.

At the same time, I believe that some serious issues still remain unresolved. For example, my main concern remains: I believe that wrapping the problem in the embodied setting is not introducing additional insights. I must clarify that I fully agree that studying how embodiment affects cognition/behavior is an extremely important and exciting area of research. But in the present paper, the models have no chance to benefit from embodiment (since there is no prior / shared embodied experience), but rather have to solve the task despite being placed in an embodied setting.

The main insight (in my opinion) is the observation that Zipfian intent distribution together with energy costs could be a good basis for zero-shot communication. I think that additional experiments that the authors introduced help to strengthen this point, although more experiments could still be beneficial (e.g. systematically varying the population size), as well as a more thorough theoretical discussion. At the same time, the limitations of the main "embodied" experiment remain (most importantly, the fact that fairly high accuracy can be achieved because of the unique "do nothing" action trajectory). 

In short, a large part of the paper (on embodiment) contributes relatively little in terms of its impact and conclusions that can be drawn from it, which necessarily limits the extent to which the main insightful point can be explored. The main point is truly interesting, however, which makes the paper borderline.

Overall, I believe that the paper is extremely promising and I would love to see an expanded version published. I feel very torn about the decision, but at the moment, I believe that the paper is still below the acceptance threshold, although only marginally. I am happy to adjust the score up, and I regret that I can not switch it to an "accept" recommendation.

As a minor aside - the newly introduced Colab Notebook does not fully run and crashes at the cell #4 (model loading), so I can not fully explore the newly introduced experiments. That being said, I think that after fixing, this resource can be very useful in the future. This minor issue did not affect my evaluation.

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
The paper tackles emergent gesture‐based communication in embodied multi‐agent populations, where continuous joint trajectories serve as a signaling channel. Under a nonuniform Zipf intent prior and an energy‐cost penalty, agents learn gestures whose effort correlates inversely with concept frequency. A third‐party observer is then tasked with zero‐shot (ZS) generalization to novel partners. The authors supplement the main experiments with a discrete toy domain, an explicit‐energy‐value intervention, and a torque‐curriculum pretraining.

Strengths and Novelty  
- Extends emergent communication to a realistic continuous, high‐dimensional modality (4-joint robot arm trajectories), which is underexplored in the literature (Alternative Reviews 1–4).  
- Well‐motivated combination of Zipfian intent distributions and energy regularization as common‐knowledge inductive biases, with clear formalization of implicit versus explicit structure (Alternative Review 2).  
- Ablation studies compare uniform vs. Zipf distributions, implicit vs. explicit energy information, and curriculum vs. no curriculum across intent set sizes (2, 5, 10), enhancing our understanding of when ZS generalization arises (Alternative Reviews 1, 3, 4).  
- The discrete analogue experiments effectively illustrate the necessity of tightly coupling effort and intent frequency before scaling up to the embodied setting.

Clarity  
Overall the manuscript is clearly written and structured. Visualizations of learned gestures help interpret results. However, Figure 4’s upsampling of a T=5 timestep trajectory is confusing — this should be noted prominently in the caption, not only the appendix. Table 1 would benefit from showing mean ± std over multiple seeds and avoiding line breaks in “Train Input.” Some conflicting notation (N for agents vs. intents) and minor typos (“execution decentralized”) should be corrected for readability.

Quality of Experimental Support  
While the embodied setting is ambitious, it introduces complications that cloud interpretation:  
- Scale and Population Size: Experiments use only 10 intents and 10 agents. With such a small population, an observer could simply memorize partner‐specific patterns rather than infer a latent effort‐intent structure (Main Review).  
- Limited Morphology and Horizon: A single four‐joint arm operating over T=5 steps (upsampled for visualization) may not capture richer gestural dynamics; the feedforward sender network without state history precludes pendulum‐like wave gestures (Main Review).  
- Modest ZS Accuracy: Peak cross‐play accuracy (~56% for 10 intents) modestly exceeds the 34% baseline and remains brittle under greater vocabulary size (Alternative Reviews 1, 3, 4).  
- Missing Controls and Variance: Results lack variance measures and details on the number of runs, seeds, and hyperparameter sensitivity (Alternative Reviews 1, 2, 3).  

Technical Soundness  
Several core technical and theoretical assumptions require further rigor:  
- Justification of Energy–Zipf Interaction: The claim that a limited set of effort “slots” drives an inverse mapping between intent frequency and energy lacks formal backing. Given continuous symmetry (e.g. mirrored joint‐torque trajectories), it is unclear why energy levels become uniquely occupied (Main Review).  
- Alternative Explanations: Unique zero-effort “do‐nothing” actions and exposure bias (rare intents sampled less) may underlie observed energy differences, rather than principled Zipf+cost dynamics (Main Review, Alternative Review 3).  
- State Representation and Architecture: The sender’s state input (only current joint angles) coupled with a feedforward policy restricts sequential gestural patterns, limiting expressivity and making certain expected dynamics impossible (Main Review).  

Reproducibility and Baselines  
- Key hyperparameters (curriculum schedule, noise magnitude) are buried in the appendix; statistical tests and multiple runs are not reported (Alternative Reviews 1–3).  
- No comparison to established zero-shot coordination methods (e.g. Other-Play), alternative grounding signals (distance travelled, entropy regularization), or symbol‐based baselines in the continuous setting (Alternative Reviews 1–4).

Conclusion and Recommendation  
The paper addresses a highly relevant and novel problem—grounding emergent communication in physically embodied gestures—and offers valuable initial insights through toy‐domain analogues and ablations. However, the joint introduction of embodiment, Zipfian intents, and costly actions without stronger theoretical guarantees or broader empirical validation leaves core questions unresolved. In particular, the embodied experiments are too narrowly scoped (limited agents, intents, morphology, and architecture) to unequivocally support the claimed generalization mechanism. As such, I find the current manuscript to be marginally below the acceptance threshold. I encourage the authors to refine the theoretical justification, expand empirical scope (larger populations, richer morphologies, baseline comparisons, variance reporting), and simplify or isolate components (e.g. toy discrete effort selection) before resubmitting.

Overall Rating: 5 – Marginally below acceptance threshold
reviews.original_text:
Review:
*** Summary ***

The paper investigates emergent gesture-based communication in Embodied Multi-Agent Populations. A noticeable feature of the paper is that it investigates emergent communication in the case of non-uniform distribution of intents and costly communication (i.e. agents are penalized for effort). The authors find that in certain scenarios, these conditions may lead to communication generalization of learned communication strategies to new, previously unseen agents.

*** Relevance, Originality, Novelty *** 

I believe that the paper meets ICLR conference standards when it comes to the importance of the problem considered, the originality of the approach and the novelty of the proposed ideas.

*** Clarity ***

The paper is, in general, clearly written and is a pleasure to read. Figure 4 is deeply confusing, however. As mentioned in the appendix, the actual number of timesteps is equal to 5 and is upsampled during visualization. I believe that it is important to mention it right away (i.e. in the image description).
Otherwise, the reader can easily misunderstand the setup after seeing this picture.

*** Quality ***

The quality of the experimental support is, unfortunately, the weakest part of the contribution.

The authors introduce a number of exciting ideas, all of which make the communication learning setting more realistic. Specifically, the authors are studying communication in an embodied setting, Zipfian intent distribution, and the idea of effort-based action cost.

At the same time, while the ideas sound very promising in theory, in practice, the complications introduced by the embodied setting complicate the interpretation of the obtained results when it comes to the effects of Zipfian intent distribution and effort-based action costs.

In order to mitigate the difficulties, authors propose additional measures: providing explicit feature information to the outside observer (i.e. the observer directly gets the action effort); using "torque pretraining" to make the model more familiar with the reward landscape, dramatically reducing the number of actions.
These measures, in my opinion, are significantly detrimental to the potential impact of the paper. Providing effort information directly they makes the setting less realistic, defeating the purpose of the embodied approach.  Torque curriculum makes the results very specific to the considered setting.

There are other significant limitations of the experimental results that are probably indirectly induced by the embodied setting. Specifically, figure 2 and table 1 report no variability measures for the obtained result, and it's not clear, how many runs were made to obtain these results. Moreover, the agent population considered in these experiments is 10 (mentioned in the appendix). With such a small population, it is not surprising that the observer can simply memorize the individual patterns, and is not pressured to infer the underlying structure.  It seems very natural, therefore, to increase the number of agents in the simulation.
I suspect that these limitations are a consequence of the fact that training in the multidimentional setting is very costly and time-consuming.

Moreover, some of the limitations I describe in the "technical soundness" section could have been controlled for in a simpler setting.

Overall, all things considered, it's not clear what are the insights gained from the embodied approach.

*** Technical soundness ***

There are certain technical problems with the contribution:

Firstly, the justification for why Zipfian intents, together with effort-based penalty should lead to generalization is not rigorous (paragraph 1, section 4.1), and not completely convincing.

One of the sentences in the intuitive justification raises particularly serious concerns. The authors say "Coupled, [Zipfian intents and Energy regularization] incentivize an inverse relationship between energy exertion and intent frequency, assigning minimum energy trajectories to maximally occurring intents.". This assumes that energy levels are "occupied" by a limited number of actions. Why that would be true is entirely unclear to me.

For example, for any action with a certain energy level, there is a mirror action (with all joint torques multiplied by -1) with exactly the same energy consumption. Even if for some reason such specific pair of distinct actions with exactly the same effort 
is impossible in a given setting, given how high the degree of freedom of the system is, it is still easy to imagine that every energy level offers a plethora of actions to choose from.

This raises a question of why then we see good performance in 2-action scenario. There is an alternative explanation that was not explored: there is a unique action with zero effort: doing nothing.
I suspect that it may be the only special case, where energy level is mapped uniquely onto an action (i.e. there is no different action with the same (0) energy penalty).

Another alternative explanation that must be addressed is the exposure bias. Rare intents are sampled less frequently, therefore the model has less time to optimize its actions for that intent. This may explain some of the differences in energy costs between the rare and frequent actions.

Lastly, the architecture choice and problem setting formalization also raise certain concerns. Specifically, the sender only receives joint positions and angles at time t, with no history. This does not seem to be an appropriate state specification. Coupled with the fact that the network is a simple feedforward architecture (no recurrence), this 
limits the number of possible jestures that can be generated.

*** Conclusion ***

The paper addresses a highly relevant problem and proposes a number of interesting ideas. Unfortunately, in my opinion, 
some of the proposed ideas (studying communication in an embodied setting, Zipfian intent distribution, costly actions), when introduced together, are not interacting well and are hindering result interpretation.

As authors mention, using an embodied setting with multidimentional continuous action and observation spaces makes the problem extremely challenging. Therefore, when we see only partial success or no success in learning, it becomes difficult to identify the true source of the problem. That is, it is unclear if we are hitting a fundamental limit of what can be achieved by the combination of the Zipfian intent + costly actions, or is whether it a just a limitation of the architectures/algorithms that the authors used, when those algorithms are applied in a challenging multidimentional setting.

The latter concern is exacerbated by some technical questions. In particular, the choice of a feedforward architecture seems extremely limiting, when combined with the state description that the authors used. The state space for the policy (sender) agent only includes the current joint configuration, without the history. This renders a large number of actions impossible (i.e. the "pendulum-like" wave of a hand becomes impossible, since when the hand returns to the middle after the first half-swing, the policy would act as if the action just started).

Lastly, the logic behind the main hypothesis is not fully theoretically supported, and the alternative explanations are not addressed.

Overall, I believe that the contribution is extremely promising, but it still requires some polishing and potential restructuring in order to be up to the standards of the ICLR conference. I do deeply hope to see this paper improved and published in the future, as I believe that after some improvements, it will be of interest to a large portion of the community.

*** Suggestions ***

I believe that the paper can be made much stronger if the authors take a step back and start with a simplified communication game setting. E.g. instead of actually producing an action trajectory, agents could pick among a number of actions with pre-defined effort levels, or they can separately pick the gesture and the amount of effort to allocate to that gesture (i.e. a subtle hand-wave vs flailing one's arms in the air).
This would allow to explore the benefits and limitations of the proposed Zipfian intent+Costly actions approach. In that case, demonstrating that the approach also works in an emboddied setting would be a beautiful cherry on top. Alternatively, it is possible to "boost" the embodiment part of the paper, 
e.g. by pre-training the agents on some other tasks, thus making certain actions more natural, etc. Currently, it just seems that embodiment itself is not adding additional insights, but complicating the experiments.

Adding noize (mentioned in the appendix) is a very good idea as intuitively, it seems that it is the only reason why the agents don't converge on an array of extremely subtle actions with near-zero effort. I believe that it was not discussed enough in the paper and maybe its importance is somewhat overlooked.

*** Questions *** 

- "Furthermore, with an increase in task complexity to 10 concepts, the external observer never performs better than this baseline strategy" I thought the baseline accuracy was 0.34, so it seems that with curriculum the external observer does outperform the baseline.
- Apart from the zero-effort action, why can't the agents converge on minimally distinguishable actions with the same effort level? 

*** Typos and other minor suggestions ***

"execution decentralized" -> "decentralized execution"
"Agent reward" verbally described as a "function of state and actions taken", but the formula is written as if that it is a function of observation and actions taken.

Table 1 is slightly confusing to read, it may be better to make it broader so that the phrase "Train Input" does not break into two lines.

Conflicting notation: N is used as the number of agents on page 3, but later N refers to the number of concepts. 10 agents - not enough to infer the latent variable, especially if no randomness is added to the agents.

I personally believe that the use of the term "zero-shot" in this setting is not ideal. The observer is trained on a sample of communication protocols and is then tested on a held-out set. 
In my view, it's analogous to "generalization performance", not "zero-shot" performance. I understand that the authors are referring to another study that did use "zero-shot", so this consideration did not affect my score.
Before the name for this problem setting is thoroughly established, it may be possible to use "out of sample coordination" or some other option.

*** Update after rebuttal ***

I have read the rebuttal and I deeply appreciate the detailed response by the authors. I especially appreciate the introduction of a number of experiments on a simple domain that help to illustrate the main point of the paper.

At the same time, I believe that some serious issues still remain unresolved. For example, my main concern remains: I believe that wrapping the problem in the embodied setting is not introducing additional insights. But in the present paper, the models have no chance to benefit from embodiment (since there is no prior / shared embodied experience), but rather have to solve the task despite being placed in an embodied setting.

The main insight (in my opinion) is the observation that Zipfian intent distribution together with energy costs could be a good basis for zero-shot communication. At the same time, the limitations of the main "embodied" experiment remain (most importantly, the fact that fairly high accuracy can be achieved because of the unique "do nothing" action trajectory).

The main point is truly interesting, however, which makes the paper borderline.

Overall, I believe that the paper is extremely promising and I would love to see an expanded version published. I feel very torn about the decision, but at the moment, I believe that the paper is still below the acceptance threshold, although only marginally. I am happy to adjust the score up, and I regret that I can not switch it to an "accept" recommendation.

title: Parameter-Efficient Transfer Learning with Diff Pruning

reviews.rating: 8: Top 50% of accepted papers, clear accept
reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This paper proposes diff pruning, an alternative paradigm for parameter-efficient transfer learning of pre-trained models. Similar to adapters, diff pruning leaves the body of the pre-trained model unchanged. Rather than inserting additional task-specific parameters into the pre-trained model, diff pruning adds reparameterizes the parameters of the transferred model $\theta_\tau$ by adding a diff vector $\delta_\tau$ to them: $\theta_\tau = \theta_{\text{pretrained}} + \delta_\tau$. Parameter efficiency is achieved by regularizing $\theta_\tau$ to be sparse. The authors achieve this by using a relaxed mask vector to approximate the $L_0$ norm. They also propose a way to control for a specific sparsity rate via projection onto the $L_0$ ball after training and to enforce group sparsity that takes the model's structure into account. The approach is evaluated on the GLUE benchmark where it achieves competitive performance to full fine-tuning a BERT Large model and adapters while being more parameter-efficient than both of them.

Pros:
1. The proposed method is intuitive and the different modelling choices are principled and well motivated.
2. The method achieves strong results. It is competitive with full fine-tuning and more parameter-efficient than adapters, the prevalent approach for parameter-efficient transfer learning.
3. The authors show how to effectively control the sparsity rate and incorporating structure via group sparsity brings further gains.
4. The authors conduct extensive analyses, which pre-empted many of my questions, such as the variation across different sparsity masks, sparsity patterns across different tasks, etc. Overall, the analyses shed additional light on the characteristics and preferences of different tasks in transfer learning.

Cons:
1. The approach is potentially more complicated than the baseline, so it is important that the authors open-source their code.
2. The diff vector is distributed over the entire set of parameters of the model rather than focused in a few layers. This makes it potentially harder to combine the diff vectors from different tasks as can be done with adapters (see e.g. https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.00247) and to compose multiple diff vectors. 

Questions:
1. Does a visualization of diff vectors of different tasks (such as using t-SNE) reveal any interesting patterns?
2. Are there any transfer settings that the addition of task-specific parameters can model but inserting layer-specific transformations via adapters cannot (or vice versa)? Adapters have been used to transfer across modalities such as languages (see e.g. Pfeiffer et al. (2020), https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.00052) and I am wondering whether the same would be possible by adding task-specific parameters.
3. How long does your approach take to converge in comparison to the baselines? How much longer do you need to fine-tune with non-zero masks for magnitude pruning for sparsity control? What is the performance benefit of this further fine-tuning?

Rating:
8: Top 50% of accepted papers, clear accept
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper introduces “diff pruning,” a parameter-efficient transfer learning approach for pre-trained models whereby task adaptation is achieved by learning a sparse “diff” vector that is added to frozen pretrained parameters. Unlike adapter-based methods that insert additional layers, diff pruning reparameterizes the fine-tuned weights θτ as θpretrained + δτ and enforces sparsity on δτ via a relaxed L₀-norm regularizer, followed by an L₀-ball projection to guarantee an exact sparsity budget. The authors further extend this with a structured variant that groups parameters according to the model’s architecture. Evaluated on the GLUE benchmark (and additionally on SQuAD in some experiments), diff pruning matches or exceeds full fine-tuning and adapter baselines while modifying only 0.5–1% of parameters per task—substantially fewer than the 3–8% required by adapters.

Strengths  
• Conceptual elegance and solid foundations—the formulation unifies sparse L₀ regularization techniques with parameter-efficient transfer learning in a principled way.  
• Strong empirical performance—comprehensive experiments on GLUE (and SQuAD) show that diff pruning consistently rivals or outperforms both full fine-tuning and adapter-based methods at dramatically lower parameter budgets.  
• Effective control of sparsity—the relaxed mask and post-training projection ensure exact sparsity rates, and the structured variant yields even better sparsity/performance trade-offs with interpretable layer-wise adaptation patterns.  
• Extensive analyses—the authors investigate layer-wise and task-specific sparsity patterns, variation across mask initializations, and the impact of structured versus unstructured masks, providing deep insights into where and how adaptation occurs.  
• Clarity and reproducibility—the technical derivations and hyperparameter settings (e.g., λ, hard-concrete bounds) are described in detail, and the paper anticipates many natural questions about convergence behavior and sparsity control.

Weaknesses and Areas for Improvement  
• Training overhead and efficiency metrics—the optimization of mask parameters incurs a 1.5×–2× increase in memory and runtime compared to standard fine-tuning. A detailed runtime and GPU memory breakdown versus both full fine-tuning and adapters, as well as inference latency measurements, would help practitioners assess trade-offs.  
• Hyperparameter sensitivity—the method relies on several key hyperparameters (λ, stretching bounds l and r, initialization of α and weight magnitudes). An ablation study over these choices and an analysis of variability across random seeds would strengthen claims of robustness.  
• Limited baselines—recent parameter-efficient methods such as prefix-tuning, LoRA, BitFit, and multilingual or multi-domain adapters (e.g., MAD-X) are not compared. Including these baselines or at least discussing their distinctions would situate diff pruning more clearly within the broader PEFT landscape.  
• Scope of evaluation—the experiments are confined to English GLUE (and SQuAD) using BERTLarge. Additional tests on other architectures (e.g., RoBERTa, T5), modalities (vision Transformers), generative tasks, or true multi-task/continual learning settings would demonstrate the generality and amortized storage benefits claimed.  
• Composition of diff vectors—because the diff is distributed across all parameters rather than focused in a few layers, it may be harder to compose or merge task adapters (as has been done with adapters in prior work). A discussion or empirical study (e.g., visualizing diff vectors via t-SNE to reveal clustering by task) could elucidate this point.  
• Reproducibility and code release—the approach is nontrivial to implement from scratch. Open-sourcing the code, pretrained diff masks, and data preprocessing pipelines is essential to facilitate adoption.  
• Presentation polish—minor formatting inconsistencies (e.g., misplaced footnotes, reference numbering) should be addressed in the final version.

Actionable Suggestions  
1. Provide comprehensive runtime, memory, and latency benchmarks comparing diff pruning to full fine-tuning and adapter methods during both training and inference.  
2. Conduct ablations on λ, hard-concrete stretch bounds, and mask initialization to assess hyperparameter sensitivity, and report performance variance across multiple random seeds.  
3. Compare against additional PEFT baselines such as prefix-tuning, LoRA, and BitFit, and discuss trade-offs in parameter count, performance, and composability.  
4. Extend experiments to other model families (e.g., RoBERTa, T5), modalities (vision transformers), and generative or multilingual tasks to demonstrate broader applicability.  
5. Empirically evaluate multi-task or continual learning settings to validate the amortized storage and composition claims, potentially including visualizations (e.g., t-SNE) of diff vectors across tasks.  
6. Release the full codebase, pretrained diff masks, and detailed training scripts to ensure reproducibility.  
7. Proofread the manuscript to correct formatting artifacts, footnote placements, and reference numbering.

Recommendation  
I recommend accepting this paper (Rating: 8: Top 50% of accepted papers). Diff pruning represents a significant advance in ultra-lightweight task adaptation, combining theoretical elegance with strong empirical results. The approach opens up promising directions for sparse transfer learning, and with the suggested additional analyses and broader baselines, it will serve as a valuable foundation for future work in parameter-efficient fine-tuning.

reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
The authors present an interesting approach to learn task-specific models with only a few tunable parameters. They propose learning a diff vector with a sparsity constraint and then pruning the vector using magnitude pruning. They also impose a structured sparsity constraint by introducing a group penalty.

This work is interesting and important with the growing size of the pretrained models. It enables the model to learn a new task with relatively few parameters would be very beneficial. The experiments and analysis conducted by the authors is thorough.

A few questions/thoughts which could improve the paper: 
* It would be good to list the epochs for training for each of the different approaches? Authors mention 2,3,4,5 epochs for training but would good to highlight the cost or savings of fine tuning with their approach.
* In table 1, it would be also helpful to include some information about the finetuning steps/sec with the different approaches. This would help understand the tradeoff of memory vs compute for the proposed approach.
* What’s the intuition for structured pruning performing better than non unstructured? Typically, enforcing some structure should have worse accuracy. If the structure seems to help, then the model should be able to learn structure without the group constraint as well.

Overall, I recommend accepting the paper. As the size of pre-trained models is growing quite rapidly, research that investigates parameter sharing and adapting a pretrained model to a new task with few parameters is essential.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review Summary  
The paper presents “diff pruning,” a parameter- and compute-efficient fine-tuning method that represents task adaptation as a sparse delta vector added on top of frozen pretrained weights. Using a differentiable L₀-norm relaxation (Hard Concrete) to learn both the mask and weight values, followed by magnitude-based L₀-ball projection, the approach sparsely adapts only 0.5–1% of BERT_LARGE parameters on GLUE and SQuAD, matching or exceeding full fine-tuning and adapter baselines. A structured variant further groups parameters for improved sparsity–performance trade-offs and yields interpretable layer-wise adaptation patterns. Overall, this line of work is timely given the rapid growth of pretrained model sizes and the practical need for lightweight task-specific adapters. I recommend acceptance (Rating: 6: Marginally above acceptance threshold).  

Strengths  
1. Novel, principled formulation  
   - Recasting fine-tuning as learning a sparse additive “diff” vector unifies parameter-efficient transfer with L₀ regularization techniques (Alternative Reviews 1 & 2).  
   - The combination of Hard Concrete relaxation and post-hoc magnitude pruning is conceptually elegant and practically effective.  

2. Strong empirical performance  
   - On BERT_LARGE, diff pruning at 0.5–1% parameter budgets matches or surpasses full fine-tuning and prominent adapter methods across 9 GLUE tasks and SQuAD v1.1 (Alternative Reviews 1, 2 & 3).  
   - The structured sparsity variant consistently outperforms the unstructured mask and yields clear per-layer and per-task sparsity insights (Alternative Reviews 1 & 3).  

3. Thorough analysis and clarity  
   - The paper includes layer-wise sparsity patterns, ablations over different sparsity budgets, and a comparison to nonadaptive pruning baselines (Alternative Reviews 2 & 3).  
   - Mathematical derivations, training hyperparameters, and group-penalty details are well documented, supporting reproducibility (Alternative Review 1).  

Weaknesses and Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Efficiency and cost reporting  
   - While the authors note a 1.5–2× training overhead for learning mask parameters, concrete benchmarks are absent. Please report fine-tuning steps/sec, GPU memory usage, and end-to-end runtime comparisons versus full fine-tuning and adapter baselines (Alternative Reviews 1, 2 & 3). Including these numbers in Table 1 would clarify the trade-off between memory and compute.  

2. Hyperparameter sensitivity and robustness  
   - The chosen regularization weight λ, Hard Concrete stretch bounds (l, r), and mask initialization strategy critically influence performance. An ablation study over these hyperparameters and seed variability would strengthen the claim that diff pruning is robust across tasks (Alternative Reviews 1, 2 & 4).  

3. Baseline comparisons  
   - The evaluation omits several recent parameter-efficient tuning methods such as prefix-tuning, LoRA, and BitFit. Adding these baselines or thoroughly discussing their relative merits would position diff pruning more clearly within the PETL landscape (Alternative Reviews 2 & 4).  

4. Scope of evaluation  
   - Experiments are limited to English NLP tasks on BERT_LARGE. Extending to other architectures (e.g., RoBERTa, T5) or domains (vision Transformers, multilingual benchmarks) would demonstrate broader applicability and validate claims around amortized storage for streaming tasks (Alternative Reviews 2 & 4).  

5. Intuition for structured pruning  
   - The paper observes that structured diff pruning sometimes outperforms unstructured masks, which is counter-intuitive. A deeper discussion or diagnostic experiment showing why grouping helps—rather than assuming grouping imposes unnecessary constraints—would clarify this point (Main Review).  

6. Presentation and reproducibility  
   - A few formatting inconsistencies (e.g., stray “(cid: …)” artifacts, footnote placements) distract from the narrative (Alternative Review 3).  
   - Public release of code, exact seed settings, and data preprocessing scripts would greatly facilitate adoption and reproducibility (Alternative Reviews 1 & 2).  

Final Recommendation  
This work addresses a critical challenge in scaling pretrained models to multiple tasks by offering a parameter-light, conceptually elegant solution. Despite minor gaps in efficiency benchmarking, hyperparameter robustness, and breadth of baselines, the methodological contributions and strong empirical results merit acceptance.

reviews.rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This work studies the problem of parameter-efficient transfer learning in the paradigm of pretraining/finetuning. The proposed method, diff pruning, can match the performance of fully finetuned baselines on the GLUE benchmark while only modifying 0.5 of the pretrained model's parameters per task.

I don't get the problem definition of this work. In other words, the authors need to better motivate and justify parameter efficiency. Given that all the parameters of a big model are used in downstream tasks, what are the benefits of only modifying a few parameters? Here are several possibilities.
1. Does it speed up the finetuning process by modifying a few parameters? 
2. Does it speed up inference for downstream tasks?
3. Another possibility is model size reduction, which leads to reduced storage costs. "This approach can become parameter-efficient as the number of tasks increases as it only requires storing the nonzero positions and weights of the diff vector for each task. The cost of storing the shared pretrained model remains constant and is amortized across multiple tasks." Unfortunately, in real-world scenarios, inference of  large models like BERT_base/large is usually conducted on servers or in cloud, where storage is not a big issue while latency (speed) is critical. Inference in edge devices like phones, IoT sensors cares about storage, speed, and power consumption.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents “diff pruning,” a parameter‐efficient fine‐tuning technique that represents task adaptation as a sparse additive “diff” vector applied to frozen pretrained weights. By employing a differentiable L₀ relaxation (the hard‐concrete distribution) to learn both the mask and values of the diff, followed by magnitude‐based L₀‐ball projection and an optional structured grouping variant, the authors demonstrate that only 0.5–1% of BERT_LARGE parameters need to be modified per task. Empirical results on GLUE and SQuAD v1.1 show that diff pruning matches or slightly exceeds fully fine‐tuned and adapter‐based baselines at a fraction of the parameter cost.

Strengths  
• Novel formulation: Recasting fine‐tuning as learning a sparse delta vector unifies parameter‐efficient transfer with established L₀‐regularization techniques. This conceptual simplicity and elegance were noted across reviews.  
• Strong empirical performance: On GLUE and SQuAD, diff pruning matches full fine‐tuning (and in some cases adapters) with only 0.5–1% additional parameters. The structured sparsity variant further tightens the trade‐off between sparsity and accuracy.  
• Detailed technical exposition: The paper provides a clear derivation of the hard‐concrete relaxation, the L₀‐ball projection algorithm, and the option for grouping masks, facilitating reproducibility.  
• Insightful analysis: Layer‐wise sparsity patterns and task‐specific distributions offer valuable interpretability of where and how the model adapts.  

Weaknesses and Major Concerns  
1. Unclear Motivation for Parameter Efficiency  
   The central claim—that modifying fewer parameters per task yields practical benefits—remains under‐motivated. Possible advantages include faster fine‐tuning, reduced inference latency, and lower storage costs when scaling to many tasks. However, the manuscript does not measure or demonstrate any of these. In realistic deployments (e.g., server or cloud inference), storage is often abundant, and latency—rather than model size—is critical; on edge devices, power consumption and speed become paramount. Concrete benchmarks comparing:  
   • Fine‐tuning time versus full fine‐tuning and adapter methods (current reports of 1.5×–2× slower training lack granularity).  
   • Inference latency and memory footprint on representative hardware (CPU, mobile GPU, or TPU).  
   • Storage savings in a true multi‐task or continual learning setup to validate the amortized cost argument.

2. Limited Baseline Coverage  
   While adapters (Houlsby et al.) are included, recent parameter‐efficient tuning methods such as prefix‐tuning, LoRA, BitFit, MAD-X, and bit‐level distillation are absent. Including at least one of these strong baselines—or, if infeasible, a detailed discussion of their relative trade‐offs—would better position diff pruning within the rapidly evolving PETL landscape.

3. Hyperparameter Sensitivity and Robustness  
   The method relies on several critical hyperparameters (λ for sparsity strength, hard‐concrete stretch bounds l/r, mask initialization α, projection schedules). The paper does not provide ablations to show how sensitive performance is to these settings or to random seed variation. A systematic study of λ and the hard‐concrete bounds, as well as variance across seeds, would help establish robustness and guide users.

4. Scope of Evaluation  
   Experiments are confined to English BERT_LARGE on GLUE and SQuAD. To claim general applicability, the method should be tested—at least in preliminary form—on:  
   • Another language or multilingual benchmark (e.g., XNLI).  
   • A different architecture (RoBERTa, T5) or modality (vision Transformer).  
   • A true multi‐task or streaming tasks scenario to demonstrate the claimed storage and continual learning benefits.

5. Reporting and Reproducibility  
   • Training overhead: Although noted qualitatively (1.5×–2× slower, higher memory usage), precise training/memory benchmarks are missing. A breakdown of GPU hours, peak memory, and wall‐clock time per epoch would clarify the trade‐offs.  
   • Code and artifacts: Release of code, precomputed diff masks, seed settings, and exact preprocessing pipelines is crucial for adoption and verification.

Minor Issues  
• Formatting artifacts (e.g., misplaced footnotes, reference numbering) should be cleaned up.  
• Discussion of limitations and potential extensions (e.g., pretraining with diff‐aware objectives) would strengthen the narrative.

Recommendation  
Rating: 4 (Ok but not good enough – rejection)  
Although diff pruning is a well‐engineered and promising approach to ultra‐lightweight task adaptation, the manuscript falls short in demonstrating why its parameter efficiency matters in practice. Without concrete evidence of improved fine‐tuning speed, inference latency, or storage benefits in realistic multi‐task or edge settings—and lacking comparisons to the latest PETL methods—it is premature to accept the paper in its current form. The authors are encouraged to address these deficiencies and resubmit.

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This work combines model pruning with transfer learning/multi-task learning in NLP. Instead of finetuning pretrained models on each individual task, the author propose to learn 'residual' parameters with sparse masks for each task independently, hence reducing per-task parameter requirements. The evaluation on GLUE shows some promising results with better efficiency compared to adapter networks. However, I am concerned with the motivation and applicability of the proposed method, such that I am left with the impression that the method may be hard to use in practice.

Pros:
1. To the best of my knowledge, combining pruning on pretrained models to transfer learning in NLP is novel.
2. Empirical results show the method can performance comparably with adapter networks but with fewer parameters.

Cons (see below for detailed questions):
1. Although the method is proposed for 'multi-task' learning, it is not really evaluated on multi-task settings. The motivation and applicability are not very clear to me.
2. Compared to adapter networks, the proposed method contains more hyper-parameters and is harder to tune in practice.

Questions:
1. In section 2, the author introduced multi-task learning as the background. However, the proposed method is not really designed for multi-task learning (training multiple tasks simultaneously), but rather for transfer learning on multiple tasks independently. In practice, however, training some tasks together can improve performance (GLUE for example), but the proposed method is trained for each task independently and thus there is no positive transfer between tasks. So have you evaluated your method when trained multiple tasks simultaneously (where the shared parameters are jointly finetuned)? How does that work? This is an important extension that might be useful for the community.
2. Please point it out if I miss this information, but how did you select your hyper-parameters for your approach? Besides, how sensitive they are compared to adapter networks?
3. Prior work [1] has shown the resulting mask of pruning can be compared to evaluate task similarities. Did you have similar observations in your experiments?

Missing reference:
The idea of adding 'residual' parameters for new tasks is not new in lifelong learning. It is good to mention [2] and [3]. [1] is also related of comparing pruning masks for pretrained models.

Typo:
Page 4, last line: QQC -> QQP

[1] On negative interference in multilingual models: findings and a meta-learning treatment. Wang et al., EMNLP 2020.

[2] Progressive neural networks. Rusu et al., arxiv 2016

[3] BatchEnsemble: an alternative approach to efficient ensemble and lifelong learning. Wen et al., ICLR 2020.

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper proposes “diff pruning,” a novel parameter-efficient fine-tuning technique that combines structured pruning with transfer learning in NLP. Rather than fully fine-tuning all weights, the method learns sparse “residual” (or “diff”) vectors and binary masks on top of frozen pretrained parameters for each task independently. By leveraging a differentiable L₀ norm relaxation (Hard-Concrete) and a post hoc magnitude pruning (L₀-ball projection), the approach aims to minimize per-task storage while matching or exceeding the performance of adapter networks. Experiments on GLUE and SQuAD v1.1 demonstrate that sparingly updating just 0.5–1% of parameters per task can achieve comparable accuracy to full fine-tuning or adapter methods that use 3–8% extra parameters.

Strengths  
1. Novel Formulation: The reframing of fine-tuning as learning a sparse additive diff vector on frozen weights is both elegant and rooted in contemporary L₀ regularization techniques. To the best of our knowledge, combining pruning with transfer learning in this way is novel.  
2. Empirical Efficacy: On GLUE and SQuAD, diff pruning matches or outperforms full fine-tuning and existing adapter baselines, while reducing per-task parameter budgets by an order of magnitude. The structured mask variant further improves the sparsity–performance tradeoff.  
3. Interpretability: Layer-wise and group-wise sparsity patterns reveal intuitive adaptation behaviors, suggesting that the method yields meaningful insights into where task-specific adaptations occur.  
4. Clarity of Technical Description: The paper provides a detailed derivation of the L₀ relaxation, mask learning procedure, and the post-training pruning step, facilitating reproducibility.

Weaknesses  
1. Limited Multi-Task Evaluation & Motivation Gap  
   – Although multi-task learning is discussed in the background, all experiments train tasks independently. This leaves open whether the shared parameters and sparse diffs would enable positive transfer when tasks are trained jointly. Prior work (e.g., Progressive Neural Networks, BatchEnsemble) suggests that streaming or continual settings can benefit from shared representations. An empirical study of simultaneous multi-task or continual fine-tuning would strengthen the applicability claims.  
2. Hyperparameter Sensitivity & Overhead  
   – The method introduces several new hyperparameters (e.g., λ, Hard-Concrete stretch bounds, mask initialization). While strong results are reported, no ablation is provided on these settings or on random seed variability. Alternative reviews note that choices of λ, l, r, and α initialization are critical yet under-explored. Additionally, mask learning incurs a 1.5×–2× increase in training time and memory compared to adapters, but concrete runtime/memory benchmarks and mitigation strategies are missing.  
3. Incomplete Baseline Comparisons  
   – Recent parameter-efficient tuning approaches—such as prefix-tuning, LoRA, BitFit, and multilingual adapter variants (e.g., MAD-X)—are not compared. Including these baselines or at least discussing their trade-offs would better position diff pruning relative to the current state of the art.  
4. Scope & Generalization  
   – Experiments are confined to English BERT_LARGE on classification and extractive QA. Evaluating on other architectures (e.g., RoBERTa, T5), generative tasks, multilingual benchmarks, or even vision Transformers would demonstrate broader impact.  
5. Missing References & Presentation Issues  
   – The lifelong learning literature on residual adapters (e.g., Rusu et al., 2016; Wen et al., 2020) should be cited to contextualize the idea of adding task-specific modules. The typo “QQC → QQP” and formatting artifacts (e.g., misplaced footnotes or “(cid:…)” tokens) need correction to improve readability.

Questions & Suggestions  
1. Multi-Task & Continual Learning: Have you tried joint training of multiple GLUE tasks with shared frozen parameters and separate masks? How does performance compare to independent training?  
2. Hyperparameter Ablations: Please report the sensitivity of final accuracy to variations in λ, stretching bounds for Hard-Concrete, mask density targets, and random seeds.  
3. Runtime/Memory Profiling: Provide quantitative comparisons of training time, GPU memory usage, and inference latency between diff pruning, full fine-tuning, and adapter methods.  
4. Mask Analysis for Task Similarity: Prior work has used pruning masks to infer task relatedness. Did you observe any clustering or similarity patterns among learned masks across GLUE tasks?  
5. Baseline Extensions: If space permits, include additional comparisons to LoRA, prefix-tuning, and BitFit, or otherwise discuss their relative advantages and limitations.

Minor Comments  
– Correct the reference “[1] On negative interference…” and add [2] Progressive Neural Networks and [3] BatchEnsemble.  
– Fix the QQC typo on page 4.  
– Clean up formatting artifacts and ensure consistent numbering of equations and footnotes.  
– Consider releasing code, pretrained diff masks, and exact preprocessing scripts to facilitate adoption.

Decision  
Rating: 5: Marginally below acceptance threshold  
While diff pruning presents an innovative and promising approach to parameter-efficient transfer learning, key gaps in multi-task evaluation, hyperparameter robustness, baseline coverage, and efficiency analysis prevent a full endorsement at this time. Addressing these issues would substantially strengthen the paper’s practical impact and clarity.

title: Sparse Linear Networks with a Fixed Butterfly Structure: Theory and Practice

reviews.confidence: 5: The reviewer is absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct and very familiar with the relevant literature
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
The paper provides an interesting and novel use of butterfly factoziations in encoder-decoder networks. Specifically, the paper proposes replacing the 
encoder with a truncated butterfly network followed by a dense linear layer. The parameters are chosen so as to keep the number of weights in the (replaced) encoder near linear in the input dimension. The authors provide a theoretical result related to auto-encoder optimization.

######

I vote for accepting the paper. The main reason is for my vote is that the main idea introduced is novel and the algorithmic contribution is substantial. 

######

pros 
+ The proposed truncated butterfly network is novel. Aside from the algorithmic contribution, the theorem in the paper raise important questions about
the optimization landscape of butterfly networks. 
+ The paper is clearly written and well justified 
+ Exhaustive literature survey and background on relevant work in both the matrix factorization and neural networks front

######

cons 
- The beginning of section 7 needs to be expanded with more details on the original Indyk et al 2019 paper -- the authors mention that they use the same setting as 
the 2019 paper but the details on how Indyk et al train their network are lacking without looking up the original paper. 

- I think that low matrix approximation experimentation (Sec 7) can be more thorough. Why are only three datasets (in Table 3) used? Additionally and more importantly, Table 4 shows the approximation results for low values of k (max of 30) -- what happens when k=(min(n,d))? Also, the error is measured with respect to the best rank k approximation of X (the eq. following eq. 20) While this way of measuring the error is often used in the low rank matrix approximation literature it is not very informative of the actual approximation quality of \tilde X. It could well be the case that \tilde X = X_k but the error ||X-\tilde X||_F/||X||_F might  be poor hence this way of measuring the approximation performance is a better indicator of how well X is actually approximated in practice.   

######

questions: see the above section

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper introduces a novel replacement for dense linear encoders in encoder–decoder networks: a truncated butterfly transform (inspired by the Fast Johnson–Lindenstrauss Transform) followed by a small dense core. The key insight is that by choosing the butterfly parameters appropriately, the overall encoder’s parameter count can be kept near O(n log n) rather than O(n²). Beyond this algorithmic contribution, the authors prove a landscape result (Theorem 1) for linear auto-encoder optimization with a fixed butterfly encoder. Empirically, they validate the approach on vision tasks (CIFAR-10, ImageNet), NLP benchmarks (CoNLL-03, PTB), and synthetic/real low-rank matrix approximation datasets, demonstrating substantial parameter reduction, faster convergence, and competitive or superior accuracy relative to standard fully-connected layers and various sketching baselines.  

Recommendation  
I vote to accept (Rating 7: Good paper, accept). The combination of a fresh structured-transform design, solid theoretical backing, and broad empirical validation makes this a strong contribution.  

Pros  
1. Novelty and Theoretical Insight  
   • The truncated butterfly architecture is innovative, extending classical FJLT ideas to learned network layers.  
   • Theorem 1 offers a rigorous characterization of critical points in the linear encoder–decoder setting, addressing optimization landscape concerns and raising important questions about deeper models.  
2. Empirical Breadth  
   • Vision (EfficientNet, ResNet variants) and NLP (Flair tagger) experiments show O(n log n) parameter counts with negligible or positive impact on accuracy and training speed.  
   • Supervised sketching results (Section 7) convincingly outperform both random FJLT and learned sparse sketches on low-rank approximation tasks.  
3. Clarity and Context  
   • The paper is clearly written with an exhaustive literature survey spanning matrix factorization, FJLT, and structured neural network layers.  
   • Practical motivations—memory savings, inference speed, potential energy reduction—are well articulated.  

Cons and Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Theoretical Scope and Assumptions  
   • The current analysis is limited to single-layer linear encoder–decoder models. It remains unclear how multiple stacked butterfly blocks or nonlinear activations affect critical-point behavior. Please discuss—or empirically probe—the optimization stability when replacing several layers and the extra logarithmic depth overhead.  
   • Proposition 1 relies on strong assumptions (distinct eigenvalues, invertibility of BXXᵀBᵀ). A discussion of their practical validity or relaxation would strengthen the theoretical claims.  

2. Ablation Studies  
   • Hyperparameter sensitivity is underexplored. The choice k = log n is heuristic; an ablation varying (k₁, k₂) and butterfly depth would clarify the accuracy/parameter/speed trade-off.  
   • Only one dense layer is replaced in most experiments. Include ablations where multiple fully-connected (or even convolutional) layers are swapped out to demonstrate generality.  
   • The role of FJLT-style initialization versus standard random initialization is not isolated; controlled experiments here would guide practitioners.  

3. Experimental Details and Comparisons  
   • Section 7 briefly cites Indyk et al. (2019) settings but omits training details; please expand this discussion so readers need not cross-reference the original paper.  
   • Low-rank approximation experiments use only three datasets and k up to 30. Extending to k = min(n,d) and additional benchmarks would paint a fuller picture of approximation quality. Also, consider reporting absolute error measures or alternative metrics, since normalized Frobenius error can mask poor approximations in practice.  
   • Comparisons to other compression strategies (e.g., network pruning, tensor decompositions, low-displacement-rank or Toeplitz-like structured transforms) are limited. Including these baselines would contextualize the practical benefits of butterfly factorizations.  

4. Reproducibility and Implementation  
   • Detailed hyperparameters, training schedules, and random seeds should be reported, and open-sourcing the code would greatly enhance reproducibility.  
   • While parameter counts and convergence speed are promising, wall-clock latency and memory benchmarks on modern hardware (including any overhead of sparse butterfly operations) are needed to demonstrate real-world applicability.  

Overall, this work presents a compelling blend of theory and practice: a structured, parameter-efficient transform backed by rigorous analysis and validated across diverse tasks. Addressing the above points would only strengthen an already substantive contribution.

reviews.confidence: 3: The reviewer is fairly confident that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This paper proposes to impose a particular sparsity structure (butterfly network) to replace dense connected layers in deep neural networks. It is motivated by the theoretical results involving the Fast Johnson Lindenstrauss Transform (FJLT). The work is well motivated and experimental results validate the theoretical findings. However, it is not clear the advantage for the case of image datasets as CIFAR10 and CIFAR100. I have the following comments and questions that should be clarified to evaluate the relevance of the results:
-	When comparing with other architectures on image classification tasks (CIFAR10 and CIFAR 100), what is the dense layer that is replaced by the butterfly structure? Most very well-known architectures are based on large concatenation of convolutional layers with a dense layer at the end. Is this last layer the one that is replaced? What is the compression attained by this replacement? 
-	In this architecture, the sparsity pattern is fixed, and it seems to work very well for the dataset used in the paper. It would be interesting to provide some insights on why this particularly structure works well on natural data. Is there any property on datasets that makes butterfly pattern optimal?
-	For natural images, it is well known that sparsity structure imposed by convolutional layers is optimal because of local structure of natural images. I think a comparison replacing a convolutional layer by a butterfly layer could bring some useful insights.
-	Theorem 1: What is Omega in the exponent? I couldn’t find its definition.
-	Figure 1(a): The small difference between normal and BF models seems not to be statistically significant. It would be good to show some variability of results (error bars).
-	 For the results of Figure 1(b), it would be also useful to report the training time reduction and model compression attained by the normal and BF models.
-	Figure 1(b): The starting point for the four methods should be the same. Please include in the plot the Test Accuracy at the beginning (epoch 0).
-	Figure 1(b): Please extend the plots beyond epoch 20 to see how the values are stabilized for the four methods.
-	Quality of Figures, for example Fig 3, must be improved (too small fonts, blurred, etc.)

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper proposes to replace standard fully-connected layers in deep neural networks with a fixed sparse “butterfly” structure motivated by the Fast Johnson–Lindenstrauss Transform (FJLT). The authors provide a theoretical grounding via a landscape analysis for linear encoder–decoder networks (Theorem 1) and demonstrate empirical benefits—substantial parameter savings (O(n log n) vs. O(n²)), faster convergence, and competitive or improved accuracy—across vision benchmarks (CIFAR-10/100, ImageNet variants), NLP tagging tasks, synthetic autoencoders, and supervised sketching experiments. Overall, the work is well motivated, and the decision is to accept marginally above the threshold (rating 6), subject to addressing the points below.

Strengths  
• Clear theoretical motivation: The connection to FJLT and the proof that local minima remain global in the linear encoder–decoder setting provide valuable insight into optimization behavior.  
• Broad empirical evaluation: Results on CIFAR-10/100, ResNet/EfficientNet backbones, CoNLL-03/Flair taggers, synthetic low-rank data, and supervised sketching convincingly show parameter reduction, wall-clock speedups, and matching or superior accuracy. In particular, the learned butterfly sketch outperforms both random FJLT and learned sparse sketches in low-rank approximation.  
• Practical impact: By reducing model size and potentially energy consumption, the method has strong implications for resource‐constrained deployment. The reported faster convergence hints at real‐world training time benefits.

Weaknesses and Points for Clarification  
1. Clarity on layer replacement and compression ratio  
   – Which dense layer(s) in standard architectures (e.g., the final classification head vs. intermediate layers) are replaced, and what exact parameter compression does this yield?  
   – For convolutional‐heavy backbones, would replacing a convolutional block with a butterfly transform offer further insight into the structure’s suitability for natural images?  
2. Theoretical scope and assumptions  
   – Theorem 1 invokes an Ω-notation in the exponent whose definition is not provided; please clarify.  
   – The landscape analysis is restricted to linear encoder–decoders under strong assumptions (distinct eigenvalues, invertibility of B X Xᵀ Bᵀ). Discuss how these translate to nonlinear networks with activations.  
3. Ablation and sensitivity studies  
   – The choice of intermediate rank (k or (k₁, k₂)) is heuristic (often set to log n). An ablation study varying k and butterfly depth would help practitioners navigate the accuracy vs. parameter vs. speed trade-off.  
   – Experiments currently replace only a single dense layer. Extending to multiple-layer replacement, and even to convolutional replacements, would demonstrate generality and expose potential optimization challenges when stacking multiple butterfly modules.  
   – The role of FJLT‐style initialization vs. random initialization is not isolated. A controlled experiment here would clarify its impact on convergence.  
4. Empirical reporting and reproducibility  
   – Statistical significance: In Figure 1(a), the small accuracy differences between dense and butterfly models may not be significant. Please include error bars or confidence intervals over multiple seeds.  
   – Training time and memory benchmarks: Report wall-clock training/inference time and peak memory usage for both dense and butterfly models on modern hardware.  
   – Learning curves: In Figure 1(b), start all methods at epoch 0 (including initial test accuracy) and extend plots beyond epoch 20 to show stability of the training dynamics.  
   – Figure quality: Improve the resolution and font size of all figures (e.g., Fig 3) for readability.  
   – Reproducibility: Release code, full hyperparameter settings, and random seeds to facilitate adoption and verification.

Additional Comparisons  
– Include stronger baselines from model compression literature, such as structured pruning, tensor decomposition (e.g., SVD or low-displacement‐rank layers), and other FFT-like transforms.  
– Compare against recent network‐pruning and low-rank approximation techniques to contextualize performance gains.

Conclusion  
This paper makes a compelling case for replacing dense layers with structured butterfly transforms by unifying theory and practice. While several extensions and clarifications are needed—particularly around ablations, theoretical assumptions, and empirical reporting—the core contribution is sound and merits acceptance (rating 6: marginally above the threshold).
